title: Mesenteric cyst: non-specific abdominal pain in the emergency department
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic disease affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age with significant clinical manifestations across multiple symptom domains. The condition is strongly associated with three major clinical presentations: infertility, chronic pelvic pain, and dysmenorrhea. These associations underscore the multifaceted impact of endometriosis on reproductive health and quality of life, affecting both fertility potential and pain management in affected women. The high prevalence of these interconnected symptoms highlights the importance of considering endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of reproductive-aged women presenting with these clinical complaints, particularly in emergency settings where non-specific abdominal pain may mask underlying endometriotic disease.

title: Society of Radiologists in Ultrasound Consensus on Routine Pelvic US
summary: Endometriosis presents clinically with chronic pelvic pain and infertility, making transvaginal ultrasound (US) the first-line imaging modality for patients with these symptoms. While endometriomas are readily visualized on routine transvaginal US, sonologists frequently fail to detect other forms of endometriosis using standard imaging protocols. The Society of Radiologists in Ultrasound panel recommends specific technical approaches including examination of the posterior compartment and implementation of the uterine sliding sign maneuver to improve endometriosis detection. These targeted ultrasound techniques enhance sensitivity for identifying endometriotic lesions beyond the obvious endometriomas, providing more accurate diagnosis for patients presenting with pain and infertility. The consensus emphasizes that standardized protocols using these diagnostic maneuvers are essential for comprehensive endometriosis assessment on transvaginal ultrasound.

title: Laparoscopically confirmed endometriosis and anti-Müllerian hormone levels: findings from the Nurses’ Health Study II
summary:
# Summary

This study from the Nurses' Health Study II prospectively examined the relationship between laparoscopically confirmed endometriosis and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, a marker of ovarian reserve. Women with endometriosis had substantially lower AMH levels—34.7% lower than controls—and showed earlier natural menopause with nearly 30% greater risk compared to unaffected women. The mechanism appears multifaceted: endometriosis causes multiple clinical symptoms including dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain, reduces fertility through mechanisms involving poor oocyte quality and endometrial dysfunction, and may involve inflammatory markers and oxidative stress that damage ovaries. Additionally, endometriotic lesions themselves express elevated AMH and its receptor (AMHRII), suggesting AMH may contribute to disease etiology and growth. The association between endometriosis and reduced AMH was stronger among women with BMI ≥25 kg/m², indicating potential metabolic influences on disease severity and ovarian impact.

title: Isolated urinary bladder endometriosis
summary:
# Summary: Isolated Urinary Bladder Endometriosis

Endometriosis involves the presence of ectopic endometrial tissue outside the normal uterine cavity, and while the condition typically affects pelvic organs, urinary tract involvement is rare, occurring in approximately 0.3–12% of endometriosis cases with the bladder being the most commonly affected site. Bladder endometriosis presents a significant diagnostic challenge because it often does not manifest with cyclical hematuria, the classic symptom expected from ectopic endometrial tissue, which frequently leads to delayed diagnosis. The atypical presentation of isolated urinary bladder endometriosis emphasizes the need for heightened clinical suspicion and appropriate diagnostic evaluation when evaluating patients with unexplained lower urinary tract symptoms, particularly those with a history of endometriosis.

title: Marfan syndrome is associated with increased risk for gynecologic disorders and maternal complications
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: Patients with Marfan syndrome demonstrate a significantly elevated risk for multiple gynecologic disorders, including endometriosis, dysmenorrhea, abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic and perineal pain, and dyspareunia. Beyond these pain-related conditions, Marfan syndrome is also associated with structural gynecologic pathologies such as leiomyoma, polyps of the female genital tract, female genital prolapse, and urinary incontinence. Additionally, women with Marfan syndrome face increased fertility challenges requiring testing and experience adverse maternal outcomes during pregnancy, including gestational hypertension and pre-eclampsia, along with a higher rate of cesarean delivery. These findings suggest that the connective tissue dysfunction characteristic of Marfan syndrome broadly compromises gynecologic health, affecting both the structural integrity of pelvic organs and reproductive function. The constellation of reproductive complications in this population warrants comprehensive gynecologic screening and specialized obstetric management to optimize outcomes.

title: Prevalence and Clinical Correlates of Endometriosis in Patients with IC/BPS
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that endometriosis is significantly associated with interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS), particularly in younger female patients presenting with a non-bladder-centric phenotype characterized by systemic pain symptoms. Patients with concurrent endometriosis and IC/BPS demonstrate a distinctive clinical profile: they are younger, have larger bladder capacity, and experience a higher burden of non-urological associated symptoms including irritable bowel syndrome, fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue syndrome, migraines, and psychiatric conditions such as depression and panic disorder. Binary logistic regression confirmed a positive association between endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain, with endometriosis increasing the odds of IC/BPS development approximately four-fold. Notably, patients with comorbid endometriosis and IC/BPS report greater dyspareunia and interference with daily activities, resulting in substantially lower quality of life compared to those with either condition alone. The presence of endometriosis also correlates with reduced likelihood of cystectomy and suggests a distinct pathophysiological mechanism separate from the bladder-centric inflammatory features typical of classic IC/BPS.

title: Pain Phenotypes in Endometriosis: A Population‐Based Study Using Latent Class Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This population-based study using latent class analysis identified distinct pain phenotypes in endometriosis patients, revealing that the condition presents as a heterogeneous syndrome with multiple pain manifestations and comorbidities. Endometriosis is strongly associated with dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dysuria, dyschezia, and chronic pelvic pain, with underlying mechanisms involving systemic inflammation, altered brain gene expression, pain sensitization, and mood disorders. The study identified a clinically significant high pain phenotype characterized by previous treatment failure, heavy painkiller use, family history of endometriosis, and lower socioeconomic status, alongside multiple comorbidities including fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome, migraines, anxiety, and depression. In contrast, patients with endometriomas showed association with a low pain phenotype and better quality of life outcomes. The high pain phenotype likely involves nociplastic pain mechanisms and central sensitization, affecting younger, unemployed, nulliparous patients without college degrees, and was independently predictive of substantially worse quality of life compared to the low pain phenotype.

title: Real world perspectives on endometriosis disease phenotyping through surgery, omics, health data, and artificial intelligence
summary: Endometriosis is a prevalent gynecological condition affecting 10-15% of reproductive-age individuals, presenting with multiple clinical manifestations including chronic pelvic pain (50% of patients), infertility, and subfertility, alongside diverse comorbidities such as irritable bowel syndrome, migraines, and depression. The pathophysiology involves ectopic endometrial lesions that trigger inflammation, fibrosis, and neovascularization through dysregulated estrogen signaling and diminished progesterone responsiveness, with multiple proposed mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, and dysfunctional immune clearance. Distinct endometriotic phenotypes exhibit characteristic molecular signatures—superficial peritoneal lesions show high estrogen and progesterone receptor expression with lower fibrotic burden, while ovarian endometriomas and deep infiltrating lesions demonstrate elevated KRAS mutations, increased fibrotic tissue, and inflammation-driven fibrosis mediated by TGF-β1 signaling that compromises ovarian reserve and follicle function. Fibrosis emerges as a critical pathogenic feature positively correlating with disease progression, lesion invasion, dysmenorrhea severity, and reduced ovarian follicle density. Surgical intervention remains highly effective for pain and fertility outcomes, though approximately 50% of patients experience recurrent pain, while medical approaches targeting inflammation and estrogen synthesis show limited efficacy, suggesting the need for refined biomarker-driven and phenotype-specific therapeutic strategies.

title: Endometriosis in later life: an intersectional analysis from the perspective of epistemic injustice
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory condition characterized by the growth of uterine-like tissue outside the uterus, presenting with diverse symptoms including severe menstrual pain, heavy bleeding, chronic pelvic pain, sexual dysfunction, infertility, and systemic manifestations such as bowel disorders and nerve pain. While traditionally framed as a reproductive-age disease managed through surgical removal of lesions, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, and hormonal therapy, substantial evidence demonstrates that endometriosis persists into perimenopausal and postmenopausal years, with symptom resolution associated with estrogen decline but potential reactivation with hormone replacement therapy. The article identifies significant epistemic injustice in endometriosis care, whereby symptoms are dismissed as normal menstruation, adequate interpretive frameworks for older women are absent, and inadequate medical knowledge perpetuates neglect of female pain through gendered stereotypes and sexism. Research gaps are substantial: endometriosis effects on overall health appear in only 1% of aging studies, medical research historically centered on men while female-specific research narrowly focuses on reproductive health, thereby marginalizing uniquely female health experiences in later life. These intersecting forms of injustice—testimonial, hermeneutic, and compounded by ageism—underscore how social and epistemic factors compound the clinical challenges of diagnosing and treating endometriosis across the lifespan.

title: Spectrochemical differentiation in endometriosis based on infrared spectroscopy advanced data fusion and multivariate analysis
summary: Endometriosis, a benign gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial growth causing scar tissue formation, adhesions, and inflammatory reactions, presents with major clinical symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, pelvic pain, and infertility. This study demonstrates that infrared spectroscopy combined with multivariate analysis provides a novel non-invasive diagnostic approach for endometriosis using blood plasma. Attenuated total reflection Fourier-transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) and near-infrared (NIR) spectroscopy identified characteristic spectral biomarkers associated with endometriosis, including protein-related Amide I and II bands at specific wavenumbers and distinct NIR absorption patterns that correlate with disease status. When ATR-FTIR and NIR data were integrated using advanced classification algorithms such as PCA-LDA, GA-LDA, and SPA-LDA, the fused dataset achieved near-perfect diagnostic accuracy, substantially exceeding individual spectroscopy methods. This spectrochemical approach offers significant advantages over conventional invasive laparoscopic diagnosis, including speed, non-invasiveness, and portability, establishing infrared spectroscopy as a promising biomarker-based tool for early endometriosis detection and clinical management.

title: Comprehensive Management of Bowel Endometriosis: Surgical Techniques, Outcomes, and Best Practices
summary:
# Summary

Bowel endometriosis is a complex estrogen-modulated condition affecting women of reproductive age that causes chronic pelvic pain, gastrointestinal symptoms including cyclic diarrhea and nausea, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. The disease develops through multiple mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, dysbiosis-driven immune dysregulation, and disruption of the intestinal microbiota's estrogen metabolism via the estrobolome, with infiltration of endometriotic lesions into the enteric nervous system amplifying gastrointestinal dysfunction. Diagnosis relies on transvaginal ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging, while the rectosigmoid colon, rectum, ileum, appendix, and cecum are most commonly affected. Surgical management is tailored to disease severity, with shaving excision preferred for minimal invasiveness in less severe cases and segmental resection reserved for deeply infiltrating disease, with these excisional approaches providing both pain relief and improved fertility outcomes. Additionally, endometriosis carries an increased risk for neoplastic complications, particularly colorectal cancer, necessitating comprehensive long-term management combining surgery with postoperative hormonal therapy.

title: Adenomyosis: A potential cause of surgical failure in treating dyspareunia in rectovaginal septum endometriosis
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: This study examines how adenomyosis affects surgical outcomes in patients with rectovaginal septum endometriosis presenting with deep dyspareunia. Rectovaginal endometriosis is a well-established cause of deep dyspareunia, and rectovaginal shaving surgery typically reduces pain and improves sexual quality of life in affected patients. However, the presence of concurrent adenomyosis significantly diminishes surgical treatment effectiveness, with adenomyosis independently causing pain, abnormal uterine bleeding, infertility, and dyspareunia through infiltration of endometrial tissue into the myometrium. The study found that focal adenomyosis of the outer myometrium strongly correlates with the severity of deep infiltrating endometriosis, and multiparity emerged as a significant risk factor for adenomyosis development in this patient population. Notably, patients with isolated rectovaginal endometriosis (without adenomyosis) demonstrated superior surgical responses compared to those with coexisting adenomyosis, suggesting that adenomyosis-related pathology persists despite rectovaginal lesion treatment. These findings indicate that adenomyosis screening and consideration should be integrated into preoperative evaluation to appropriately counsel patients on realistic surgical outcomes and treatment expectations.

title: Current research status and development trends of urinary tract endometriosis: a bibliometric analysis
summary:
# Summary

Urinary tract endometriosis (UTE), a rare manifestation affecting 1-2% of endometriosis patients, involves ectopic endometrial tissue in the bladder, ureters, kidneys, and urethra, with the bladder being the most commonly affected site (70-85%). The condition arises from the chronic inflammatory nature of endometriosis and manifests through distinct clinical presentations: bladder involvement causes dysuria, urinary frequency, hematuria, and incontinence, while ureteral involvement often presents asymptomatically but can lead to obstructive uropathy and renal failure. This bibliometric analysis identified strong associations between UTE and deep infiltrating endometriosis, uterosacral ligament involvement, and prior endometriosis surgery, with diagnostic modalities including transvaginal ultrasound and pelvic magnetic resonance imaging. Research in this field, predominantly published in the Journal of Minimally Invasive Gynecology and led by European institutions, has historically focused on surgical treatments but is evolving toward integrated management strategies that address the systemic inflammatory nature of the disease.

title: Ultrasound of the uterosacral ligaments: A reliability study for diagnosing endometriosis in Australian non‐specialised medical imaging and radiology settings
journal: Australasian Journal of Ultrasound in Medicine
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis commonly affects the uterosacral ligaments (USL), causing dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. Transvaginal ultrasound (TVS) has emerged as the recommended first-line diagnostic tool, demonstrating high accuracy for detecting deep endometriosis when specific USL characteristics are assessed, including thickening (>3 mm), heterogeneous echotexture, nodules, and irregular margins. This reliability study found substantial interobserver agreement for USL echogenicity assessment (0.96) and nodule identification (0.61), though moderate agreement for thickness measurement (0.47) and lower agreement for contour assessment (0.42). Importantly, observers achieved 92% accuracy in correctly identifying thin USLs in patients without deep endometriosis but showed greater variability (69%) in identifying thickened USLs in affected patients. The findings support integrating systematic USL examination into standard TVS protocols when deep endometriosis is clinically suspected, as targeted sonographer education and departmental protocols can improve diagnostic accuracy and reduce delays in diagnosis, particularly in non-specialized imaging settings.

title: Comorbidity analysis and clustering of endometriosis patients using electronic health records
summary: This large-scale electronic health record analysis identified hundreds of comorbidities significantly associated with endometriosis, revealing a complex disease profile extending far beyond gynecological manifestations. Endometriosis characteristically produces inflammation, scarring, and adhesions through ectopic endometrial tissue growth, directly contributing to its cardinal symptoms of chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. Beyond reproductive complications, the study discovered strong associations with autoimmune diseases, genitourinary and gastrointestinal disorders, as well as unexpected conditions including migraines, asthma, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and vitamin D deficiency. Notably, uterine adenomyosis and pelvic peritoneal adhesions showed the strongest associations (odds ratios of 181 and higher), while female infertility and general autoimmune disease demonstrated five and 4.3-fold increased odds respectively. The analysis also identified elevated cancer antigen 125 levels and revealed interesting protective associations with hyperlipidemia, suggesting potential therapeutic relevance of lipid-modulating agents like statins. Patient clustering analysis demonstrated heterogeneous comorbidity patterns, with some patients predominantly exhibiting autoimmune, chronic pain, and psychiatric comorbidities, indicating endometriosis presents distinct clinical phenotypes requiring personalized management approaches.

title: The Improved-EFI Score: A Multi-Omics-Based Novel Efficacy Predictive Tool for Predicting the Natural Fertility of Endometriosis Patients
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes infertility in approximately one-third of infertile women by disrupting normal reproduction through ectopic endometrial gland and stromal tissue in the ovaries and uterosacral ligaments. This study developed an improved Endometriosis Fertility Index (EFI) prediction model that integrates five ultrasound radiomics parameters with three urinary proteomic markers (HE4, ARF3, and MYH10) to predict natural pregnancy outcomes in endometriosis patients following laparoscopic surgery. The model demonstrated superior predictive performance with AUC values of 0.921 and 0.909 compared to the traditional EFI, utilizing advanced machine learning algorithms and validated through ROC curves, calibration curves, and decision curve analysis. Key proteomic findings revealed that HE4 levels were elevated while ARF3 and MYH10 were reduced in patients achieving pregnancy, alongside elevated AMH levels, enabling discrimination between successful and unsuccessful pregnancy outcomes. This multi-omics approach provides clinicians with a more accurate tool to counsel endometriosis patients on their fertility prognosis and guide decisions regarding natural conception versus assisted reproductive technology.

title: Bladder Endometriosis: Diagnostic, Therapy, and Outcome of a Single-Center Experience
summary: Bladder endometriosis, affecting 0.35% of endometriosis patients and up to 80% of those with urinary tract involvement, is characterized by the presence of endometrium-like epithelium and stromal tissue within the detrusor muscle. The condition produces a constellation of symptoms including dysmenorrhea (96.1%), dysuria (67.1%), suprapubic pain, polyuria, and hematuria, which arise from inflammatory processes triggered by retrograde menstruation, adenomyosis metaplasia, or uterine surgical trauma. Diagnostic confirmation relies on transvaginal sonography to assess lesion size and location, cystoscopy for direct visualization of bladder involvement, and histological confirmation of resected tissue. Laparoscopic surgical treatment—either partial bladder resection for full-thickness infiltration or conservative shaving for partial mucosa involvement—represents the primary therapeutic approach, with 90% of patients experiencing symptom improvement, complete dysuria resolution, and significantly reduced recurrence when lesions are completely excised. Hormonal therapies including progestogens and combined oral contraceptives offer first-line medical management for symptom reduction. While partial bladder resection carries risks including postoperative bleeding and potential ureteral stenosis, conservative shaving demonstrates excellent safety with no reported complications, supporting tissue-preserving surgical strategies when feasible.

title: Endometriosis in Adolescents: A Closer Look at the Pain Characteristics and Atypical Symptoms: A Prospective Cohort Study
summary: This prospective cohort study characterizes the distinctive clinical presentation of endometriosis in adolescents compared to adults, revealing that early-onset disease manifests with more severe and atypical symptomatology. Adolescents with endometriosis experience earlier menarche, longer menstrual periods, and significantly more intense dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, headaches, and nausea than adult patients, along with reduced responsiveness to NSAID treatment attributed to neuropathic pain mechanisms and central sensitization. Beyond traditional pain symptoms, adolescents frequently present with atypical features including genitourinary symptoms, dyschezia, migraines, and psychological comorbidities such as depression and anxiety, which substantially impact quality of life and cause school absenteeism. Risk factors for early-onset endometriosis include early menarche, low body mass index, and prolonged menstrual exposure facilitating retrograde menstrual flow and pelvic inflammation. Both hormonal contraceptives and dienogest effectively improved pain and quality of life metrics in adolescents over six months of treatment, with vitamin D supplementation emerging as a potential adjunctive strategy for managing dysmenorrhea. These findings underscore the need for heightened clinical awareness of endometriosis in adolescents and the importance of early intervention to prevent long-term debilitation from this inflammatory disease.

title: A Narrative Review Regarding Implication of Ovarian Endometriomas in Infertility
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a systemic estrogen-dependent disease affecting 30–50% of infertile women, frequently manifests as ovarian endometriomas in 17–44% of cases, which represent advanced disease and significantly compromise fertility through multiple mechanisms. The condition creates a local inflammatory microenvironment where estradiol promotes prostaglandin E2 synthesis, which in turn increases aromatase activity and local estrogen production, perpetuating a pathological cycle that impairs normal reproductive physiology. Ovarian endometriomas directly damage ovarian reserve through hypoperfusion, ischemia, and iron-induced oxidative stress that impairs follicular growth and oocyte quality, while peritoneal macrophages release proteinases that further compromise ovarian tissue. Additionally, endometriotic lesions cause structural reproductive abnormalities including peritubal adhesions, impaired tubal motility, and tubo-ovarian deformation, along with cellular dysfunction such as increased granulosa cell apoptosis and altered aromatase expression that disrupts oocyte maturation. Clinically, endometriosis manifests as pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and menorrhagia, with diagnostic confirmation achieved through transvaginal ultrasound (89% sensitivity) or laparoscopic visualization and tissue sampling, while surgical cystectomy remains the primary treatment for ovarian endometriomas.

title: The endometrioma paradox
summary:
# Summary: The Endometrioma Paradox

Endometriosis affects approximately 10% of reproductive-age women and is a common cause of infertility, with ovarian endometriomas present in 17-44% of cases. These cysts impair fertility through mechanical stretching and local inflammation that promotes oxidative stress, ultimately leading to oocyte apoptosis and necrosis. While laparoscopic cystectomy significantly improves spontaneous pregnancy rates (59.4% versus 23.3% with fenestration) and reduces disease recurrence through lesion eradication, it paradoxically damages ovarian reserve by reducing anti-müllerian hormone levels, antral follicle count, and oocyte yield. Patients with endometriomas demonstrate diminished ovarian responsiveness, requiring higher gonadotropin doses and producing fewer mature oocytes during controlled ovarian stimulation. Post-operative hormonal suppression with dienogest or oral contraceptives reduces pain recurrence and endometrioma recurrence, though the fundamental challenge remains balancing the fertility benefits of surgical excision against the threat to ovarian reserve necessary for assisted reproduction.

title: Symptom assessment related to the menstrual cycle to predict
summary: Endometriosis and adenomyosis present as distinct but frequently co-occurring conditions characterized by severe, progressive dysmenorrhea and multiple pelvic symptoms that significantly impact quality of life. Both conditions are strongly associated with dysmenorrhea, infertility, abnormal uterine bleeding, and sexual dysfunction including dyspareunia and dysuria, with patients experiencing these symptoms at markedly higher rates than controls. Notably, progressive worsening of dysmenorrhea over a 12-month period emerges as a particularly strong predictor of endometriosis or adenomyosis, with patients in these disease groups experiencing severe pain (VAS≥8) and missing classes during menstruation at substantially higher frequencies. The assessment of menstrual cycle-related symptoms can serve as an effective screening tool for identifying university-aged women at risk for these conditions, as patients with endometriosis or adenomyosis demonstrated distinctive symptom patterns including higher dysmenorrhea severity but paradoxically lower reported menstrual bleeding. Beyond pelvic pain, endometriosis causes significant disruptions to academic, work, and social functioning in young women, establishing its impact as a disease of substantial clinical and social consequence. These findings underscore the importance of symptom-based assessment during the menstrual cycle as a practical diagnostic approach for identifying at-risk individuals in clinical and educational settings.

title: Impact of deep or ovarian endometriosis on pelvic pain and quality of life: prospective cross‐sectional ultrasound study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective ultrasound study examined how deep and ovarian endometriosis impact pelvic pain symptoms and quality of life in women of reproductive age. Endometriosis is strongly associated with chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and menstrual dyschezia, with posterior compartment deep endometriotic lesions particularly linked to severe menstrual pain and bowel/bladder dysfunction. The mechanisms underlying these symptoms involve infiltration of the sacral plexus by endometriotic nodules and cyclic microbleeding with subsequent inflammation within lesions. While endometriosis commonly co-exists with adenomyosis and fibroids, multivariable analysis revealed that menstruation-specific symptoms—particularly menstrual dyschezia and difficulty emptying the bladder—were the most specific indicators of endometriosis compared to other pelvic pathology. Additionally, younger age, migraine history, and non-endometriotic ovarian cysts showed independent associations with dyspareunia severity, suggesting that pelvic pain in endometriosis is multifactorial and influenced by both disease-specific and systemic factors.

title: Endometriosis in Adolescence: A Narrative Review of the Psychological and Clinical Implications
summary: Endometriosis in adolescents is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth that causes a multifaceted symptom burden encompassing severe dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, gastrointestinal discomfort, and infertility. The pathophysiology involves hormonal dysregulation featuring elevated estradiol and progesterone coupled with progesterone resistance and estrogen receptor hyperexpression, which promote endometriotic lesion proliferation, while dysbiosis of the gut microbiome—marked by reduced protective bacteria such as Clostridia and Ruminococcus and enrichment of pathogenic organisms including Shigella and Prevotella—drives a pro-inflammatory state through elevated cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β) and increased brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) that amplify pain perception via nociceptive pathways. Beyond physical symptoms, endometriosis substantially impacts psychological well-being in adolescents, with chronic pelvic pain serving as a primary driver of anxiety, depression, mood disturbances, reduced self-esteem, social withdrawal, and feelings of worthlessness, consequences further exacerbated by sleep disruption and school absenteeism. The inflammatory cytokines implicated in pain pathology also directly contribute to psychiatric symptoms, while the gut-brain axis modulates pain and mood through microglial activation, glutamate elevation, and stimulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis. Emerging non-invasive blood-based biomarkers including Cancer Antigen 125, miR-200 family microRNAs, and inflammatory proteins hold promise for improving diagnostic accuracy and potentially enabling earlier detection in this vulnerable adolescent population.

title: Knowledge and awareness of endometriosis among women in Southwest China: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study of women in Southwest China examined knowledge and awareness of endometriosis, a chronic gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial-like tissue that causes pelvic pain, infertility, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and associated inflammation, adhesions, and scarring. The survey revealed variable public recognition of endometriosis symptoms, with pelvic pain identified by 62.2% of participants as the most recognized symptom, followed by irregular vaginal bleeding (55.2%) and dysmenorrhea (52.5%), while less common manifestations such as painful bowel movements (34.5%) and dyspareunia (41.4%) showed lower awareness rates. Treatment knowledge was particularly limited, with only 27.6% of participants aware that hormonal treatments effectively manage endometriosis symptoms. Demographic and clinical factors significantly predicted better endometriosis knowledge, including younger age (26–45 years), higher education attainment (master's degree or above), active employment status, recent pelvic ultrasound within three months, single marital status, and personal history of endometriosis, while a history of abortion was inversely associated with knowledge levels. These findings highlight substantial gaps in endometriosis awareness among the surveyed population and identify key demographic groups requiring targeted education interventions.

title: Long-Term Survival of Endometriosis-Related Ovarian Clear Cell Carcinoma with Endometriosis Surgical History
summary: Endometriosis significantly increases the risk of ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC), with women carrying a 2-3 times higher cancer risk than the general population, and specific genetic mutations in p53, KRAS, PTEN, and ARID1A drive malignant transformation. Endometriosis-related OCCC presents distinct clinical features, occurring in younger patients (average age 45.68 years) at earlier disease stages with characteristic symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and pelvic pain, yet endometriosis status itself does not independently impact cancer prognosis. The microenvironment of endometriotic cysts promotes malignant transformation through oxidative stress, chronic inflammation, immune cell dysfunction, and elevated transforming growth factor-β1 (TGF-β1), which enhances cell survival, migration, and invasion while suppressing natural killer cell activity. Notably, patients with a history of endometriotic ovarian cyst surgery experience significantly worse survival outcomes, with incidence rates increasing from 3.17 to 13.36 per 1000 person-years, likely due to spillage of TGF-β1-enriched cyst fluid during surgery and subsequent pelvic adhesion formation. Optimal cytoreductive surgery and early detection remain crucial prognostic factors, while emerging biomarkers such as circulating tumor DNA, extracellular vesicles, and non-coding RNAs show promise for improving ovarian neoplasia diagnosis and surveillance in endometriosis patients.

title: Comparative pregnancy rate after colorectal resection versus other surgical procedures for deep infiltrating rectal endometriosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE), particularly in the rectovaginal region, significantly impairs fertility through multiple mechanisms including disrupted ovulation, fertilization, and implantation, with patients experiencing reduced pregnancy rates and increased miscarriage risk even with minimal disease. Surgical excision improves fertility prospects, but the choice of surgical technique substantially affects outcomes: colorectal resection is associated with markedly lower pregnancy rates compared to less invasive approaches like rectal shaving and disc excision. While segmental colorectal resection carries the highest complication rates, including anastomotic fistulas, bowel obstruction, and severe infections, rectal shaving demonstrates a significantly lower overall complication rate of 6% compared to 23% for more extensive procedures. Women with rectovaginal DIE who become pregnant face elevated risks of preterm birth, low birth weight, and increased hospital admissions, and notably, pregnancy does not halt disease progression. Medical interventions provide symptomatic relief but do not eliminate the underlying disease and frequently cause adverse effects including irregular bleeding and reduced sexual desire. These findings suggest that fertility-conscious surgical management with tissue-sparing techniques should be prioritized for women with DIE seeking pregnancy.

title: Fertility preservation in women with endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis compromises fertility in 30-50% of affected patients through multiple mechanisms, including chronic pelvic inflammation, distortion of pelvic anatomy, and direct damage to ovarian function. Ovarian endometriomas, which contain high levels of free iron and reactive oxygen species, cause particular harm by damaging ovarian parenchyma and reducing ovarian reserve, as evidenced by significantly lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels and increased risk of premature ovarian failure. Additionally, endometriosis is associated with reduced oocyte quality, lower in vitro maturation and fertilization rates, and embryonic nuclear abnormalities, collectively compromising reproductive outcomes. Surgical excision of endometriomas, while reducing recurrence risk and pain, paradoxically further decreases AMH levels and carries a 2.4-13% risk of premature ovarian failure, making tissue-conserving ablation techniques preferable for fertility preservation. Current clinical consensus recommends assisted reproductive technology as the first-line treatment for infertile women with ovarian endometriomas to avoid additional surgical damage to ovarian reserve, and oocyte cryopreservation represents an emerging strategy for fertility preservation in this population.

title: Endometriosis Quality of Life Cohort Study: Long-term Impact of Radical Laparoscopic Excision of Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition causing pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyschezia, dyspareunia, and subfertility that significantly impairs quality of life. This cohort study evaluated radical laparoscopic excision involving peritoneal resection, ureterolysis, bowel dissection, and endometrioma management, demonstrating substantial symptom improvement with 79.2% of patients showing reduced menstrual pain, 64.4% improvement in noncyclical pelvic pain, and 65.6% improvement in dyspareunia. Quality of life scores improved in 63% of patients overall, with 77.1% of those desiring pregnancy achieving successful conception, likely through restoration of pelvic anatomy and reduction of proinflammatory markers. Notably, patients with lower baseline quality of life scores experienced the most significant postoperative improvements, though mental health scores did not improve. Earlier menarche and need for repeat surgery within ten years were associated with poorer long-term outcomes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive surgical approach and preventing recurrence for optimal patient benefit.

title: Longer operative times but equally safe - a propensity score-matched analysis for laparoscopic procedures in women with early-stage (r-ASRM I-II) endometriosis performed by gynecology residents compared to attending surgeons
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a condition characterized by chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, is commonly treated through laparoscopic surgery. This study examined whether gynecology residents and fellows could safely perform laparoscopic procedures for early-stage (rASRM stage I-II) endometriosis compared to attending surgeons, finding that while residents required significantly longer operative times—approximately 10.5% longer for stage I and 15.4% longer for stage II disease—they achieved equivalent safety profiles with no significant differences in intraoperative or postoperative complications, length of hospital stay, or revision surgery rates. The prolonged operative times in residents reflect the inherent learning curves associated with minimally invasive surgery, which are compounded by the loss of haptic feedback and indirect visualization of the operative field. Despite comparable overall complication rates between groups, the specific types of complications differed, with attending surgeons experiencing more postoperative complications overall, suggesting that structured supervision and a step-wise training approach enable safe surgical education in early-stage endometriosis. The findings support the use of early-stage endometriosis cases as appropriate training opportunities for residents, potentially addressing declining surgical training opportunities while maintaining patient safety and emphasizing the value of additional interventions like surgical simulation training to accelerate the learning curve.

title: Social media to understand the endometriosis patient journey: Listening to influences driving treatment choices
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic estrogen-dependent condition characterized by dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and non-menstrual pelvic pain that frequently causes infertility and profoundly impacts quality of life through work and school absences, mental health effects, relationship challenges, and stigma. Social media analysis revealed that patients experience substantial diagnostic delays and face significant barriers to care including financial constraints, insurance limitations, and specialist shortages, while multiple treatment modalities—including oral contraceptives, progestins, GnRH agonists/antagonists, surgical procedures (laparoscopy and excision), and complementary approaches—are employed with variable success and often accompanied by side effects. Despite surgical interventions being widely discussed online, patient experiences demonstrate that surgery does not reliably cure the condition and misconceptions about treatment efficacy persist, complicated by online misinformation that shapes treatment decisions. Fertility concerns dominated patient discussions across nearly 1500 posts, underscoring the substantial psychological burden of infertility associated with endometriosis alongside persistent physical symptoms and the emotional consequences of restricted daily functioning.

title: Endometriosis Severity and Risk of Preeclampsia: A Combined Mendelian Randomization and Observational Study
summary: This combined Mendelian randomization and observational study demonstrates that advanced endometriosis (ASRM stages III–IV) and deep infiltrating endometriosis are significantly associated with increased risk of preeclampsia and eclampsia, while mild-to-moderate disease shows no significant association. Adenomyosis emerged as the strongest predictor of preeclampsia (OR: 9.96), followed by dysmenorrhea (OR: 2.72) and advanced endometriosis stage, with maternal age also contributing to risk. The proposed mechanism involves inflammatory and immunological alterations characteristic of endometriosis interfering with placental function and contributing to adverse pregnancy outcomes. Clinical observations revealed that women with preeclampsia had higher revised American Fertility Society (r-AFS) scores, increased CA125 levels, and substantially higher rates of assisted reproductive technology use (82% versus 24%), alongside worse neonatal outcomes including growth restriction. These findings suggest that the severity and extent of endometriotic disease, particularly adenomyosis and deep infiltration, are critical determinants of hypertensive pregnancy complications, with implications for pregnancy risk stratification in endometriosis patients.

title: Diagnosing diagnostic error of endometriosis: a secondary analysis of patient experiences from a mixed-methods survey
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis presents a complex diagnostic challenge characterized by multiple overlapping symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea, dyspareunia, fatigue, and infertility, which collectively contribute to significant physical and psychological morbidity. While definitive diagnosis currently requires surgical visualization and histological confirmation, emerging evidence supports advanced ultrasonography as an alternative diagnostic tool, though standard blood tests and transvaginal ultrasound lack sufficient specificity. Diagnostic errors arise from multiple interconnected factors: clinician-related issues such as symptom invalidation, poor endometriosis knowledge, and failure to consider the diagnosis; disease-related factors including variable symptom presentation and overlap with conditions like irritable bowel syndrome, ovarian cysts, and pelvic inflammatory disease; and system-level challenges involving delayed specialist referrals and misuse of oral contraceptives as diagnostic tools. Patient experiences reveal a troubling pattern where symptom invalidation erodes trust in healthcare providers, leading patients to avoid seeking care and clinicians to lack feedback on diagnostic errors. Recognition of symptom severity, improved clinician familiarity with endometriosis, and appropriate test selection are essential to reducing diagnostic delays and errors in this underdiagnosed condition.

title: Investigating The Rate of Pregnancy Success after Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis causes significant reproductive dysfunction through multiple overlapping mechanisms, with infertility affecting over 50% of affected women. The pathophysiology involves intraperitonal inflammation driven by activated macrophages that produce elevated levels of cyclooxygenase and prostaglandins (PGF2α and PGE2), which upregulate inflammatory mediators including interleukin-6, interleukin-8, and nuclear factor-kappa B. These inflammatory factors compromise oocyte quality and fertility through multiple routes: peritoneal inflammation, adhesion-induced anatomical disturbances, reduced oocyte quality, and disrupted uterine-tubal transport, with effects present across all forms of endometriosis including superficial, deep infiltrating, and endometriomas. Laparoscopic surgery improves fertility outcomes, with pregnancy rates around 50%, and combined surgical therapy followed by assisted reproductive technologies (ART) yields considerably higher cumulative live birth rates than either approach alone. Patient age and duration of infertility emerged as critical prognostic factors, with younger patients and those with shorter infertility duration achieving higher spontaneous conception rates, while adenomyosis was more prevalent in IVF-achieving patients. Medical management of endometriomas can reduce lesion size and pain but does not improve fertility, emphasizing the role of surgical intervention in restoring reproductive capacity.

title: Precision surgery for endometriosis: preventing chronic pelvic pain in patients with higher pre-operative pain scores and in patients of advanced age
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a benign, estrogen-dependent disease that causes a spectrum of symptoms including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain (CPP), dyspareunia, infertility, and gastrointestinal symptoms through chronic inflammatory mechanisms that affect brain structure and induce myofascial dysfunction. Laparoscopic precision surgery aimed at complete excision of endometriotic tissue substantially reduces these symptoms, with postoperative pain scores decreasing from 77.2 to 26.6 and symptom prevalence declining significantly across menstrual pain (85.2% to 32.0%), CPP (67.2% to 40.2%), and dyspareunia (59.8% to 34.4%), while 45.2% of previously infertile women achieved pregnancy. Notably, patients with higher preoperative pain scores and advanced age showed better long-term pain relief following surgery, whereas younger patients with CPP and moderate preoperative pain demonstrated increased risk for therapy resistance and reoperation. Postoperative hormonal therapy with progestins or combined oral contraceptives provided additional pain relief in patients not sufficiently improved by surgery alone, though recurrence remained a concern with certain treatments.

title: The clinical characteristics and prognosis of surgically treated ovarian endometrioma in pregnant women
summary:
# Summary

This study examines ovarian endometriomas (OMA) identified and surgically managed in pregnant women, highlighting the unique clinical challenges posed by pregnancy-induced changes in endometriotic lesions. Endometriosis causes infertility, pain, and nodule formation, with OMA specifically reducing fertility and associating with adverse pregnancy outcomes including spontaneous miscarriage, preterm birth, placenta previa, and postpartum hemorrhage. During pregnancy, elevated progesterone and estrogen levels trigger decidualization of endometrial tissue within ovarian endometriomas, which can enlarge the cysts and increase rupture risk while creating diagnostic confusion with malignant tumors due to papillary projections and enhanced blood flow on imaging. Laparoscopic cystectomy proved safe and effective for managing symptomatic OMA during mid-pregnancy, reducing dysmenorrhea without causing miscarriage, though post-operative complications included adenomyosis development and recurrence in some cases requiring hormonal therapy with dienogest or GnRHa. The study underscores that while most ovarian endometriomas remain stable or shrink during pregnancy, surgical intervention should be considered for symptomatic cases or those with imaging features concerning for malignancy.

title: Efficacy of Transvaginal Ultrasound vs Pelvic MRI in Preoperative Diagnosis of Pelvic Endometriosis
journal: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
summary: Endometriosis, characterized by the proliferation of endometrial tissue outside the uterus that triggers a persistent inflammatory response, affects 6–10% of reproductive-aged women and 25–35% of infertile women. The condition presents with multiple clinical manifestations including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, constipation, and dysuria. This study compared transvaginal ultrasound and pelvic MRI for preoperative diagnosis, finding that while transvaginal ultrasound is cost-effective and accessible, it has inferior diagnostic sensitivity and higher operator-dependency, with particular difficulty detecting adenomyosis and cysts. Pelvic MRI demonstrated superior diagnostic accuracy and specificity, particularly for detecting uterine enlargement and asymmetry, and showed lower inter-observer variability; additionally, MRI's radiation-free approach allows visualization of lesion depth and hemorrhagic changes. Both imaging modalities identify characteristic endometrial features including chocolate cysts, myometrial cysts, sub-endometrial microcysts, hypoechoic lesions, junctional zone thickening, and peritoneal thickening. Clinical accuracy improves when imaging findings are combined with patient symptomatology, though both techniques have limitations in detecting adenomyosis and certain cystic lesions.

title: Extragenital endometriosis associated with uterine scar defects
journal: European Journal of Obstetrics & Gynecology and Reproductive Biology: X
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a mechanistic link between cesarean scar defects (CSDs) and extragenital endometriosis, establishing poor hysterotomy healing as a significant predisposing factor. Uterine scar defects cause anterior wall weakening and retroflexion, which in turn contributes to endometriosis development, likely through disrupted uterine contractility that promotes retrograde menstruation—the primary mechanism recognized in endometriosis pathogenesis. Nearly half of symptomatic niche patients presented with endometriosis, with implants predominantly localized to the peritoneum behind the uterus, sacrouterine ligaments, and Pouch of Douglas, supporting the retrograde menstruation hypothesis. Endometriosis patients with scar defects showed significantly higher rates of heavy menstrual bleeding, retroflexion, and classic symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. The severity of symptoms correlated with scar width, and immunohistochemical markers such as CD10 positivity confirmed endometrial origin of ectopic lesions. These findings highlight the importance of recognizing cesarean scar defects as a modifiable risk factor in endometriosis development.

title: Epidemiology with real-world data: deep endometriosis in women of reproductive age
summary: This real-world epidemiological study characterizes deep endometriosis in reproductive-age women, demonstrating that the condition is strongly associated with multiple clinical manifestations including pelvic pain, infertility (occurring 6.5 times more frequently than in unaffected women), dysmenorrhea, deep dyspareunia, and cyclical urinary and intestinal symptoms. Retrocervical endometriosis emerged as the most common lesion presentation (95.7% of cases), with additional disease found in the ovaries, rectosigmoid colon, bladder, and appendix. Physical examination findings including palpable posterior cul-de-sac nodules and retroverted uterus showed strong associations with endometriosis diagnosis, while retrocervical thickening and nodules were significantly more prevalent in affected women. Transvaginal ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging were identified as key diagnostic imaging modalities, with bimanual examination detecting 98.2% of suspected cases. Notably, menstrual characteristics such as cycle length, menarche age, and reproductive history were not associated with endometriosis risk, and the study highlighted diagnostic challenges due to symptom overlap with other conditions including irritable bowel syndrome and pelvic inflammatory disease. These findings underscore the clinical importance of integrating symptomatology with physical examination and imaging to accurately diagnose deep endometriosis in women of reproductive age.

title: Identification and validation of a novel machine learning model for predicting severe pelvic endometriosis: A retrospective study
summary: This retrospective study developed and validated a machine learning model to preoperatively identify severe pelvic endometriosis, a condition characterized by altered pelvic anatomy, extensive adhesions, and multiple clinical symptoms including dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, dysuria, and infertility. Using LASSO regression on 39 variables, researchers identified 18 predictive features and compared seven machine learning algorithms, with the random forest model demonstrating superior discriminative ability. The final six-feature random forest model incorporated negative sliding sign, pelvic fluid in the cul-de-sac, bilateral ovarian endometriosis, severe dysmenorrhea, eosinophil percentage, and menstrual cycle-related symptoms, achieving areas under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.865 in training and 0.720 in testing datasets. The negative sliding sign emerged as the most impactful predictor, with bilateral ovarian endometriosis and pelvic fluid accumulation also serving as strong indicators of severe disease risk. This preoperative predictive tool could enable clinicians to identify severe endometriosis cases before surgery and allow earlier intervention with treatments such as GnRH agonists or laparoscopic surgery, potentially improving patient outcomes and surgical planning.

title: A Study on the Quality of Patient-Centred Endometriosis Mobile Applications: Analysis and Future Prospects
summary: Endometriosis affects 10–15% of reproductive-age women and is associated with severe clinical manifestations including chronic pelvic pain in up to 70% of affected patients and infertility. Mobile applications have emerged as patient-centered tools for endometriosis management, with several apps incorporating validated diagnostic instruments like the CE-certified ENDOscore questionnaire to assess disease likelihood and recommend appropriate follow-up testing. High-quality evidence-based apps such as LUNA-endometriosis and Frendo provide comprehensive information and symptom tracking through features like digital diaries (ENdi, Endometriosis Diary, myFLO), while others emphasize complementary therapies and dietary modifications including Mediterranean and gluten-free diets. Among complementary approaches, acupuncture demonstrates significant pain reduction benefits, whereas other modalities like yoga show limited efficacy. Patient-generated health data through symptom logs enhances patient-physician collaboration by allowing clinicians to review comprehensive symptom patterns beyond standard consultations. The review identifies a critical gap in app quality, with most applications failing to provide high-quality evidence-based information necessary for conjunction with medical care, highlighting the need for improved standards in endometriosis mobile health technologies.

title: Future of Sclerotherapy in the Treatment of Endometriosis: A Narrative Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a hormone-dependent gynecological disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue most commonly found in the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and pelvic peritoneum, presenting clinically with chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. The disease pathogenesis involves retrograde menstruation and cellular metaplasia, with estrogen playing a central role in both disease development and pain generation through inflammatory mechanisms. Ovarian endometriomas reduce anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels and impair fertility by limiting follicular access and causing long-term fibrosis in the ovarian cortex. Sclerotherapy approaches, particularly ultrasound-guided and laparoscopic methods, are emerging as alternatives to traditional laparoscopic cystectomy for treating endometriomas, with laparoscopic sclerotherapy achieving superior clinical pregnancy rates (27.87%) and lower recurrence rates while preserving greater ovarian reserve compared to cystectomy. Both sclerotherapy and cystectomy improve endometrioma-related symptoms and reduce cancer antigen-125 levels, though all surgical approaches carry some risk of ovarian reserve reduction and endometrioma recurrence.

title: Ovarian Tissue Removed with Endometrioma May Reflect the Quality of the Adjacent Ovary
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, particularly ovarian endometriomas (OMA), is strongly associated with infertility and pelvic symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain, driven by local inflammatory mediators such as interleukins, tumor necrosis factor, and transforming growth factor. While laparoscopic excision using the stripping technique is the gold standard surgical treatment for OMA and effectively reduces endometriosis-associated pain, it paradoxically damages healthy ovarian parenchyma in approximately half of cases and reduces ovarian reserve markers including anti-müllerian hormone levels and antral follicle count. The tissue excised around the endometrioma cyst wall reflects the quality of the adjacent remaining ovary, with follicular score correlating negatively with patient age and parity and positively with preoperative CA-125 levels, suggesting that assessment of pericystic ovarian tissue can serve as a biomarker for the functional status of the remaining ovary. This study highlights the dual challenge in managing OMA: balancing symptom relief through surgery against the unintended consequence of iatrogenic reduction in ovarian reserve and oocyte quality, which are further compromised by the endometriotic lesion itself and the aging process.

title: Follow-up of bowel endometriosis resections performed using the double circular stapler technique: A decade’s experience
summary:
# Summary

Deep infiltrating endometriosis frequently affects the bowel, particularly the rectosigmoid region, causing significant pelvic pain, infertility, and gastrointestinal symptoms that substantially impair quality of life. This study evaluates the double circular stapler technique (DCST) for treating rectosigmoid deep endometriosis, a surgical approach designed to preserve pelvic innervation and vascularization while avoiding segmental colorectal resection and its associated functional complications including constipation and bowel dysfunction. Over a decade of follow-up in 43 women, DCST achieved excellent symptomatic outcomes, with 88-100% of patients reporting resolution of dysuria, dyschezia, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and cyclic low back pain, while maintaining low complication rates including no anastomotic leakage and only one case of rectovaginal fistula. The technique enables excision of larger endometriosis nodules than conventional single-load stapler methods while preserving sexual function, urinary continence, and fertility, with 70% of women with pregnancy intent successfully conceiving post-operatively. By avoiding extensive segmental resection through careful nerve-sparing surgical techniques, DCST optimizes both symptom relief and long-term functional bowel and pelvic outcomes in deep colorectal endometriosis.

title: Management of women with endometriosis in the 21st century
summary:
# Summary: Management of Women with Endometriosis in the 21st Century

Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial growth, causes significant pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility that profoundly impact patient quality of life. The disease involves multiple pathophysiological mechanisms, including estrogen-driven lesion proliferation, progesterone resistance, central sensitization, and local neurogenesis mediated by nerve growth factor, all contributing to persistent pain. Current medical management employs hormonal therapies—including progestins, combined oral contraceptives, GnRH antagonists (such as elagolix and relugolix combination therapy), and dienogest—to suppress estrogen production and induce endometrial atrophy, though approximately one-third of patients demonstrate treatment resistance due to molecular abnormalities. Laparoscopic excisional surgery effectively improves quality of life, reduces opioid use, and alleviates pain unresponsive to medical therapy while potentially improving natural conception rates. For endometriosis-associated infertility, the disease impairs reproductive outcomes through multiple mechanisms including adhesion-related anatomical distortion, elevated inflammatory mediators affecting ovulation and sperm function, and ovarian damage from reactive oxygen species and proteolytic substances. Emerging complementary approaches, including dietary interventions and multidisciplinary pain management addressing central sensitization, alongside assisted reproductive technologies, offer additional therapeutic options for comprehensive endometriosis care.

title: Laparoscopic Resection and Anastomosis in Bowel Endometriosis: Single Stapler Surgical Technique
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary: Endometriosis affects approximately 10% of reproductive-age women globally, with bowel involvement occurring in 3.8–37% of cases, predominantly affecting the rectosigmoid region. Patients with bowel endometriosis present with characteristic symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia. Surgical excision represents the gold-standard treatment for deep infiltrating endometriosis, and this study evaluated a single-stapler laparoscopic technique for rectosigmoid resection and anastomosis in patients with bowel involvement. The procedure successfully treated bowel endometriosis while significantly reducing all three major symptom categories—dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia—in the postoperative period. Patients experienced smooth recovery with no surgical complications, demonstrating that the single-stapler technique provides a safe and effective minimally invasive approach for managing deep infiltrating bowel endometriosis.

title: Association between sedentary behaviour and chronic pelvic pain in women: a protocol for systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review protocol examines the relationship between sedentary behavior and chronic pelvic pain (CPP) in women, proposing that physical inactivity contributes to pelvic pain through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Sedentary behavior is hypothesized to impair blood circulation, increase sustained muscle tension in pelvic structures, and activate inflammatory pathways—all of which independently lead to chronic pelvic pain. CPP itself is characterized by complex interactions involving the reproductive organs, urinary system, gastrointestinal tract, and musculoskeletal structures, where dysfunction in any of these systems can precipitate or exacerbate symptoms. The authors establish sedentary behavior as a recognized risk factor for broader chronic health conditions including musculoskeletal disorders, cardiovascular disease, and metabolic dysfunction. Notably, preliminary evidence suggests that exercise interventions may reduce CPP symptoms, positioning increased physical activity as a potential therapeutic strategy. This protocol aims to systematically synthesize existing evidence to clarify the association between sedentary behavior and CPP in women, which remains understudied despite the high prevalence and burden of this condition.

title: Long-term outcomes of catheter-directed sclerotherapy for ovarian endometrioma
summary: Catheter-directed sclerotherapy (CDS) with 99% ethanol represents an effective minimally invasive alternative to laparoscopic cystectomy for treating ovarian endometriomas. The procedure significantly reduces endometrioma volume (mean decrease from 80.22 to 0.73 mL) and improves endometriosis-related symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and pelvic pain while substantially decreasing serum CA-125 levels at six months and maintaining this improvement at one year. Unlike surgical cystectomy, which risks damage to adjacent healthy ovarian tissue through follicular loss and hemostatic cauterization, CDS preserves ovarian reserve as evidenced by stable serum anti-Müllerian hormone levels post-procedure. To prevent the high recurrence rate characteristic of ovarian endometriomas, adjunctive hormonal therapy with dienogest or oral contraceptive pills is recommended following the procedure. CDS demonstrated excellent safety and efficacy at long-term follow-up, with minimal adverse effects, positioning it as a valuable minimally invasive option that maintains fertility potential while effectively treating symptomatic ovarian endometriosis.

title: Pilot study MOVENDOP protocol - impact on quality of life following postoperative osteopathic abdominal mobilizations in patients operated for endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis, a condition affecting 5-10% of women of reproductive age, is characterized by chronic inflammation and presents with the classic triad of dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain, significantly impairing quality of life and contributing to infertility. Surgical treatment remains the gold standard for symptom management; however, abdominal surgery itself triggers a cascade of inflammatory responses that lead to peritoneal adhesion formation—a complication occurring in over 90% of abdominal surgeries that can perpetuate chronic pain, dyspareunia, infertility, and gastrointestinal dysfunction, thereby limiting long-term surgical benefits. The MOVENDOP pilot study investigated postoperative osteopathic visceral mobilizations as an adjunctive intervention following endometriosis surgery, with the goal of preventing adhesion development and facilitating lymphatic drainage to promote rapid reabsorption of intraperitoneal fluids. This approach teaches patients self-mobilization techniques and abdominal diaphragmatic breathing exercises specifically designed to reduce postoperative abdominal pain and prevent adhesion-related complications. The study hypothesizes that early and repeated osteopathic sessions combined with patient-directed self-mobilizations can significantly improve quality of life outcomes following endometriosis surgery by mitigating the negative effects of postoperative tissue adhesions.

title: Differences in the Sonographic Features of Adenomyosis and Concurrent Endometriosis Compared to Isolated Adenomyosis
summary: This ultrasound study identifies distinct sonographic patterns distinguishing isolated adenomyosis from adenomyosis concurrent with endometriosis. Isolated adenomyosis predominantly displays direct features such as myometrial cysts (51%), hyperechogenic islands, and endometrial cysts, while concurrent adenomyosis and endometriosis show predominantly indirect features including globular uterine configuration, asymmetrical wall thickening, irregular junctional zones, and fan-shaped shadowing. The co-existence of endometriosis appears to alter myometrial characteristics through cytokine release and other inflammatory mechanisms, reducing the visibility of direct adenomyotic features. Both conditions are associated with similar clinical symptoms including menorrhagia, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, though adenomyosis severity correlates with endometriosis severity and deep infiltrating endometriosis variants. These findings suggest that adenomyosis and endometriosis represent a spectrum of related conditions with shared clinical, histological, and genetic characteristics but distinct imaging presentations depending on their co-occurrence.

title: Evaluation of laparoscopic surgery effects on pain severity and quality of life in different subtypes of endometriosis: A follow-up study
summary: Endometriosis causes significant clinical burden through chronic pelvic pain, impaired daily movement, diminished quality of life, and negative impacts on mental health, while also being associated with serious complications including adverse pregnancy outcomes, epithelial ovarian cancer, and atherosclerosis. The disease presents in multiple subtypes—superficial, deep, and endometrioma cysts—classified by ASRM staging from minimal (stage I) to severe (stage IV), with tissue composition varying across lesion types. Laparoscopic surgery effectively treats endometriosis and produces substantial improvements across multiple quality-of-life domains, including reductions in pain severity, dysmenorrhea, dysuria, and dyspareunia during the 12-month follow-up period. Beyond pain relief, laparoscopic intervention enhances activity levels, sexual function, emotional well-being, self-image, and social support while improving overall patient satisfaction, with complete excision yielding the highest satisfaction rates. Laparoscopic management of deep endometriosis specifically demonstrates efficacy in reducing pain and improving quality of life while preserving ovarian function and uterine integrity. These findings establish laparoscopic excision as a valid and comprehensive treatment approach that addresses both the physical symptoms and psychosocial impacts of endometriosis across disease subtypes.

title: Retrospective Assessment of Endometriosis Pain Over the Life Course: A Reliability Study Within the ComPaRe‐Endometriosis Cohort
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth outside the uterus, which consistently causes multiple pain phenotypes including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, dysuria, and abdominal pain. This retrospective study within the ComPaRe-Endometriosis cohort validated the reliability of pain symptom reporting using the WERF-EPHect Patient Questionnaire Standard (EPQ-S) and numeric rating scales, finding that dysmenorrhea demonstrated the highest reliability (ICC=0.74), while dyschezia and abdominal pain showed moderate reliability. Patient-reported pain severity showed age-dependent patterns, with participants aged ≤30 years demonstrating higher reliability for abdominal pain reporting, whereas those over 40 exhibited lower reliability for dysmenorrhea and dyschezia. Psychological and socioeconomic factors influenced pain reporting consistency, with depression, anxiety status, higher education level, and favorable financial situation associated with more reliable pain assessments. These findings establish that endometriosis patients can reliably report their long-term pain history across the lifespan, though age and psychosocial factors should be considered when interpreting patient-reported pain outcomes in clinical and research settings.

title: Analysis of the effects of cystic cavity flushing with lidocaine during ultrasound-guided anhydrous ethanol sclerotherapy in the treatment of ovarian endometrioma
journal: Quantitative Imaging in Medicine and Surgery
summary:
# Summary

Ultrasound-guided anhydrous ethanol sclerotherapy (UGAES) is an effective minimally invasive treatment for ovarian endometrioma that addresses clinical manifestations including dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, and infertility while preserving ovarian function and fertility potential. The procedure works by injecting anhydrous ethanol into the cyst, which causes rapid protein denaturation and triggers a local inflammatory response that activates nerve endings, resulting in significant pain; however, adding intracavitary lidocaine irrigation substantially mitigates this intraoperative pain by blocking sodium channels and preventing nerve impulse conduction to the central nervous system. Patients receiving lidocaine during UGAES demonstrated significantly lower intraoperative pain scores compared to those without lidocaine, while both groups achieved comparable cyst reduction rates (92% versus 90% volume reduction) and 100% clinical effectiveness at three-month follow-up. Lidocaine is preferred as an adjunct because of its rapid onset of action (1-3 minutes) and minimal adverse effects compared to alternative anesthetics like procaine or ropivacaine. The addition of pre-emptive intracavitary lidocaine represents a cost-effective, low-risk modification that maintains the therapeutic efficacy of UGAES while significantly improving patient comfort during the procedure, making it an important enhancement to standard sclerotherapy protocols for endometrioma management.

title: Unmasking the Masquerade: Navigating the Diagnostic Enigma of Abdominal TB Mimicking Endometriosis
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary:
# Summary

Abdominal tuberculosis presents a significant diagnostic challenge as it mimics endometriosis through overlapping clinical presentations, including abdominal pain, pelvic pain, and menstrual irregularities, while potentially causing infertility. Both conditions elevate CA-125 levels, a biomarker typically associated with ovarian malignancy, further complicating differential diagnosis. Genital tuberculosis characteristically affects the fallopian tubes (90% of cases), uterus (50-80%), ovaries (20-30%), and cervix (5-15%), whereas tuberculous peritonitis produces exudative ascites with peritoneal tubercles alongside fever and weight loss. Diagnostic laparoscopy remains necessary to definitively distinguish abdominal tuberculosis from endometriosis through visualization of characteristic white miliary nodules and ascites, with adenosine deaminase testing in ascitic fluid providing additional diagnostic support. Anti-tuberculous therapy (ATT) administered for six months effectively treats abdominal tuberculosis and reverses associated infertility, highlighting the critical importance of accurate diagnosis to prevent prolonged unnecessary endometriosis treatment.

title: Clinical Characteristics and Locations of Lesions in Patients with Endometriosis Using #ENZIAN Classification: An Observational Study
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a benign gynecological condition that causes chronic pelvic pain, impaired fertility, and dysmenorrhea, with lesions commonly found in the ovaries, pouch of Douglas, and uterosacral ligaments, though extra-pelvic involvement in the bladder, gastrointestinal tract, and umbilicus also occurs. This observational study using the ENZIAN classification system revealed that endometriotic lesions preferentially affect the left side of the pelvis, with left ovarian involvement in 67.4% of patients, rectovaginal space involvement in 61%, and rectal involvement in 33.1% of cases. Beyond the primary symptom of dysmenorrhea affecting most patients, endometriosis associates with diverse clinical manifestations including deep dyspareunia (40.9%), painful bowel movements (43.5%), constipation (33.7%), infertility (40.8%), and systemic symptoms such as fatigue (31.16%) and headache (19.48%). The severity of pain directly correlates with both the depth of lesion invasion and the extent of disease spread, findings that the ENZIAN classification system effectively captures by comprehensively assessing anatomical location, lesion size, adenomyosis involvement, and surgical complexity. In contrast, the older rASRM classification system demonstrates poor correlation with deep infiltrating endometriosis, extra-pelvic locations, and clinical pain outcomes, making ENZIAN superior for predicting surgical complexity and treatment planning in endometriosis management.

title: An Asymptomatic Giant Endometrioma Presented as Lower Extremity Deep Vein Thrombosis with Early Pregnancy: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian endometriomas are cystic lesions arising from ectopic endometrial tissue within the ovary that affect 17–44% of women with endometriosis and typically cause pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, menorrhagia, and infertility. This case report describes an unusual presentation of a giant 30 cm endometrioma that compressed the inferior vena cava and right ureter, resulting in deep vein thrombosis and hydroureteronephrosis during early pregnancy—complications not typically associated with endometriomas. Beyond mechanical compression effects, endometriosis in pregnancy is linked to increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, which impairs maternal endothelial function and contributes to adverse pregnancy outcomes including spontaneous miscarriage, preterm birth, and placenta previa, with high iron content in endometriomas potentially elevating miscarriage risk. Diagnosis relies on transvaginal ultrasound showing characteristic homogenous low-level internal echoes and thick walls, with MRI and laparoscopy providing confirmatory imaging and visualization. Surgical management of huge endometriomas raises clinical challenges regarding balancing fertility preservation against the effects of salpingo-oophorectomy on ovarian reserve, particularly since recurrence is common and negatively impacts fertility outcomes.

title: Correction to: Endometriosis and risk of depression among oral contraceptive users: a pooled analysis of cohort studies from 13 countries
summary: Endometriosis, a condition affecting 6-10% of reproductive-age women, is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue and is strongly associated with dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. The pathogenesis of endometriosis involves multiple interconnected biological mechanisms, including aberrant innervation of endometrial lesions, pathological angiogenesis, and chronic inflammation within the lesion microenvironment.
