Additionally, endometrial lesions in endometriosis undergo epithelial-mesenchymal transition, a process that may facilitate their establishment and progression outside the uterus. These complex pathophysiological alterations collectively contribute to the clinical manifestations of the disease and its reproductive consequences. Understanding these mechanistic underpinnings is essential for developing targeted therapeutic approaches to manage the pain and fertility complications associated with endometriosis.

title: Two-Step Parametrial Endometriosis Nodule Excision Using Virtual Reality Technology and 3D Modelling for Surgical Planning
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

This case report presents a 29-year-old woman with parametrial endometriosis causing severe dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia who underwent successful surgical management. MRI imaging identified a left parametrial endometriosis nodule (21×19 mm) involving the left ureter, rectum, vaginal fornix, and uterosacral ligament, with extensive fibrous adhesions complicating the disease. Virtual reality technology combined with 3D modeling enhanced presurgical planning by enabling detailed anatomical visualization and allowing the surgical team to design a two-step robotic excision strategy. The da Vinci Xi robot-assisted procedure successfully performed left ureterolysis, bowel nodule shaving, and full-thickness vaginal wall excision, resulting in dramatic symptom improvement with dysmenorrhea pain reduced to 2/10 and complete resolution of dyspareunia. The authors emphasize that virtual reality imaging and 3D modeling are particularly valuable for complex parametrial endometriosis cases, improving surgeon anatomical comprehension and enabling precise disease excision while minimizing complications. This case demonstrates that robotic surgery combined with advanced VR-guided planning represents an effective approach for managing deep infiltrating endometriosis involving multiple anatomical structures.

title: Endometriosis and Nutrition: Therapeutic Perspectives
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, hormone-dependent inflammatory disorder affecting 10% of reproductive-age women and up to 60% of those with infertility, characterized by ectopic endometrial implants that cause dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, and gastrointestinal symptoms including constipation and diarrhea. The disease pathogenesis centers on chronic inflammation driven by ovulation and menstruation, with elevated macrophage activity and inflammatory mediators like TNF-α promoting cellular proliferation, angiogenesis, and lesion development, while oxidative stress further perpetuates disease progression. Emerging evidence demonstrates that dietary patterns substantially modulate the inflammatory environment and symptom severity by affecting estrogen metabolism, oxidative stress, and sex hormone-binding globulin levels, with diets rich in antioxidants, polyunsaturated fatty acids, and vitamins D, C, and E—including Mediterranean dietary patterns with abundant vegetables and fruits—reducing endometriosis symptoms and lesion growth. Conversely, consumption of processed foods, red meat, dairy products, and unhealthy fats exacerbates disease manifestations, while regular exercise reduces estrogen levels and symptom severity. Dietary interventions offer dual therapeutic benefits by alleviating pelvic pain and improving fertility outcomes while reducing lesion recurrence, positioning nutrition as a key adjunctive therapeutic strategy alongside conventional medical management of endometriosis.

title: Dietary Supplements for Endometriosis-Associated Pain: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Placebo-Controlled Trials
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-dependent condition affecting approximately 190 million women worldwide, is characterized by three primary pain manifestations: dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and pelvic pain. This systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized placebo-controlled trials evaluated whether dietary supplements could effectively reduce endometriosis-associated pain and improve quality of life. The analysis found no significant differences between dietary supplements and placebo across all three pain domains, with pooled results showing minimal reductions in dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and pelvic pain. Additionally, dietary supplements demonstrated little to no improvement in pain catastrophizing or quality-of-life measures assessed through SF-12 scores and the Endometriosis Health Profile-30. While hormonal treatments remain the first-line therapeutic approach for endometriosis despite concerns about long-term adverse effects in women over 35, the current evidence suggests that dietary supplements lack efficacy for managing endometriosis-associated pain symptoms.

title: Laparoscopic Sciatic Nerve Dissection: A Reproducible Approach
summary: Endometriosis can cause sciatic nerve compression, resulting in sciatica and chronic pelvic pain that radiates to the lower limbs. This article presents laparoscopic sciatic nerve dissection as a surgical treatment approach for patients experiencing endometriosis-related sciatic nerve compression. The procedure involves careful dissection of the compressed nerve through minimally invasive laparoscopic techniques to relieve nerve compression. In the described case of a 28-year-old patient with chronic pelvic pain radiating to the posterior lower limb due to endometriotic nerve compression, the laparoscopic approach successfully reduced symptoms with minimal postoperative morbidity. This work establishes laparoscopic sciatic nerve dissection as a reproducible surgical option for treating this specific manifestation of endometriosis, offering a minimally invasive alternative to address endometriosis-associated sciatic nerve compression.

title: Laparoscopic Optimal Excision of Deep Rectovaginal Endometriosis: Tips and Techniques
summary: Deep rectovaginal endometriosis is a chronic progressive condition characterized by ectopic endometrial implants that bleed and trigger inflammatory responses contributing to pelvic adhesions and symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyschezia, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain. While medical management with progesterone provides temporary relief, surgical excision via laparoscopy remains the definitive treatment for symptom management and improved quality of life. The laparoscopic approach offers significant surgical advantages including enhanced magnification and visualization of peritoneal surfaces and anatomical structures, though it demands high-level surgical skills and careful execution of specialized techniques such as nerve-sparing dissection, pararectal and presacral space opening, sigmoid colon mobilization, ureterolysis, and ovarian suspension to prevent iatrogenic injury. The extent of bowel involvement determines the surgical strategy: discoid resection for intermediate disease and segmental rectal resection for deeper lesions, with segmental and discoid approaches demonstrating considerably lower recurrence rates compared to rectal shaving. Successful management of deep rectovaginal endometriosis requires multidisciplinary team input, comprehensive preoperative imaging with transvaginal ultrasound and MRI, and systematic surgical planning to optimize complete excision and patient outcomes.

title: Pelvic nerve endometriosis: MRI features and key findings for surgical decision
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, is the leading cause of chronic pelvic pain, with deep infiltrating endometriosis frequently involving pelvic nerves through direct extension and perineural spread along autonomic pathways. The pathophysiology involves neuroangiogenesis, whereby ectopic endometrial lesions establish neural and vascular networks characterized by heightened neural density, promoting inflammation and angiogenesis while potentially compressing, distorting, or infiltrating nerve tissue, including the commonly affected inferior hypogastric and sacral plexuses. Clinical manifestations of neural endometriosis are variable and nonspecific, ranging from severe dysmenorrhea and deep dyspareunia to vegetative symptoms such as bladder dysfunction, rectal dysfunction, and lower limb weakness when plexuses are involved. MRI, considered the gold standard for diagnosis, employs advanced techniques including 3D T2-weighted sequences, diffusion tensor imaging, and gadolinium-enhanced imaging to visualize neural involvement and microstructural abnormalities such as reduced fractional anisotropy values. Management integrates medical approaches using GnRH analogs, analgesics, tricyclic antidepressants, and antiepileptics with surgical interventions including laparoscopic nerve-sparing techniques and neuronavigation-guided resection. Detailed preoperative imaging assessment of neural endometriosis extent and severity is essential for surgical decision-making and optimizing patient outcomes.

title: Bladder endometriosis in a patient with undirected manifestations: a case report
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of bladder endometriosis in a 37-year-old woman with atypical clinical manifestations. Bladder endometriosis, involving ectopic endometrial tissue in the posterior bladder wall, typically causes diverse urinary and gynecological symptoms including urinary hesitancy, dysuria, hematuria, suprapubic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and pelvic pain that had persisted for three years in this patient. While endometriosis commonly affects the ovaries, pelvic ligaments, and uterosacral regions through mechanisms involving retrograde menstruation, urinary tract involvement remains rare and may be classified as primary (spontaneous) or secondary (iatrogenic following pelvic surgery). Diagnostic imaging with ultrasound and particularly MRI can support the diagnosis, though laparoscopy with histopathological confirmation remains the gold standard. Treatment options include medical management with estradiol-lowering medications such as oral contraceptives, GnRH agonists, and other hormonal agents, as well as surgical approaches including laparoscopic excision, transurethral resection, or segmental bladder resection. This case underscores the importance of maintaining clinical suspicion for bladder endometriosis as a differential diagnosis of bladder masses despite its rarity, as delayed recognition can impact patient outcomes and fertility.

title: The Hôtel-Dieu MRI Classification of Uterosacral Ligament Involvement in Endometriosis: A Pictorial Guide to Clinical Use
summary: Endometriosis is a common gynecologic condition affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women and causing pelvic pain, infertility, dysmenorrhea, deep dyspareunia, and other debilitating symptoms. Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) preferentially affects the uterosacral ligaments (USLs), which are involved in 70% of DIE cases, along with the rectum, bladder, and ureters. The Hôtel-Dieu (HTD) MRI classification system provides a standardized diagnostic approach for USL involvement, distinguishing six morphologic types with varying diagnostic accuracy: nodular types (4, 5, and 6) achieve 100% positive predictive value for DIE, while linear types (1, 2, 3A, and 3B) show lower accuracy ranging from 75-88%. MRI remains the most effective non-invasive imaging modality for detecting DIE, and its diagnostic performance can be further enhanced by identifying supporting features such as torus uterinus thickening and ovarian endometriomas. The HTD classification may be complemented by emerging biomarkers such as microRNA signatures (including salivary tests like Endotest®) and potentially artificial intelligence algorithms, particularly for linear USL types where MRI alone demonstrates lower specificity.

title: Does BMI Have an Impact on Endometriosis Symptoms and Endometriosis Types According to the #ENZIAN Classification?
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic implantation of endometrial tissue, causes chronic inflammation and multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. This study reveals a paradoxical relationship between BMI and endometriosis manifestations: while higher BMI is associated with increased infertility risk, low/normal BMI (<25 kg/m²) correlates with significantly greater chronic pelvic pain intensity and more frequent dyschezia in patients with deep infiltrating endometriosis. The mechanistic basis involves divergent adipokine signaling—lean individuals exhibit M2 macrophage polarization and enhanced lesion innervation that sensitize pain pathways, whereas obesity promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines (leptin, resistin) and elevated estrogen and IGF-1 levels that support lesion persistence but impair ovulation. These findings suggest that BMI influences endometriosis symptom severity and fertility outcomes through distinct immunometabolic pathways, with lean BMI amplifying pelvic pain through enhanced angiogenesis and neural sensitization, while obesity compromises fertility through hormonal dysregulation despite potentially reducing pain perception.

title: Diagnostic Value of Serum Cancer Antigen 125, Carcinoembryonic
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent benign disorder clinically characterized by dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, with persistent ovarian cysts representing the most common manifestation. Multiple serum biomarkers including cancer antigen 125 (CA-125), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), and inflammatory markers such as neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio, and white blood cell count are significantly elevated in endometriosis patients compared to controls. CA-125, a protein produced by coelomic epithelial cells lining the endometrium, ovaries, fallopian tubes, and peritoneum, demonstrates diagnostic value with an area under the curve of 0.896 and optimal cut-off of 12.7 IU/mL (93% sensitivity). The combination of CA-125 and NLR achieves superior diagnostic performance with an AUC of 0.903, superior to either biomarker alone, and both markers correlate positively with endometriosis severity score. These biomarkers reflect the systemic inflammatory state underlying endometriosis and vary by menstrual phase and disease type, offering potential noninvasive diagnostic tools to complement clinical assessment in endometriosis diagnosis.

title: Risk of endometriosis progression in infertile women trying to conceive naturally or using IVF
summary: Endometriosis presents a complex therapeutic challenge in infertile women, with a fundamental tension between disease control and fertility preservation. While all established hormonal treatments—including GnRH agonists/antagonists, progestins, and estroprogestins—effectively reduce peripheral estrogen levels, suppress ovulation, and prevent disease progression with ~12% recurrence rates, they simultaneously hinder natural conception. In contrast, ovarian stimulation for IVF, which raises estrogen levels up to tenfold compared to natural cycles, generally has minimal impact on disease overall but may promote progression of deep invasive endometriosis (21% untreated progression rate), particularly risking bowel obstruction or ureteral complications. The disease itself causes severe debilitation through dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and dysuria, while damaging ovarian reserve through endometriomas and compromising nearby organ integrity through deep lesions. Current evidence supports ART as the preferred approach for endometriosis-related infertility, with age and residual ovarian reserve as key determinants of success, while the Endometriosis Fertility Index reliably predicts natural conception outcomes post-surgery. Post-surgical long-term medical therapy with progestins or estroprogestins significantly reduces recurrence risk, and discontinuation leads to cumulative recurrence correlating directly with treatment-free duration.

title: Targeting NGF but not VEGFR1 or BDNF signaling reduces endometriosis-associated pain in mice
summary: This study identifies nerve growth factor (NGF) signaling through the TrkA receptor as a critical mediator of endometriosis-associated pain in a mouse model. Endometriotic lesions trigger increased NGF expression, which promotes neurogenesis and contributes to the chronic pain symptoms characteristic of the disease, including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and deep pelvic pain. Notably, NGF-TrkA signaling, rather than BDNF-TrkB or VEGF-VEGFR1 pathways, drives the pain phenotype, as neither anti-BDNF antibodies nor VEGFR1 inhibition reduced pain despite sufficient receptor occupancy. Treatment with entrectinib, a pan-Trk inhibitor, or direct anti-NGF neutralization effectively reduced evoked pain, spontaneous pain, and thermal discomfort while also decreasing lesion size. These findings suggest that NGF-TrkA axis represents a selective therapeutic target for endometriosis-associated pain that may overcome limitations of current treatments such as NSAIDs, opiates, and hormonal therapies.

title: Hemorrhagic ascites in endometriosis: a case series and clinical implications
summary: Endometriosis is a prevalent estrogen-dependent condition affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-aged women, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that can implant in multiple sites including the ovaries, peritoneum, lungs, and diaphragm. While commonly associated with chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, this case series highlights hemorrhagic ascites as a rare but important clinical manifestation, particularly in nulliparous women with advanced (stage IV) disease involving deep infiltrating lesions of the rectum, uterosacral ligaments, and retrocervical space. The pathogenesis of hemorrhagic ascites involves multiple mechanisms: ectopic endometrial cells stimulate peritoneal cells to produce ascitic fluid, endometriotic cysts rupture causing peritoneal irritation, and estrogen and progesterone modulate inflammatory pathways that increase vascular permeability and promote ascites formation. Additionally, women with endometriosis exhibit elevated hemoglobin and reduced antioxidant parameters in peritoneal fluid, creating an oxidative environment that contributes to ascitic fluid formation and vascular fragility of ectopic tissue. Medical management includes aromatase inhibitors (letrozole), progestins (dienogest), and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists, though surgical intervention may be necessary for advanced cases. The clinical presentation of endometriosis-related hemorrhagic ascites can be mistaken for ovarian malignancy, necessitating careful diagnostic consideration using transvaginal ultrasound, MRI, and tumor marker assessment (CA125).

title: Ovarian remnant syndrome: an unsuspected diagnosis
summary: Ovarian remnant syndrome (ORS) is a rare but clinically significant complication often arising from incomplete ovarian tissue removal during surgery for endometriosis, which is the most frequent indication for oophorectomy in affected women. The condition develops when distorted pelvic anatomy and extensive adhesions—hallmark features of endometriosis—complicate surgical removal or when residual ovarian tissue remains following incomplete resection or morcellation in the pelvis. Patients with ORS present with chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, cyclic pelvic pain, dysuria, and tenesmus, with residual ovarian tissue posing a concerning risk of malignant transformation, particularly into endometrioid ovarian carcinoma. Medical management through ovarian suppression using oral contraceptives, gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues, or medroxyprogesterone may control symptoms, while definitive surgical treatment via laparoscopy, laparotomy, or robotic surgery can eliminate the condition, with minimally invasive approaches offering reduced blood loss and fewer postoperative complications. Regular transvaginal ultrasound surveillance is essential for early detection in women with prior salpingo-oophorectomy and suspected ORS, particularly given the association between endometriosis and increased risk of clear cell and endometrioid ovarian cancers.

title: WERF Endometriosis Phenome and Biobanking Harmonisation Project for Experimental Models in Endometriosis Research (EPHect-EM-Pain): methods to assess pain behaviour in rodent models of endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis causes a multifaceted pain syndrome characterized by dysmenorrhoea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, dysuria, and chronic pelvic pain affecting 71-87% of patients, alongside associated co-morbidities including bladder pain syndrome, irritable bowel syndrome, and vulvodynia. The heterogeneous endometriotic lesions, which contain endometrial-like epithelial and stromal components with extensive vascularization, innervation, and immune cell infiltration, generate a pro-inflammatory microenvironment that activates and sensitizes nociceptors to drive pain pathways. Estrogen plays a central regulatory role in shaping lesion cellular composition and promoting neuroangiogenesis and neuroinflammation, while peripheral cross-organ sensitization contributes to the complex pain phenotypes observed clinically. Pain heterogeneity arises from multiple factors including lesion subtype, composition differences, adhesions, neuropathic mechanisms, and post-surgical effects. Rodent models of experimental endometriosis have proven instrumental in elucidating pain mechanisms by studying nerve cell growth, inflammation, neuroangiogenesis, and central nervous system activation. The study emphasizes critical methodological considerations for rodent pain assessment, including proper handling techniques and strain selection, as inappropriate handling induces stress-related analgesia and phenotype replication failures, with C57BL/6 and Sprague-Dawley strains being most commonly utilized for endometriosis pain investigations.

title: Ureteral endometriosis post-hysterectomy for adenomyosis: a case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents ureteral endometriosis developing post-hysterectomy in a patient initially treated for adenomyosis, highlighting the risks of incomplete management in estrogen-dependent disease. Adenomyosis and endometriosis are closely associated chronic inflammatory conditions sharing estrogen-dependent pathophysiology; adenomyosis causes dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, and uterine enlargement, while endometriosis more broadly triggers dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, infertility, and urinary/bowel dysfunction. Ureteral endometriosis, a rare deep infiltrating endometriosis subtype frequently associated with ovarian endometriotic cysts and uterosacral ligament involvement, can progress to cause lumbar pain, hematuria, hydronephrosis, and ureteral dilation. The gold standard treatment combines complete surgical excision via laparoscopic approaches with postoperative GnRH agonist therapy (such as goserelin), which reduces recurrence risk and symptom progression; notably, incomplete lesion excision and absence of adjuvant hormonal therapy increase recurrence likelihood, particularly in patients under 40 years with severe presurgical dysmenorrhea or high ASRM scores. Nerve-sparing surgical techniques improve postoperative pelvic organ, urinary, and bowel function, while combined imaging with transvaginal ultrasonography, MRI, and CT, along with histopathological confirmation, enables accurate diagnosis and treatment planning.

title: The usefulness of transvaginal ultrasonography in the diagnosis of pelvic venous disorders
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic venous disorders (PeVD) are a distinct clinical entity characterized by the coexistence of chronic pelvic pain and pelvic varicose veins, presenting with a diverse symptom complex including dyspareunia, postcoital pain, urinary urgency, vulvar swelling, and abnormal vaginal discharge or bleeding. Transvaginal ultrasonography (TVUS) effectively identifies key sonographic markers of PeVD, including ovarian vein dilatation (>8 mm), reduced blood flow velocity in ovarian veins (<3 cm/s), venous reflux (>1 second), and dilatation of arcuate veins within the myometrium. The study validates TVUS as a cost-effective, non-invasive diagnostic tool for PeVD screening, with findings subsequently confirmed by pelvic venography, the gold standard diagnostic method. Because chronic pelvic pain can result from multiple etiologies including endometriosis, adenomyosis, uterine fibroids, and ovarian tumors, TVUS offers a practical advantage by enabling comprehensive pelvic assessment in an office-based setting. These findings establish TVUS as a useful first-line imaging modality for detecting pelvic venous pathology in patients with chronic pelvic pain.

title: Primary Care Approach to Endometriosis: Diagnostic Challenges and Management Strategies—A Narrative Review
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, with rates reaching 50% in infertile populations, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue primarily in pelvic organs and the peritoneal cavity. The condition causes significant morbidity through chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, with additional symptoms including dysuria, dyschezia, and fatigue that substantially impair quality of life. Pathogenesis is multifactorial and involves several interconnected mechanisms: retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, vascular and lymphatic dissemination, altered immunity, imbalanced cell proliferation and apoptosis, abnormal endocrine signaling, and genetic factors—all underpinned by an estrogen-dependent inflammatory response that produces elevated cytokines, chemokines, and prostaglandins. Multiple risk factors span prenatal and postnatal periods, including intrauterine diethylstilbestrol exposure, early menarche, low birth weight, short menstrual cycles, low body mass index, and nulliparity, alongside genetic predisposition indicated by strong family history patterns. Diagnosis combines transvaginal ultrasound as the first-line imaging modality with laparoscopy and biopsy as the gold standard, while management employs analgesics, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, and hormonal therapies including combined oral contraceptives and progestins. Associated comorbidities including anxiety, migraine, and allergic rhinitis frequently accompany endometriosis, underscoring its complex systemic nature.

title: Screening for endometriosis: A scoping review of screening measures that could support early diagnosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a systemic inflammatory condition affecting 6–10% of women of reproductive age, presents with multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility, with diagnostic delays contributing significantly to disease progression and chronic complications. Because endometriosis symptoms overlap with other gastrointestinal and gynecological conditions and require surgical confirmation for definitive diagnosis, the review identified and evaluated patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) as potential screening tools to enable earlier detection and reduce diagnostic delays. The ENDOPAIN-4D emerged as the highest-quality screening measure suitable for primary care settings, demonstrating strong criterion validity (AUC of 0.95) and positive measurement properties, though it showed moderate reliability with a weighted Kappa of 0.63. Machine learning algorithms, particularly those developed by Bendifallah et al., offered superior measurement properties for tertiary care settings and showed promise for automated endometriosis diagnosis with AUC values ranging from 0.50–0.93. Early detection through reliable symptom-based screening tools can substantially minimize diagnostic delays, chronic pain, infertility complications, and disease progression, addressing a critical gap since approximately 60% of endometriosis cases remain undiagnosed and leave patients struggling with persistent pain and reduced quality of life.

title: Exploring endometriosis before surgical treatment: unraveling pain, sexual function and quality of life patterns
journal: Revista Brasileira de Ginecologia e Obstetrícia
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that causes multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dysuria, dyschezia, and chronic pelvic pain, while also significantly impairing sexual function and quality of life in affected women. The disease is associated with infertility in 30-50% of patients, with mechanisms involving mechanical factors, pain, and altered gamete function and transport. Notably, endometriosis also correlates with depression, anxiety, and fatigue, creating a complex burden beyond pain alone. Using cluster analysis, the study identified that severe disease (ASRM stage IV) correlates with Cluster B, which demonstrates significantly worse dyspareunia, dysuria, and sexual dysfunction compared to Cluster A (predominantly ASRM stage III), indicating that disease severity strongly predicts sexual and psychological morbidity. The findings underscore that the current ASRM classification system, while useful for staging, inadequately captures pain, sexual dysfunction, and quality-of-life impacts, highlighting the need for more comprehensive assessment approaches before surgical intervention.

title: Impact of Sarcopenia on Outcomes Following Endovascular Treatment of Patients with Pelvic Venous Insufficiency
summary:
# Summary

Sarcopenia, characterized by loss of skeletal muscle mass and associated with poor dietary habits and sedentary lifestyle, substantially impairs outcomes of endovascular treatment for pelvic venous insufficiency (PVI), a major cause of chronic pelvic pain. A moderate negative correlation was observed between sarcopenia status and treatment success, with non-sarcopenic patients achieving 100% clinical success compared to significantly lower rates in sarcopenic patients, and demonstrating greater reductions in visual analog pain scores post-treatment. The mechanism underlying this differential response involves sarcopenia-induced pelvic muscle weakness and pelvic floor dysfunction, which cause loss of muscle synchrony, urinary retention, and perpetuate chronic pelvic pain—conditions that coexist with and potentially compound the effects of PVI. Beyond its direct association with chronic pain and limited physical activity, sarcopenia reduces cardiopulmonary performance and increases mortality risk, compromising the physiological capacity needed for successful vascular intervention. These findings demonstrate that baseline muscle health modulates endovascular treatment efficacy, suggesting that sarcopenia assessment should be incorporated into risk stratification for patients with PVI-related chronic pelvic pain, a symptom complex that overlaps with endometriosis, adenomyosis, and uterine fibroids as common gynecological causes of pelvic pain.

title: Diagnostic Accuracy of MRI Using the #Enzian Classification in Endometriosis: A Single-Center Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that responds to hormonal cycles, causes multiple clinical manifestations including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, often accompanied by adhesions and pelvic scarring. While the exact etiology remains unclear, retrograde menstruation, immune dysfunction, and genetic factors have been proposed as causative mechanisms. Magnetic resonance imaging using the Enzian classification has emerged as a valuable diagnostic tool for detecting and mapping endometriotic lesions, particularly deep infiltrating endometriosis in compartments such as the uterosacral ligaments and rectovaginal septum, where accuracy reaches 42.9-60%. However, MRI demonstrates suboptimal sensitivity for superficial peritoneal lesions and extragenital sites (bladder, ureter, rectum), with overall lesion localization accuracy of only 31.3% and size estimation at 20.3%. Patient factors including older age, higher parity, and gastrointestinal symptoms were significantly associated with improved MRI diagnostic accuracy. Achieving consistent and accurate Enzian staging requires specialized radiologists and standardized imaging protocols, making MRI a valuable complementary rather than definitive modality for surgical planning in endometriosis.

title: The Effect of Dienogest on Deep Endometriosis Nodules Involving the Recto-Sigmoid Colon: A Prospective Longitudinal Long-Term Study
summary: This prospective longitudinal study demonstrates that Dienogest, a progestin, effectively treats deep endometriosis involving the recto-sigmoid colon by significantly reducing symptom severity and lesion volume. Deep endometriosis affecting the bowel, particularly the sigmoid rectum, manifests with multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and chronic pelvic pain that substantially impact quality of life. Long-term Dienogest therapy improved dysmenorrhea in 85% of patients, dyspareunia in 66%, and dyschezia in 82%, while also reducing median lesion volume from 1.1 mL to 0.9 mL, though maximum lesion diameter remained unchanged. Notably, symptom severity did not correlate with lesion size, suggesting that medical management provides symptomatic relief through hormonal suppression mechanisms independent of structural lesion reduction. The maintenance of amenorrhea during treatment was the most protective factor against lesion progression, indicating that continuous hormonal suppression prevents disease advancement in recto-sigmoid endometriosis. These findings support Dienogest as an effective long-term medical therapy option for managing deep colorectal endometriosis and improving patient outcomes without surgical intervention.

title: The Hidden Danger in Endometriosis: Bilateral Pelvic Abscesses Following Fertility Treatment
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition affecting approximately 10-15% of reproductive-age women, causes debilitating pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility by impairing tubal function, reducing oocyte quality, and creating a hostile peritoneal environment. Advanced endometriosis frequently leads to endometriomas, hydrosalpinx, and chronic pelvic adhesions that compromise fertility and increase the risk of complications during assisted reproductive technology procedures. This case report highlights a critical hidden danger: in women with pre-existing endometriosis undergoing in vitro fertilization, embryo transfer and endometrial manipulation can trigger ascending infections that progress to bilateral tubo-ovarian abscesses, pelvic inflammatory disease, and potentially life-threatening sepsis. The patient presented eight months post-IVF with elevated inflammatory markers (CRP 298 mg/L) and imaging findings of multiloculated bilateral pelvic abscesses with hematopyosalpinx requiring surgical intervention. While medical therapies including GnRH agonists, progestins, and combined oral contraceptives can suppress endometriosis, and surgical excision improves pain and fertility outcomes, clinicians must recognize that endometriosis symptoms can overlap with pelvic inflammatory disease, delaying diagnosis of serious infectious complications. This case underscores the importance of careful patient selection, pre-treatment assessment, and heightened clinical vigilance for infectious complications in endometriosis patients pursuing fertility treatment.

title: Diagnostic Tools for Endometriosis in Poland: A Comparative Assessment of Reliability and Out-of-Pocket Costs
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease with multisystemic effects, causing dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and fatigue while impairing fertility and quality of life. The condition triggers systemic inflammation, alters hepatic and adipose tissue metabolism, dysregulates brain gene expression leading to pain sensitization and mood disorders, and is associated with dysbiotic microbiota alterations including increased Proteobacteria, Enterobacteriaceae, Streptococcus, and Escherichia coli. Transvaginal ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging demonstrate ≥90% sensitivity and specificity for diagnosis and are recommended by ESHRE guidelines, though imaging has limited ability to detect superficial endometriosis and moderate sensitivity for deep disease. Blood-based and saliva-based biomarker tests currently available in private markets lack adequate sensitivity and specificity (below 90%) and should not be used for diagnosis according to clinical recommendations. Significant cost disparities exist between public and private healthcare sectors, with private transvaginal ultrasound and MRI costing substantially more, potentially creating financial barriers that contribute to diagnostic delays. The lack of specialist training in transvaginal ultrasound techniques and social normalization of women's pain further delay diagnosis, emphasizing the critical need for improved diagnostic accessibility and clinical education.

title: Development and validation of MRI-based radiomics model for clinical symptom stratification of extrinsic adenomyosis
summary: Adenomyosis, characterized by pathological invasion of endometrial glands and stroma into the myometrium, presents with heterogeneous clinical symptoms including pain, abnormal uterine bleeding, and infertility. This study developed and validated machine learning-based MRI radiomics models to differentiate and stratify these distinct symptom presentations in extrinsic adenomyosis. The radiomic models achieved robust diagnostic performance with areas under the curve of 0.73 for pain, 0.82 for abnormal uterine bleeding, and 0.84 for infertility, with combined clinical-radiomic nomograms showing improved stratification accuracy. Radiomic features extracted from T2-weighted MRI images correlated with tissue heterogeneity and enabled identification of high-risk patients, particularly those at risk of infertility, allowing for personalized treatment planning and prediction of therapeutic outcomes including response to magnetic resonance-guided focused ultrasound ablation. Independent clinical factors such as age, deep infiltrating endometriosis, body mass index, and CA125 levels further informed symptom prediction across different patient groups. These findings establish MRI-based radiomics as a promising non-invasive imaging biomarker tool for comprehensive adenomyosis diagnosis and personalized clinical management strategies.

title: Factors Affecting IVF Success after Laparoscopic Surgery in Women with Endometriosis
journal: Medical Journal of the Islamic Republic of Iran
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes infertility and chronic pelvic pain through complex genetic, immunological, and hormonal mechanisms. Laparoscopic surgery treats endometriosis and improves IVF outcomes by removing endometriotic tissue, reducing inflammation, and enhancing pelvic anatomy, with particularly favorable results in deep infiltrating endometriosis when combined with postoperative GnRH agonist therapy. However, surgical excision of ovarian endometriomas carries a significant risk of reducing ovarian reserve, as reflected in decreased anti-Müllerian hormone levels, which independently correlate with IVF success. IVF outcomes are influenced by multiple factors including disease severity according to the American Society for Reproductive Medicine classification, duration of infertility, time lag between diagnosis and treatment, and pre-surgical AMH levels. Postoperative GnRH agonist therapy enhances uterine receptivity and suppresses residual endometriotic activity, increasing clinical pregnancy rates approximately fourfold in endometriosis patients. Clinicians should tailor treatment by considering specific lesion type, disease extent, and individual ovarian reserve status when planning laparoscopic surgery and subsequent IVF protocols.

title: Mapping deep endometriosis in patients with ovarian endometriomas according to the #Enzian classification: a single-center retrospective analysis
summary: This retrospective study examined the spatial distribution of deep endometriosis in 121 patients with ovarian endometriomas using the Enzian classification system. The majority of patients (87.6%) with ovarian endometriomas had coexisting deep endometriosis, predominantly involving the uterosacral ligaments and rectovaginal septum. A significant spatial correlation was observed between ipsilateral ovarian endometriomas and same-sided deep endometriosis in pelvic compartment B, with left-sided predominance noted particularly in patients under 35 years old. Patients with bilateral ovarian endometriomas demonstrated higher rates of left Enzian B involvement, rectovaginal septum involvement, and right-sided tubal involvement, and were more likely to receive hormonal treatment. The study emphasizes that careful preoperative screening using transvaginal ultrasound and MRI is essential to identify deep endometriosis in all patients with ovarian endometriomas, as this knowledge is crucial for surgical planning and patient counseling regarding the extent of disease requiring treatment with procedures such as cystectomy or ureterolysis.

title: The impact of surgical intervention on time to conception and obstetric outcomes in women with endometriomas: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriomas, ovarian manifestations of endometriosis found in 17-44% of patients with the disease, are associated with reduced fertility and infertility, though they do not invariably cause conception failure. This retrospective cohort study compared surgical versus nonsurgical management of endometriomas and found that while laparoscopic cystectomy is technically associated with lower recurrence rates and higher pregnancy rates, surgical treatment paradoxically prolonged time to conception compared to conservative management (24 versus 18 months). Importantly, surgical intervention did not improve live birth rates, delivery outcomes, mode of delivery, or pregnancy complications between groups, suggesting that surgery's potential benefit in reducing inflammatory factors and recurrence may be offset by its risk of reducing ovarian reserve. Nonsurgical management preserved ovarian reserve and was associated with significantly higher probability of conception over time, particularly in patients without large cysts (>4 cm). These findings suggest that conservative management should be considered as an initial approach for endometrioma-related infertility, with surgery reserved for select cases such as larger cysts or failed conservative management.

title: Current status of robot-assisted surgery implementation in endometriosis centers: an international multicentric cross-sectional study
summary: Endometriosis, a condition affecting women of reproductive age, causes multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dysuria, dyschezia, infertility, and chronic pelvic pain, significantly impacting quality of life. Treatment approaches include endocrine therapy for pain management and surgical removal of lesions, with minimally invasive surgery (MIS) now established as the standard for deep endometriosis, which often involves multiple pelvic structures including the bowel and ureter. This international multicentric study reveals that robot-assisted surgery (RAS) has gained considerable traction in endometriosis centers, with 60.8% of centers with robotic systems using them for endometriosis management, demonstrating an upward trend with use nearly tripling. RAS offers significant technical advantages over conventional laparoscopic surgery (CLS), including enhanced precision, improved visualization, and superior instrument mobility, with 69.2% of centers reporting better patient outcomes and 73.1% indicating preference for RAS if costs were equivalent. Primary barriers to RAS adoption are cost (100% of centers without systems cite this) and limited scientific evidence, though RAS is particularly preferred in complex multidisciplinary cases, obese patients, and deep endometriosis involving specific anatomical sites. Academic and tertiary centers predominate in RAS implementation, with 69.2% maintaining dedicated robotic teams, simulators, and second consoles to support training and research initiatives.

title: Is there a relationship between symptoms and types of endometriosis according to #ENZIAN? A comparative study of preoperative questionnaires
summary: This comparative study using the #ENZIAN classification system reveals distinct symptom-phenotype relationships across different endometriosis presentations. Endometriosis is characterized by endometrium-like tissue outside the uterus, triggering inflammatory processes, fibrosis, and adhesions that manifest as the classic "4 Ds"—dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dysuria, and dyschezia—along with chronic pelvic pain and gastrointestinal symptoms including nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and bloating. The study demonstrates important compartment-specific symptom correlations: severe dyschezia and bowel-related symptoms associate with C compartment (bowel endometriosis) involvement, dyspareunia correlates with adenomyosis in the FA compartment, and dysmenorrhea predominates across peritoneal disease (Group 1). Notably, chronic pelvic pain prevalence was significantly lower in patients with bowel deep infiltrating endometriosis compared to peritoneal and ovarian groups, likely because deep bowel lesions induce localized pain through visceral hypersensitivity rather than diffuse pelvic discomfort. Modern imaging modalities including transvaginal ultrasound and MRI effectively identify deep infiltrating endometriosis and ovarian endometriomas but are less effective for superficial peritoneal lesions. These findings support the clinical utility of #ENZIAN classification in predicting symptom profiles and guiding targeted diagnostic and therapeutic approaches based on disease localization.

title: Endometriosis—The scapegoat for pelvic pain?
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a well-established cause of chronic pelvic pain and associated symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and defecation pain, though the relationship between disease presence and symptom severity is complex and nonlinear. While endometriosis is found in approximately 30% of chronic pelvic pain patients, a significant proportion of women with extensive disease remain asymptomatic, suggesting that endometriosis alone does not fully explain pelvic pain in all cases. Deep endometriosis affecting the vagina, uterosacral ligaments, and rectovaginal area shows the strongest associations with severe dyspareunia and cyclic defecation pain, indicating that anatomical location and depth of lesions are important determinants of symptoms. The underlying mechanisms of endometriosis-associated pain involve multiple factors beyond structural disease, including central sensitization, psychological factors (anxiety and depression), and behavioral/social influences that collectively contribute to chronic pain persistence. Adenomyosis frequently coexists with endometriosis and confounds symptom attribution, sharing similar pain mechanisms and symptoms. The therapeutic challenge is highlighted by uncertain benefits of lesion removal on pain relief and the persistence of symptoms even after hysterectomy in some cases, underscoring the need for comprehensive, multifactorial approaches addressing pain sensitization mechanisms rather than focusing solely on disease extent.

title: Certification of a Rural Endometriosis Clinic Leads to Improvement in the Diagnosis and Treatment of Endometriosis – a Retrospective Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue outside the uterus typically located in the pelvis, affects approximately ten percent of reproductive-age women and presents with a constellation of symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, pain, and infertility. This retrospective analysis demonstrates that EuroEndoCert certification of a rural endometriosis clinic substantially improved diagnostic accuracy and surgical outcomes, with post-certification cases showing increased transvaginal ultrasound utilization (47.7% versus 35.6%) and enhanced adenomyosis recognition (65.8% versus 57.0%). Surgical management became more targeted and conservative following certification, with higher rates of complete lesion removal (92.5% versus 87.8%), increased use of organ-sparing techniques such as rectum shaving (2.3% to 7.5%), and decreased unnecessary procedures including salpingectomy and ovariectomy. Patient outcomes significantly improved post-certification, with 40.5% of patients achieving pain-free status at twelve months compared to 34.5% previously, accompanied by better documentation of disease extent using the Enzian classification system and more consistent follow-up assessments (64.1% versus 40.7%). Certification furthermore enhanced recognition of site-specific involvement, particularly improved documentation of rectal lesions in symptomatic patients and better stratification of surgical approaches based on lesion size classification.

title: Evaluation of the association between self-reported pre-operative symptoms with surgically diagnosed endometriosis using the #ENZIAN classification in a multi-centre cohort
summary: This multi-centre study evaluated the correlation between pre-operative patient-reported symptoms and surgically confirmed endometriosis using the #ENZIAN classification system. Dysmenorrhea was nearly universal (98.5% of 521 patients), while dyspareunia, dyschezia, and dysuria were reported by 56.4%, 39.9%, and 19.6% of patients respectively. The localization of endometrial lesions strongly predicted specific symptom presentations: uterosacral ligament and parametrial involvement associated with dyspareunia, rectal/sigmoid compartment lesions with dyschezia, and bladder lesions with dysuria. Adenomyosis demonstrated particularly high symptom prevalence (86-90% across all four symptom types) and was especially associated with severe dyspareunia, while deep infiltrating endometriosis showed approximately 50% dyschezia prevalence. Importantly, involvement of multiple pelvic compartments substantially increased pain severity, and larger lesion size within compartments generally correlated with higher symptom prevalence, though this relationship was not uniform across all sites. These findings demonstrate that compartment-specific endometriosis mapping using the ENZIAN classification enables targeted clinical prediction of patient symptomatology.

title: Predicting pain reduction following laparoscopic surgery for endometriosis: a retrospective cohort study using UK national and research databases
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes debilitating pelvic pain, infertility, and fatigue, with symptoms often exacerbated by menstruation and sexual intercourse. This retrospective cohort study identified key predictors of pain relief following laparoscopic surgical treatment. Surgical removal of ovarian and uterosacral ligament endometriosis was strongly associated with pain reduction, whereas treatment of superficial peritoneal endometriosis did not improve outcomes, indicating that lesion location is critical for surgical success. Increasing patient age and concurrent hysterectomy also predicted better pain relief, potentially due to removal of adenomyosis. Conversely, higher baseline pain scores and use of strong painkillers were associated with decreased odds of achieving pain reduction, suggesting more severe disease may have poorer surgical outcomes. These findings support the clinical value of preoperative imaging to identify specific endometriotic lesions that respond favorably to surgical intervention.

title: Recurrent Hemothorax and Hemoperitoneum in Endometriosis: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 34-year-old woman with extra-pelvic endometriosis who presented with recurrent, cyclical hemothorax and hemorrhagic ascites synchronized with her menstrual cycle. Extra-pelvic endometrial tissue can implant in diverse sites including the lungs, diaphragm, gastrointestinal tract, and peritoneum, where it responds to hormonal fluctuations by undergoing proliferation and bleeding. The bleeding from ectopic endometrial implants triggers inflammation and fibrin deposition, leading to fibrinous pleuritis and ascites formation; chronic intra-abdominal bleeding further activates macrophages and inflammatory pathways. Thoracic endometriosis syndrome, the pulmonary manifestation of extra-pelvic disease, most commonly presents as catamenial pneumothorax or hemothorax with characteristic right-sided predominance. While first-line treatment involves NSAIDs and hormonal therapy, this patient's poor tolerance to hormonal agents and development of refractory hemorrhagic complications highlight the need for surgical excision of endometrial implants, though surgical biopsy is required for definitive diagnosis.

title: Sexual health outcomes after colorectal cancer diagnosis in females: a population-based cohort study
journal: JNCI Journal of the National Cancer Institute
summary: This population-based cohort study reveals that females with colorectal cancer (CRC) experience significantly elevated risks of multiple gynecological and sexual health complications compared to controls, with endometriosis risk increased 1.95-fold, pelvic inflammatory disease risk increased 3.42-fold, and dyspareunia risk increased 1.67-fold. Cancer treatments contribute substantially to these outcomes: surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation each independently increase dyspareunia risk by 23-25%, while chemotherapy and radiation compromise immune and anatomical barriers that increase pelvic inflammatory disease susceptibility. In younger women (≤39 years), chemotherapy exposure is associated with a particularly severe 164% increased risk of premature ovarian failure, likely through direct gonadotoxicity and iatrogenic menopause. Left colon and rectal cancers showed the highest associations with pelvic inflammatory disease, suggesting anatomical proximity influences infection risk. Beyond gynecological sequelae, female CRC patients also report vaginal dryness, reduced libido, body image concerns, and poorer mental health outcomes. These findings underscore the importance of comprehensive sexual health counseling and reproductive preservation strategies in female colorectal cancer survivors.

title: First documented case of decidualized endometriosis on the anterior peritoneum during emergency cesarean section: a case report and literature review
summary: This case report documents the first reported instance of decidualized endometriosis identified on the anterior peritoneum during emergency cesarean section, representing an unusual anatomical location since endometriosis typically occurs on the ovaries, uterine serosa, and cesarean scars. Decidualization, a physiological process triggered by elevated progesterone during pregnancy, causes significant morphological changes in ectopic endometrial tissue including stromal cell enlargement and increased vascularization, which can obscure typical endometriotic lesions and create diagnostic challenges. While endometriosis is commonly associated with pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility in reproductive-aged women, its incidental discovery during cesarean section remains rare, and decidualized lesions may mimic malignancy or inflammatory conditions, complicating histopathological evaluation. Immunohistochemical staining for CD10, a sensitive marker of endometrial stroma, was instrumental in confirming the diagnosis by identifying ectopic endometrial glands surrounded by decidualized stroma. The progression or regression of endometriosis during pregnancy varies depending on lesion location, hormonal milieu, and immune responses, highlighting the complex interplay between pregnancy physiology and ectopic endometrial tissue behavior.

title: Clinical value of preoperative serum calcium level in predicting deep infiltrative endometriosis among ovarian endometrioma
summary: This study demonstrates that preoperative serum albumin-adjusted calcium levels are significantly lower in endometriosis patients compared to controls and inversely correlate with endometriosis risk (OR: 0.156), with particularly reduced levels observed in patients with deep infiltrative endometriosis (DIE). While CA 125 levels were elevated in endometriosis cases and positively associated with disease risk (OR: 1.014), the combination of adjusted calcium and CA 125 yielded superior diagnostic performance with an area under the curve of 0.810 for endometriosis detection. The study identifies age as an additional factor, with DIE patients being significantly younger and showing decreased prevalence with advancing age, alongside associations with nulliparity and severe dysmenorrhea. These findings suggest that calcium dysregulation, potentially involving altered ion channel and pump expression, contributes to endometriosis pathogenesis alongside the established role of oxidative stress. The results support the clinical utility of measuring preoperative albumin-adjusted calcium as a biomarker, particularly when combined with CA 125, to improve diagnostic accuracy and predict the presence of DIE in endometriosis patients.

title: Comorbidity analysis and clustering of endometriosis patients using electronic health records
summary: This study leveraged electronic health records to comprehensively map the comorbidity landscape of endometriosis, identifying a diverse array of associated conditions beyond the classically recognized symptoms. Endometriosis is fundamentally characterized by ectopic endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus, which triggers inflammation, scarring, and adhesions that directly contribute to its primary clinical manifestations of chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. Beyond reproductive symptoms, the analysis revealed strong associations with uterine adenomyosis, ovarian cysts, and pelvic adhesions, as well as unexpected comorbidities including autoimmune diseases, migraines, gastrointestinal disorders, and psychiatric conditions, with enriched odds ratios ranging from 4 to 181 for certain conditions. The study identified elevated cancer antigen 125 as a significant biomarker and discovered potential protective associations with hyperlipidemia and statin use, suggesting possible therapeutic avenues. Unsupervised clustering revealed distinct patient subpopulations with different comorbidity patterns, indicating heterogeneous disease phenotypes driven by shared neuroinflammatory and hormonal-immune pathways. These findings underscore endometriosis as a systemic condition with far-reaching consequences beyond the pelvis, with implications for personalized clinical management and identification of therapeutic targets.

title: A Cross-Sectional Study of Endometriosis Surgeries Across Obstetrics and Gynecology Subspecialties: Does Minimally Invasive Gynecologic Surgery (MIGS) Fellowship Training Make a Difference?
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes chronic pelvic pain and infertility through the presence of endometrial-like tissue in the pelvic and abdominal cavities, characterized as a systemic inflammatory disease affecting millions globally. Surgical excision represents the gold-standard treatment and outperforms ablation alone in reducing pain symptoms and improving quality of life, with outcomes significantly enhanced when performed by minimally invasive gynecologic surgery (MIGS) specialists who employ excisional techniques in 30-34% of cases compared to general obstetrics-gynecology surgeons who favor ablation in 78% of cases. MIGS surgeons demonstrate superior diagnostic and therapeutic capabilities, performing laparoscopic procedures in 99.3% of cases, diagnosing deep endometriosis intraoperatively in 66.4% of patients, and excising deep endometriosis in 34.4% of cases, whereas general gynecologists rarely excise deep lesions and open surgery remains prevalent in their practice. Specialized centers with multidisciplinary approaches incorporating advanced imaging protocols to map endometriosis lesions achieve lower complication rates, fewer repeat surgeries, and superior outcomes for both pain and fertility, with MIGS-treated infertility patients achieving pregnancy in 56.3% of cases. However, persistent postoperative pain remains common across all surgical groups, and complex lesions in the rectosigmoid, bladder, and diaphragm are rarely addressed surgically, indicating a need for continued specialized training and comprehensive preoperative imaging protocols to optimize endometriosis management.

title: Adolescent endometriosis: clinical insights and imaging considerations
summary: Adolescent endometriosis is a gynecologic disorder characterized by endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus that causes significant clinical morbidity, including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea (the most common cause of secondary dysmenorrhea in adolescents), dyspareunia, and infertility, with affected adolescents also experiencing higher rates of anxiety, depression, and school absenteeism compared to older patients. Pathologically, atypical endometriosis lesions produce high levels of prostaglandins, and the disease presents in three morphologic forms—superficial peritoneal disease (more prevalent in younger adolescents), deep infiltrating lesions in the uterosacral ligaments and rectosigmoid (more common with advancing age), and ovarian endometriomas—with the prevalence of retrocervical lesions increasing dramatically from 4.7% in patients under 15 years to 65% in those aged 18-20 years. Endometriosis is significantly associated with Müllerian anomalies, particularly obstructive variants, which increase disease incidence to 47% in affected patients. Diagnostic imaging has shifted from laparoscopy toward noninvasive modalities, with transabdominal ultrasound serving as first-line imaging in adolescents, while transvaginal ultrasound and MRI provide superior visualization of superficial and deep disease respectively. Treatment typically includes nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for dysmenorrhea refractory cases, continuous oral contraceptive pills, and hormone suppression therapy to manage endometriosis symptoms.

title: Clinical characteristics of endometriosis with and without dysmenorrhea diagnosed by laparoscopy
summary:
# Summary

Dysmenorrhea is a cardinal symptom of endometriosis, present in approximately 70% of patients, and strongly associated with advanced disease characteristics including deep infiltrating nodules, higher surgical staging, and elevated CA125 levels. Patients with dysmenorrhea tend to be younger at diagnosis and exhibit significantly elevated biomarkers, with CA125 and CA199 levels correlating with dysmenorrhea severity and duration respectively, and both serving as independent risk factors for the condition. The presence, severity, and prolonged duration of dysmenorrhea are strongly linked to progressive endometriosis and adenomyosis development, with adolescent dysmenorrhea predicting substantially higher future endometriosis risk and individuals experiencing frequent dysmenorrhea having an approximately 19-fold increased risk of adult-onset endometriosis compared to those without dysmenorrhea. Additionally, dysmenorrhea duration positively correlates with both surgical staging and uterine adenomyosis incidence, particularly when lasting longer than 5-10 years, suggesting dysmenorrhea severity, duration, and CA125 levels serve as valuable clinical markers of disease severity. These findings support the hypothesis of a progressive natural disease course linking dysmenorrhea to advanced endometriosis and adenomyosis, emphasizing the diagnostic importance of dysmenorrhea evaluation in identifying endometriosis patients at risk for serious pelvic pathology.

title: Deep infiltrating endometriosis: retroperitoneal endometriosis-a case report
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a benign gynecological condition characterized by the presence of endometrial tissue outside the uterine cavity, presenting with dysmenorrhea, infertility, and pelvic masses as primary clinical manifestations. Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) represents a severe form that commonly affects the uterosacral ligaments, rectovaginal space, rectosigmoid, and bladder, with the rare retroperitoneal variant demonstrating potential to invade the ureter and form significant pelvic masses. Although benign, endometriosis exhibits malignant biological behaviors including invasiveness, metastasis, and recurrence, driven by multiple mechanisms such as decreased apoptosis, increased proliferation, oxidative stress, matrix metalloproteinase activity, and neuroangiogenesis. Clinical diagnosis utilizes noninvasive imaging modalities including transvaginal sonography and magnetic resonance imaging, with laparoscopic surgical intervention providing definitive diagnosis and treatment of DIE. Medical management options include GnRH agonists and continuous oral contraceptives to alleviate pain and prevent recurrence, while preoperative ureteral stenting reduces injury risk in cases involving ureteral involvement. The condition carries significant long-term risks, including increased rates of malignant transformation to endometrioid and clear cell carcinomas, ovarian cancer, and concomitant adenomyosis in approximately fifty percent of cases.

title: Endometriosis and Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates a significant bidirectional association between endometriosis and myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS), with women with endometriosis having 2.79-fold higher odds of developing ME/CFS. Both conditions share overlapping pathophysiological mechanisms centered on chronic inflammation and immune dysregulation, where endometrial lesions outside the uterus produce pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) that create persistent low-grade systemic inflammation. This inflammatory burden contributes to hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis dysfunction, dysregulated cortisol patterns, and autonomic nervous system dysfunction, manifesting as dysautonomia with orthostatic intolerance and gastrointestinal disturbances. Additionally, chronic pain signals from endometriosis lesions promote central sensitization, amplifying pain perception and contributing to widespread hyperalgesia and fatigue characteristic of both conditions. Beyond classical endometriosis symptoms (dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, infertility), patients experience systemic manifestations including fatigue, gastrointestinal disturbances, mood disorders, sleep disturbances, and cognitive impairment. The evidence suggests that shared mechanisms of viral infection, chronic immune activation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and neuroinflammation underlie the strong epidemiological association between these two debilitating syndromes.

title: Urinary Urocortin as a Potential Non-Invasive Biomarker in Endometriosis: Exploratory Study with Histone H4
summary: This exploratory study investigates urinary urocortin and histone H4 as potential non-invasive biomarkers for endometriosis diagnosis, addressing the clinical challenge of diagnostic delay caused by reliance on invasive laparoscopy. Urocortin, a corticotropin-releasing hormone family peptide expressed in reproductive tissues including the endometrium, ovary, and placenta, plays important roles in implantation, decidualization, and pregnancy maintenance through interactions with CRH-R1 and CRH-R2 receptors. The study found that urinary urocortin levels were significantly elevated in patients with ovarian endometriosis (0.41–4.23 μg/mL) compared to controls, whereas serum urocortin and histone H4 showed limited diagnostic value across the patient groups. Elevated urocortin expression in ovarian endometriomas and ectopic endometrial tissue correlated with patient age, age at menarche, and disease duration, which collectively explained 28.3% of urinary urocortin variability. Patients with ovarian endometriosis experienced severe clinical manifestations including dysmenorrhea (83%), dyspareunia (60–100%), and chronic pelvic pain (93%), with significant improvements following surgical intervention. These findings suggest that urinary urocortin shows preliminary diagnostic promise as a non-invasive biomarker for ovarian endometriosis, though larger studies are needed to validate its clinical utility.

title: First Presentation of Endometriosis: A Case of Acute Large Bowel Obstruction
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory disorder affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, can rarely present as acute large bowel obstruction when ectopic endometrial tissue infiltrates the bowel wall. In this case, transmural infiltration of endometrial tissue into the sigmoid colon triggered fibrosis, smooth muscle hyperplasia, and stricture formation, ultimately causing mechanical obstruction requiring emergency surgical intervention. While endometriosis typically manifests as pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, bowel involvement produces non-specific gastrointestinal symptoms including bloating, constipation, and rectal bleeding that may persist undiagnosed for extended periods. The diagnosis proved challenging because superficial endoscopic biopsies and imaging modalities failed to detect disease, and the fibrotic sigmoid mass was frequently misdiagnosed as colorectal carcinoma or Crohn's disease; only microscopic histopathology definitively confirmed endometriosis. Although partial or subacute obstruction may respond to hormonal suppression with gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists, this case demonstrates that acute presentations typically require aggressive surgical management to relieve symptoms and improve quality of life. This report underscores the importance of considering endometriosis in the differential diagnosis of bowel obstruction in women of childbearing age, particularly when conventional imaging and initial diagnostic procedures fail to identify a malignant etiology.

title: Cervical Tenderness (Parametropathy) is a Diagnostic Tool for the Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome
summary: Parametropathy, defined as cervical motion tenderness detected through bimanual palpation or vaginal ultrasound, emerges as a highly sensitive diagnostic marker for chronic pelvic pain syndrome, present in 96.7% of pain patients compared to only 7.2% of controls. While endometriosis is a well-established cause of chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, the study identifies parametropathy as a distinct clinical sign localized to the parametrium that independently associates with chronic pelvic pain, with notable predominance on the left side. Multiple etiologies contribute to chronic pelvic pain including pelvic floor dysfunction with myofascial pain syndromes, pelvic congestion syndrome, and inflammatory conditions such as pelvic inflammatory disease. The cervical motion test serves as both a practical screening and confirmatory diagnostic tool, offering clinically relevant sensitivity for detecting parametropathy in chronic pelvic pain patients. This parametropathy-based approach complements existing diagnostic modalities and may help differentiate multiple overlapping pain generators in the complex presentation of chronic pelvic pain syndrome.

title: Should patients undergo endometrioma surgery before IVF/ICSI? a retrospective study with propensity score matching
summary: Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent disorder affecting 35–50% of infertile women, significantly impairs fertility outcomes through multiple mechanisms including reduced oocyte retrieval, lower fertilization rates, and diminished implantation and pregnancy rates. Advanced-stage endometriosis particularly compromises oocyte quantity and ovarian response during assisted reproductive cycles. However, this retrospective propensity score-matched study demonstrates that endometrioma surgery does not improve live birth rates or restore oocyte quantity, and paradoxically carries the risk of iatrogenic ovarian reserve depletion. After matching, patients with endometriosis showed significantly lower normal fertilization rates, day-3 embryo rates, and ovarian sensitivity indices compared to controls, with these deficits persisting regardless of prior surgery. While anti-Müllerian hormone and antral follicle count predict high-quality embryo development, neither these markers nor endometrioma size, age, or AFC predict ultimate live birth outcomes. The findings suggest that for infertile patients with endometriosis undergoing IVF/ICSI, pre-treatment endometrioma excision may not provide reproductive benefit and should be carefully weighed against the risk of further compromising ovarian reserve.

title: Addendum to consensus opinion from the International Deep Endometriosis Analysis (IDEA) group: sonographic evaluation of superficial endometriosis
summary: Superficial endometriosis, characterized by endometrial-like tissue infiltrating the peritoneal surface, is strongly associated with chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, with adjusted prevalence ratios of 1.83 for primary infertility. The pathophysiology involves peritoneal inflammatory mechanisms where estrogen and inflammatory mediators stimulate both mitosis and neoangiogenesis, promoting lesion development and hemorrhage. Sonographic evaluation, particularly sonoPODography, can detect these lesions through distinctive imaging features including hypoechogenic tissue with stromal fibrosis, hyperechogenic foci from hemosiderin deposits, cystic areas, peritoneal pockets, and characteristic lesion morphologies such as convex projections or concave defects, with the pouch of Douglas being the most common site of involvement. The appearance of superficial endometriotic lesions evolves from red through black as hemoglobin deoxygenates to methemoglobin, eventually becoming white with accumulation of biliverdin, bilirubin, and fibrous tissue. Clinical correlates include thickened uterosacral ligaments, ovarian immobility or fixation, and site-specific tenderness, which aid in diagnosis. However, clinicians must differentiate superficial endometriosis from postoperative adhesions, pelvic inflammatory disease, peritoneal carcinomatosis, and menstrual clots, which can produce similar imaging findings.

title: Laparoscopic hysterectomy for deep infiltrating endometriosis: anterior colpotomy first technique
summary: Deep infiltrating endometriosis, particularly affecting the rectovaginal space and adjacent structures such as the uterosacral ligaments, Douglas pouch, and rectosigmoid colon, presents a challenging surgical scenario characterized by severe pelvic pain including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia that often resists medical management. For women without fertility desires, laparoscopic hysterectomy with salpingo-oophorectomy represents the definitive treatment, achieving substantial symptom relief as demonstrated by marked pain reduction lasting at least 25 months post-operatively. The anterior colpotomy first technique has emerged as an innovative surgical refinement that simplifies the technically demanding dissection of the rectovaginal pouch by establishing the correct tissue planes from lateral to midline, thereby substantially reducing the risk of iatrogenic rectal and rectosigmoid injury during specimen removal. This methodological advancement improves overall surgical safety and feasibility in managing complex cases with extensive disease involving the frozen pelvis and "kissing ovaries." By prioritizing anterior entry into the rectovaginal space before deeper dissection, surgeons can more reliably distinguish healthy tissue from endometriotic infiltration, transforming previously complicated hysterectomies into more manageable procedures while preventing serious bowel complications associated with traditional approaches.

title: Enhancing clinical practice: the Endoscore app for automated surgical data capture and endometriosis scoring
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease dependent on estrogen for establishment and proliferation of ectopic endometrial glands and stroma, is strongly associated with infertility, chronic pelvic pain, and pelvic organ dysfunction in women of reproductive age. The study developed and validated a web-based application using a slider-based system to automate surgical data capture and facilitate simultaneous scoring across three established classification systems: the revised American Society for Reproductive Medicine (r-ASRM) classification for overall staging, the Enzian classification for deep endometriosis, and the Endometriosis Fertility Index (EFI) for fertility assessment. The digital tool demonstrated superior efficiency compared to traditional paper-based methods, reducing scoring time by approximately 25 seconds while maintaining very good to excellent interrater agreement for r-ASRM and EFI scores and moderate concordance for Enzian scores. Users reported high satisfaction with the web-based application, which required only low-to-moderate mental effort despite the complexity of simultaneous multi-classification scoring. This digital endometriosis scoring system represents an advancement in standardizing surgical staging, improving clinical documentation efficiency, and supporting comprehensive patient assessment including fertility counseling.

title: Validity and reliability testing of the Pelvic Pain Impact Questionnaire
summary: Endometriosis causes chronic pelvic pain in 71-87% of affected women and is associated with multiple pain symptoms including dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, dyschezia, and dysuria, all of which significantly reduce quality of life and functional capacity. This study validated the Pelvic Pain Impact Questionnaire-Hungarian version (PPIQ-HU) as a reliable and valid instrument for measuring the impact of chronic pelvic pain in endometriosis patients. The PPIQ-HU demonstrated strong convergent validity through moderate positive correlations with pain catastrophizing, perceived stress, and numeric pain ratings, while showing moderate negative correlations with quality-of-life measures assessed by the SF-36, particularly in physical functioning and bodily pain domains. Patients reporting moderate or severe pain showed significantly elevated PPIQ-HU scores compared to those with mild pain, confirming the instrument's ability to discriminate between pain severity levels. The eight-item, five-point Likert scale questionnaire provides a brief, user-friendly clinical tool for assessing how endometriosis-related pelvic pain impacts patients' daily functioning and overall well-being.

title: Robotic ureteral reconstruction for endometriosis-induced strictures: insights from a multi-institutional experience
