summary: Endometriosis causes ureteral strictures through extrinsic compression and infiltration of endometriotic lesions, predominantly affecting the distal ureter and resulting in hydronephrosis that requires clinical attention in premenopausal women presenting with ureteral stricture disease. While traditional ureterolysis can manage ureteral obstruction from endometriosis, extensively infiltrative lesions often require definitive surgical reconstruction due to risks of inadequate treatment and complications such as devascularization and stenosis. This multi-institutional study demonstrates that robotic ureteral reconstruction techniques—including refluxing reimplantation, side-to-side reimplantation, ureteroureterostomy, and buccal mucosa graft ureteroplasty—achieve high surgical success rates (94.7%) with durable outcomes at median follow-up of 22.5 months. The choice of reconstruction technique is tailored to stricture location and extent of endometrial involvement, with blood supply-preserving approaches employed particularly in redo cases or when periureteral vasculature is compromised. Beyond obstructive symptoms of pelvic and flank pain, patients may present with endometriosis-associated manifestations including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, menorrhagia, and hematuria, underscoring the importance of comprehensive clinical evaluation.

title: Clinical Characteristics of Women with Surgical Signs of Superficial Peritoneal Endometriosis but a Negative Histology: A Nested Case-Control Study
summary:
# Summary

This nested case-control study examined women with surgical signs of superficial peritoneal endometriosis (SUP) but negative histological confirmation, revealing that SUP represents a clinically significant disease entity regardless of histological verification. Women with SUP, whether histology-positive or histology-negative, experienced substantially elevated rates of dysmenorrhea, deep dyspareunia, non-cyclic chronic pelvic pain, and primary infertility compared to controls, with the histology-negative subgroup showing particularly severe dysmenorrhea indicators including higher visual analog scale scores, school absenteeism, and syncope during menstruation. Notably, the presence of SUP alone was sufficient to increase infertility risk even without histological confirmation, and the histology-negative subgroup demonstrated a higher familial history of endometriosis (14% versus 1%), suggesting a genetic predisposition component. The authors propose that thermal damage during lesion excision and insufficient stromal and glandular identification may account for false-negative histology, potentially underestimating endometriosis prevalence. Gastrointestinal symptoms were the only manifestation significantly less intense in the histology-negative group, and serum biomarkers such as CA-125 did not discriminate between groups, emphasizing the limitations of current diagnostic approaches for superficial peritoneal endometriosis.

title: Efficacy of endovenous embolization for pelvic congestion syndrome and its impact on ovarian reserve
summary: Pelvic congestion syndrome, characterized by incompetent pelvic venous valves and vascular compression syndromes such as May-Thurner and Nutcracker syndrome, causes chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and dysmenorrhea in affected patients. Endovenous embolization effectively treats pelvic congestion syndrome by reducing symptom severity across all these domains, with statistically significant improvements in visual analog scale scores observed at both 6 and 12 months post-procedure. The treatment also alters menstrual cycle characteristics, including shortening cycle duration and increasing menstrual frequency. However, endovenous embolization reduces anti-Müllerian hormone levels, a key biomarker of ovarian reserve, by 9-14% across patient groups at 6 and 12 months post-treatment, suggesting potential effects on ovarian function. Other reproductive hormones including follicle-stimulating hormone, luteinizing hormone, estradiol, and prolactin remained unchanged, indicating selective impacts on ovarian reserve rather than broad endocrine disruption. These findings highlight that while endovenous embolization provides significant symptomatic relief for pelvic congestion syndrome, particularly in nulliparous patients, careful consideration of its impact on ovarian reserve is warranted in reproductive-aged women planning future fertility.

title: Translation, cross-cultural adaptation into Brazilian Portuguese and electronic migration of the WERF EPHect endometriosis patient questionnaire
journal: Revista Brasileira de Ginecologia e Obstetrícia
summary: This study involved translating and adapting the WERF EPHect endometriosis patient questionnaire into Brazilian Portuguese, a validated tool for comprehensively assessing endometriosis across multiple clinical dimensions. Endometriosis is strongly associated with a spectrum of painful symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and acyclic pelvic pain, as well as infertility, with heterogeneous pathology encompassing superficial peritoneal lesions, ovarian cysts, and deep endometriosis infiltration. Hormonal therapy serves as the first-line approach for managing endometriosis-related pain but lacks efficacy for infertility treatment, while surgical interventions can remove visible lesions but do not provide cure. Beyond pain symptoms, anxiety and depression significantly impact women with endometriosis and are associated with chronic pelvic pain, underscoring the condition's multifactorial burden. Central sensitization is implicated in pelvic pain pathophysiology and predicts poorer pain outcomes following endometriosis surgery, highlighting the importance of considering neurobiological mechanisms in treatment planning. The Brazilian Portuguese adaptation of this questionnaire provides a critical tool for clinicians to systematically evaluate the complex clinical manifestations and psychosocial impacts of endometriosis in Portuguese-speaking populations.

title: Association between operative time and short-term postoperative complications following minimally invasive surgery for stage III–IV endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Advanced endometriosis (stages III–IV) affects approximately 10% of reproductive-age women and involves ectopic endometrial tissue with significant pelvic distortion, causing widespread symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, pelvic pain, gastrointestinal and urinary complaints, and infertility. Minimally invasive surgical management of stage III–IV endometriosis frequently requires complex multi-organ procedures such as ureterolysis, bowel resection, appendectomy, and salpingectomy, which are associated with prolonged operative times and increased intraoperative complications. Although longer operative times correlate with higher overnight hospital admission rates (with each additional hour increasing admission likelihood by 64%) and greater estimated blood loss, unexpectedly, operative time was not independently associated with increased short-term postoperative complications in this minimally invasive surgical population. Longer operative times were associated with higher rates of postoperative anxiety and depression, suggesting psychological morbidity related to recovery burden. The use of robotic-assisted approaches was more common in longer procedures, and patients with the longest operative times (>280 minutes) experienced postoperative hospital stays nearly six times longer than those below the 50th percentile, indicating prolonged operative duration meaningfully impacts hospital resource utilization despite comparable complication rates.

title: Case Report: A Challenging Superior Hypogastric Plexus Block in a Patient with Enlarged and Bifid Transverse Processes
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition that causes multiple pain syndromes including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dysuria, and dyschezia through mechanisms involving inflammation and nerve sensitization. Chronic pelvic pain from endometriosis frequently coexists with comorbid conditions such as irritable bowel syndrome, depression, anxiety, fibromyalgia, and interstitial cystitis, complicating management. The superior hypogastric plexus, a retroperitoneal sympathetic nerve network anterior to the L5-S1 region that innervates pelvic viscera including the uterus, ovaries, and bladder, represents a key anatomical target for pain transmission interruption. This case demonstrates that superior hypogastric plexus block (SHPB) with bupivacaine and dexamethasone successfully treated refractory endometriosis-related pain in a patient who had failed conventional multimodal therapy including tramadol, pregabalin, amitriptyline, pelvic floor physiotherapy, and cognitive behavioral therapy. The patient experienced substantial pain relief following the procedure, with visual analog scores decreasing from 8 to 4 within the initial postoperative period. These findings support SHPB as an effective interventional option for managing severe, treatment-resistant endometriosis-related chronic pelvic pain.

title: Systemic Immune and Tumor Marker Profiles in Ovarian and Deep Infiltrating Endometriosis: Associations with Disease Severity and Symptom Burden
summary:
# Summary: Systemic Immune and Tumor Marker Profiles in Ovarian and Deep Infiltrating Endometriosis

Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease, presents with heterogeneous clinical manifestations including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, dysuria, and infertility, with deep infiltrating endometriosis additionally causing extensive fibrosis and anatomical distortion. The study identified distinct immune dysregulation patterns characterized by increased activated macrophages, altered Th1/Th2 ratios, reduced natural killer cell cytotoxicity, and elevated pro-inflammatory mediators including IL-8 and Galectin-1, particularly in deep infiltrating disease, alongside decreased monocyte/macrophage activation markers (sCD163). Patients with ovarian endometriomas showed elevated IL-8 and CA19-9 with increased monocyte counts and reduced neutrophil proportions, while deep infiltrating endometriosis displayed elevated IL-8, Galectin-1, and CEA levels. Key biomarkers correlated with disease severity: elevated neutrophil proportions and TNF-α associated with greater disease burden and poorer physical health perception, while IL-33 showed inverse correlations with disease severity scores. The systemic immune and tumor marker profiles demonstrate that distinct endometriosis phenotypes involve specific inflammatory and myeloid cell alterations that correlate with disease severity and individual symptom burden, providing potential diagnostic and prognostic markers for clinical stratification.

title: Relation of Endometrioma and Deep Infiltrating Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary: Endometrioma and Deep Infiltrating Endometriosis

Endometriosis affects approximately 10-50% of infertile women and commonly presents with infertility and pelvic pain through multiple mechanisms that compromise reproductive function. Both endometrioma (OMA) and deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) significantly reduce ovarian reserve by inducing pro-inflammatory cytokine release, oxidative stress, vascular dysfunction, and follicular loss; notably, the combined OMA+DIE phenotype produces the greatest reduction in anti-Müllerian hormone levels (62.2% decrease post-surgery compared to 49.8% for OMA alone). Endometriosis lesions disrupt ovarian homeostasis through increased reactive oxygen species, proliferation, and fibrosis that accelerate follicular activation and impair blood supply, leading to premature ovarian aging. While surgical intervention for DIE improves spontaneous pregnancy rates and IVF outcomes, it paradoxically further reduces ovarian reserve across all disease phenotypes, indicating a trade-off between symptom relief and fertility preservation. The study demonstrates that DIE is as effective as OMA in reducing ovarian reserve both before and after surgery, and that larger endometrioma cyst size correlates with greater post-operative decline in ovarian reserve markers.

title: The Translation and Cross-Cultural Adaptation of The Persian
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, inflammatory, oestrogen-dependent disease affecting women of reproductive age that carries a substantial clinical burden, with 30-50% of patients experiencing infertility and a high prevalence of painful symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and pelvic pain. The condition is associated with significant reductions in quality of life and elevated comorbidity burden requiring substantial medical resources. This study validated the Persian version of the WERF-EPHect questionnaire as a reliable diagnostic tool for endometriosis assessment alongside traditional imaging methods such as diagnostic sonography and laparoscopy. Accurate clinical identification of endometriosis requires systematic evaluation of pain severity, menstrual status, and symptom history in women aged 18-45 years. While hormonal medications and surgical interventions including oophorectomy provide treatment options, in vitro fertilization represents a therapeutic approach for managing endometriosis-associated infertility. The development of culturally adapted, validated assessment instruments like the Persian questionnaire enhances the ability to diagnose and characterize endometriosis phenotypes across diverse populations.

title: Urogynecologic Symptoms Are Not Specific to Anatomic Region of Digestive Symptoms
summary: Women presenting with digestive symptoms frequently experience concurrent urinary and gynecologic symptoms, indicating that urogynecologic manifestations are not anatomically localized but rather reflect overlapping systemic pathways. Among patients with digestive symptoms, dysmenorrhea (61%) and menorrhagia (58.6%) are highly prevalent, while urinary complaints including dysuria, difficulty voiding, and recurrent urinary tract infections affect approximately 18-33% of this population. Endometriosis shows particularly strong bidirectional associations with irritable bowel syndrome, with over 50% of surgically confirmed endometriosis patients meeting IBS criteria and 36% of IBS patients having endometriosis. These overlapping symptoms likely arise from shared neuroanatomic pathways, specifically the dorsal root ganglion connections linking sensory afferent nerves of the bowel, bladder, and reproductive tract, combined with mechanisms including chronic inflammation, mast cell activation, altered pain sensitivity, and cross-sensitization. Endometriotic tissue itself may activate bowel sensory neural pathways, lowering pain thresholds in the gastrointestinal tract, while estrogen-dependent mechanisms may further explain the overlap between IBS and endometriosis manifestations. This constellation of findings emphasizes that integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approaches addressing the gut-brain-reproductive axis are necessary for comprehensive management of these interconnected functional somatic syndromes.

title: Insights from focus groups with trans and gender-diverse people with endometriosis: stories you tell, stories you don’t
summary: This qualitative study examines endometriosis and its intersection with gender identity in transgender and gender-diverse individuals. Endometriosis causes multiple painful symptoms including cyclic and chronic pelvic pain, subfertility, dyschezia, dysuria, dyspareunia, and painful orgasm, with significant downstream effects on employment and family planning. For transgender and gender-diverse people, menstruation and the cyclic pain associated with endometriosis exacerbate gender dysphoria and create disconnection from the body, while exogenous testosterone improves both psychological status and gender-affirming outcomes. Notably, gender-affirming care involving testosterone may interfere with endometriosis treatment, creating a clinical dilemma for this population. Healthcare discrimination leads many trans and gender-diverse individuals to withhold critical medical information, further complicating diagnosis and management. The study highlights that this population faces unique intersecting health needs requiring integrated approaches to simultaneously address endometriosis symptoms and gender-affirming care.

title: FIGO Fertility Passport: A tool for patients and their healthcare providers
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: The FIGO Fertility Passport is a comprehensive clinical tool designed to streamline fertility assessment and prevent diagnostic delays by documenting detailed reproductive and medical histories. The document emphasizes systematic evaluation of menstrual patterns to identify conditions such as polycystic ovary syndrome (associated with oligomenorrhea, hirsutism, and acne) and endometriosis (presenting with dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, and dyspareunia), as well as primary ovarian insufficiency (characterized by oligomenorrhea, hot flushes, and vaginal dryness). Key diagnostic markers include anti-Müllerian hormone for ovarian reserve assessment, luteal phase progesterone for ovulation confirmation, and prolactin measurement to identify anovulation amenable to cabergoline treatment. The passport also captures factors affecting fertility outcomes, including body mass index, lifestyle factors (smoking, alcohol, and drug use that reduce sperm parameters), prior surgeries (cesarean delivery, appendectomy), and chemotherapy history, which collectively reduce ovarian reserve and may lead to complications such as cesarean scar niches, pelvic adhesions, and tubal disease. For male subfertility, the tool prompts evaluation of testicular pathology and genetic testing (Y-chromosome microdeletion, cystic fibrosis genes) in azoospermic patients. By consolidating this information in a single portable document, the Fertility Passport reduces unnecessary test repetition and facilitates coordinated care between patients and healthcare providers.

title: Developing the FIGO‐IPPS “R U MOVVING SOMe” classification system for female chronic pelvic pain
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: The FIGO-IPPS "R U MOVVING SOMe" classification system addresses chronic pelvic pain (CPP) as a multifactorial condition lasting longer than 3-6 months with profound impacts on quality of life, disability, and psychological well-being. CPP is highly associated with endometriosis (23.4% of cases), adenomyosis, dysmenorrhea, and multiple non-gynecologic conditions including gastrointestinal, urinary, neurologic, and musculoskeletal disorders. The underlying pathophysiological mechanisms involve inflammation, tissue distortion, hormonal influences, organ dysfunction, and nociplastic pain sensitization, with musculoskeletal involvement characterized by altered biomechanics, trigger points causing nerve compression, and pelvic floor dysfunction. CPP manifestations are diverse and include cyclical or acyclical presentations with associated urinary frequency and urgency, bowel dysfunction, sexual dysfunction, allodynia, and hyperalgesia, alongside systemic consequences including cognitive impairment, economic burden, and interference with social relationships. The classification system recognizes that anxiety may exacerbate pain, particularly in conditions like endometriosis, and acknowledges the overlap with other pain syndromes such as interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome where sensitization is a primary mechanism. This comprehensive approach emphasizes that effective CPP management requires evaluation across multiple organ systems and recognition of the diverse etiologies and mechanisms contributing to this debilitating condition.

title: ESUR consensus MRI for endometriosis: protocol, lexicon, and compartment-based analysis
summary: This ESUR consensus article establishes standardized MRI protocols and terminology for diagnosing endometriosis across multiple anatomical compartments. Endometriosis presents clinically as chronic pelvic pain and infertility in approximately 50% of affected women of reproductive age, with three main phenotypes: superficial endometriosis appearing as peritoneal implants, adnexal endometriosis involving ovarian and tubal locations, and deep pelvic endometriosis characterized by subperitoneal invasion with associated fibrosis and disrupted normal anatomy. MRI diagnostic criteria are compartment-specific, including characteristic signal patterns such as low T2-weighted and high T1-weighted signal intensities in endometriomas, junctional zone thickening and myometrial cysts in adenomyosis, and fibrotic infiltration in ligamentous and bowel involvement. The article documents endometriosis can extend beyond the pelvis to the abdominal wall, sigmoid colon, appendix, and diaphragm, requiring tailored imaging protocols for each location. This consensus framework provides clinicians with a comprehensive lexicon and standardized approach to improve diagnostic accuracy and consistency in MRI evaluation of endometriosis across its diverse presentations.

title: The Diagnostic Performance of Transvaginal Ultrasound for Posterior Compartment Endometriosis Compared to Laparoscopic and Histopathological Findings: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Deep infiltrating endometriosis predominantly affects the posterior pelvic compartment, including the pouch of Douglas, uterosacral ligaments, and rectosigmoid colon, and contributes to severe chronic pelvic pain and infertility in affected women. Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) has emerged as a reliable, cost-effective first-line imaging modality for evaluating posterior compartment endometriosis, demonstrating high diagnostic accuracy with mean sensitivities and specificities of 79–83% and 90% across different anatomical sites. Advanced TVUS techniques, including dynamic assessments (ovarian mobility, site-specific tenderness, uterine sliding sign), gel sonovaginography, rectal water contrast, and fluid instillation into the pouch of Douglas, significantly enhance detection of deep infiltrating lesions and improve visualization of key anatomical features such as muscularis invasion and uterosacral ligament involvement. The identification of hypoechoic, poorly vascularized tissue on TVUS reliably indicates deep endometriosis, with excellent concordance demonstrated between ultrasound and histopathological findings for lesion characterization. The application of standardized protocols such as the International Deep Endometriosis Analysis consensus and morphological classification systems further improves reproducibility and diagnostic performance of TVUS.

title: Impact of Minimally Invasive Surgery on Quality of Life and Infertility in Deep Infiltrating Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease affecting approximately 190 million women worldwide that causes substantial morbidity through pelvic pain, infertility, and psychological distress including anxiety and depression. The pathophysiology involves retrograde menstruation combined with immune system imbalance and chronic inflammation, leading to ectopic endometrial tissue with elevated expression of estrogen receptors, progesterone receptors, and proliferation markers (Bcl-2, Ki-67) that create a pathogenic peritoneal environment inhibiting ovulation and fertilization. DIE-related peritoneal inflammation drives ovarian dysfunction and decreased ovarian reserve, while the disease also increases obstetric complications including preeclampsia, placenta previa, and preterm birth. Minimally invasive surgery (MIS), including laparoscopic and robotic-assisted approaches, significantly improves quality of life and reproductive outcomes compared to open surgery, with laparoscopic excision of DIE achieving spontaneous pregnancy rates of 45–60% within one year while providing benefits of reduced postoperative pain, faster recovery, and lower complications. Medical therapy using estrogen-suppressing hormonal agents effectively reduces pain but has limited impact on fertility, positioning surgical excision as the preferred approach for symptomatic DIE lesions infiltrating visceral organs.

title: Selective Appendectomy in Patients Undergoing Minimally Invasive Surgery for Endometriosis: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic systemic inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that causes dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility through mechanisms involving elevated estrogen, impaired progesterone signaling, and subsequent inflammation and fibrosis. This retrospective study of 236 patients undergoing minimally invasive surgery for endometriosis found abnormal appendiceal pathology in 216 patients (91%), including appendiceal endometriosis in 34 (14.4%), adhesions in 140 (59.3%), and fibrous obliteration in 82 (34.8%), with appendiceal endometriosis significantly associated with advanced-stage (III–IV) disease and extensive pelvic involvement. Although appendiceal endometriosis often mimics chronic appendicitis through localized inflammation, adhesions, and fibrosis, selective appendectomy may contribute to pain symptom improvement by removing non-endometriotic pathologies and preventing missed diagnoses. The findings suggest that appendiceal involvement is more prevalent in advanced endometriosis with bowel, uterosacral ligament, or peritoneal engagement, and that comprehensive surgical evaluation of the appendix during endometriosis treatment may be clinically warranted in high-stage disease.

title: Risk factors for postoperative recurrence of deep infiltrating endometriosis during a 6- to 12-year follow-up
summary: This longitudinal study identified critical risk and protective factors for postoperative recurrence of deep infiltrating endometriosis over 6-12 years of follow-up. Left-sided ovarian endometriomas emerged as the strongest predictor of recurrence, conferring a 6.4-fold increased risk and achieving a 47.4% cumulative recurrence rate compared to 4.8-13.6% in other locations, likely due to anatomical factors including dense adhesions and sigmoid colon involvement that complicate surgical dissection. Younger age at surgery (<34 years) was associated with a 5-fold higher recurrence rate, reflecting the hormone-dependent nature of endometriosis and elevated estrogen levels in younger women that promote more aggressive disease. Extended 6-month GnRH agonist therapy substantially reduced recurrence to 8.23% versus 27-30.6% with shorter treatment or no postoperative therapy, demonstrating the benefit of prolonged hypoestrogenic suppression alongside surgical excision. Additionally, postoperative pregnancy and advancing age at surgery emerged as independent protective factors, with parity and gravidity also associated with lower recurrence risk. These findings emphasize that long-term surveillance and individualized postoperative management strategies, particularly extended hormonal suppression and discussion of extended surgery for left-sided disease, are essential to minimize recurrence in this chronically relapsing condition.

title: Endometriosis Support and Development of Digital Technology–Based Interventions: Systematic Review
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition associated with multiple debilitating symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dysuria, dyschezia, and dyspareunia, while also causing significant psychological effects such as anxiety, depression, and reduced self-esteem. Digital health interventions have emerged as promising therapeutic tools for managing both the physical and psychological burden of endometriosis, with various platforms demonstrating efficacy in symptom reduction. Specific digital interventions including website-based resources targeting dyspareunia, virtual reality applications for pain management through guided physical activity, the Endocare immersive program utilizing binaural auditory stimulation, and mobile applications like Endo-APP designed for symptom monitoring all show clinically meaningful reductions in pain and other endometriosis-related symptoms. Mobile applications and SMS-based interventions such as EndoSMS further contribute to improved quality of life by enabling patients to actively participate in their disease management. These findings indicate that digital health technologies offer accessible, scalable solutions for symptom management and chronic disease control in endometriosis, particularly important given the expanded role of digital healthcare following the COVID-19 pandemic.

title: Phenotypic heterogeneity in adenomyosis: internal and external subtypes
summary:
# Article Summary

Adenomyosis manifests as two distinct phenotypic subtypes—intrinsic and extrinsic—with different clinical, reproductive, and pathological characteristics. Intrinsic adenomyosis is associated with reproductive history factors including higher gravidity, parity, and abortion rates, as well as endometrial procedures like curettage, and presents with heavier menstrual bleeding, higher uterine volumes, diffuse myometrial lesions, and anemia; this form aligns with the "inside-out" theory of direct endometrial invagination. In contrast, extrinsic adenomyosis frequently coexists with ovarian endometriomas and deep infiltrating endometriosis, presenting as focal posterior wall lesions, and is associated with earlier menarche, severe dysmenorrhea, infertility (especially primary infertility), and greater surgical complexity with longer operative times and increased blood loss. Deep infiltrating endometriosis lesions in the extrinsic form most commonly involve the uterosacral ligaments, followed by the rectovaginal septum, bladder, and bowel, and extrinsic adenomyosis aligns with the "outside-in" theory originating from invasion of pelvic endometriosis through the myometrium. These phenotypic distinctions have important implications for diagnosis and treatment strategies, as the overlapping symptomatology between the two subtypes complicates clinical management despite their distinct etiological pathways.

title: Neuropathic-like pain affects pain perception in patients with deep endometriosis: an observational study
summary: This observational study identifies neuropathic-like pain as a significant pain phenotype in endometriosis patients, present in 36% of the cohort and associated with substantially higher pain intensity, emotional distress, catastrophizing, and reduced quality of life compared to patients with purely nociceptive pain. Deep endometriosis and nerve fiber involvement, driven by inflammatory mediators such as TNF-α and BDNF, contribute to neuropathic-like pain through central sensitization mechanisms that can persist independently of lesion extent or endometriosis stage. Notably, neuropathic-like pain shows no correlation with endometriosis stage, surgical complexity, or psychiatric comorbidities, suggesting it represents a distinct neurobiological pain phenotype rather than a function of disease severity or psychological factors. The findings indicate that persistent nociceptive input from endometriotic lesions and their irritation of nerve fibers promotes central nervous system plasticity, maintaining chronic pain even after lesion removal. The authors recommend integrating systematic neuropathic pain screening using the DN4 questionnaire into routine clinical practice and adopting multidisciplinary management approaches incorporating neuromodulators and psychological support, with complete lesion resection potentially reducing neuropathic-like pain symptoms.

title: Paradigm shift and long-term results in the diagnosis and treatment of pelvic venous disorder
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery: Venous and Lymphatic Disorders
summary: Pelvic venous disorder is a heterogeneous condition causing chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, hematuria, flank pain, and varicose veins through multiple mechanisms including iliac vein compression, gonadal and ovarian vein reflux, and internal iliac vein reflux. Interventional treatments including iliac vein stenting, renal vein stenting, ovarian vein embolization, and spermatic vein embolization significantly reduce pain severity and improve quality of life, with visual analog scale scores decreasing from 8.1-8.7 to 1.8-3.1 and Short Form-36 scores improving from 33.8-48.45 to 74.7-79.4. Diagnostic imaging including Doppler ultrasound, computed tomography venography, and transvaginal ultrasound identify anatomical abnormalities such as iliac vein obstruction and dilated pelvic veins, which are associated with reduced health status and are more prevalent in premenopausal women. Reintervention rates remain low, ranging from 10.2% for iliac vein stenting, though complications including asymptomatic gonadal vein bleeding occasionally occur. The findings establish pelvic venous disorder as a significant but previously underrecognized contributor to chronic pelvic pain that benefits from targeted interventional approaches based on precise anatomical characterization.

title: Analysis of Risk Factors Associated With Persistent Noncyclic Pelvic Pain Despite Hormonal Treatment in Patients With Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent disease causing pelvic pain and infertility in 35-50% of affected women, with first-line hormonal treatments including progestins and combined oral contraceptives, and second-line therapy with gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists. This study identified multiple risk factors for persistent noncyclic pelvic pain despite hormonal treatment, including ovarian endometrioma lesions (4-fold increased risk), baseline high noncyclic pain or dyspareunia scores, previous cesarean section (9.5-fold increased risk), and advanced rASRM stage disease. Notably, deep infiltrating endometriosis was associated with reduced pain risk, likely because hormonal treatments effectively block estrogen action in these lesions, whereas ovarian endometriomas maintain higher local estradiol and progesterone concentrations that escape hormone suppression. The study emphasizes that persistent pain despite hormonal therapy may involve neuropathic and nociplastic pain mechanisms beyond nociceptive pain, requiring pain phenotyping and potentially multidisciplinary approaches combining medical, surgical, and physiotherapy interventions for personalized management.

title: Machine-learning-derived prediction models of recurrence of ovarian endometriosis after laparoscopic surgery
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian endometriosis causes significant clinical burden, presenting with dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility, and is a polygenic disease with strong genetic and hormonal components. Laparoscopic conservative surgery effectively treats ovarian endometriosis and improves fertility and symptom relief, yet suffers from high postoperative recurrence rates driven by incomplete lesion removal and residual tissue growth under hormonal influence. This study employed machine-learning models—particularly Gaussian Process achieving an AUROC of 0.90—to predict postoperative recurrence, identifying multiple significant risk factors including larger cyst diameter, advanced r-AFS stage, deep infiltrating endometriosis, elevated preoperative CA125 levels, bilateral ovarian involvement, peritoneal endometriosis, and elevated thrombin time. Younger age, lack of preoperative pregnancy, positive family history, and postoperative medication duration were also associated with increased recurrence risk, while postoperative live birth and extended breastfeeding appeared protective through suppression of menstrual reflux and high progesterone levels. These machine-learning-derived predictive models provide clinically valuable tools for identifying high-risk patients likely to experience endometriosis recurrence after surgery, potentially guiding personalized treatment strategies.

title: A review of the MRI features of endometriosis: what should be paid attention to during the reporting process?
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic gynecological disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that causes significant clinical burdens, including chronic pelvic pain affecting approximately 90% of affected women and infertility found in 20-50% of infertile women. The disease develops through mechanisms such as Sampson's metastatic theory (retrograde menstruation of endometrial cells) and Meyer's coelomic metaplasia (mesothelial differentiation), with ectopic tissue undergoing cyclical proliferative activity that leads to recurrent bleeding, fibrosis, and hemosiderin deposition. Endometriosis manifests in three primary forms—superficial peritoneal endometriosis (invasion <5mm), deep infiltrating endometriosis (invasion >5mm), and ovarian endometriomas (chocolate cysts)—each requiring distinct MRI imaging sequences for accurate diagnosis and preoperative assessment. Specific MRI protocols have been established: T1 weighted fat-saturated images for superficial disease, T2 weighted images for deep infiltrating lesions, and both sequences for endometriomas, with key diagnostic findings including T1 high-signal multiplicity, T2 shading, and the T2 dark spot sign. Deep infiltrating endometriosis causes significant pathological changes including ligament thickening, bladder fibrous infiltration, parametrial involvement, and can be classified using the rASRM and ENZIAN systems to guide surgical planning. Additionally, endometriotic cysts carry a small risk of malignant transformation to endometriosis-associated ovarian carcinoma, with enhanced mural nodules, lesion enlargement, and absent T2 shading serving as important indicators of malignancy requiring careful radiological surveillance.

title: Endometriosis During Adolescence: A Narrative Review
summary: Endometriosis during adolescence is characterized by endometrial glands and stroma present in the peritoneal cavity and occasionally in extra-pelvic sites, with presentations that differ markedly from adult disease. Adolescents typically present with severe dysmenorrhea, non-cyclical pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and systemic symptoms including nausea, headache, and bowel symptoms, though disease severity does not correlate with pain intensity but rather with interactions between lesions and sensory nerve fibers. The pathophysiology involves genetic predisposition (accounting for ~50% of risk), hormonal and environmental factors, with protective factors including oral contraceptives, regular exercise, omega-3 supplementation, and delayed menarche. Adolescents with endometriosis characteristically present with ASRM Stage I disease and metabolically active red lesions exhibiting high vascularization, which may indicate a more aggressive phenotype than in older women. Diagnostic approaches are expanding beyond laparoscopy to include non-invasive imaging modalities and novel biomarkers such as saliva-based microRNA signatures, while first-line medical management utilizes combined oral contraceptive pills or progestins to suppress ovulation and reduce pain. Early diagnosis and intervention are critical, as delayed diagnosis risks central sensitization, scarring, chronic pelvic pain, and reduced fertility.

title: Is the EMpressin Injection in ENDOmetrioma eXcision Surgery Useful? The EMENDOX Study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis affects 17–44% of women with ovarian endometriomas, causing chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and increased malignancy risk through mechanisms including retrograde menstruation and metaplasia of ovarian surface epithelium. The EMENDOX study demonstrates that Empressin (vasopressin) injection during laparoscopic cystectomy significantly reduces endometrioma recurrence to 5.5% compared to 19.6% in control groups by inducing local vasoconstriction, creating a clearer dissection plane, and minimizing thermal injury to ovarian follicles and remaining healthy tissue. This technique preserves postoperative ovarian reserve while limiting the need for coagulation, proving particularly effective in bilateral cases (4% recurrence versus 24.3% in controls) and showing synergistic benefits when combined with postoperative hormonal therapy such as dienogest, which further suppresses residual microscopic disease. Excisional cystectomy with Empressin injection and postoperative hormonal therapy represents a superior treatment approach compared to ablative techniques, achieving lower recurrence rates while maintaining fertility potential and avoiding the adverse effects associated with conventional thermal surgical methods.

title: Psychosocial Factors and Disease Localization as Independent Predictors of Sexual Dysfunction in Women with Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition affecting up to 10% of reproductive-age individuals, is strongly associated with sexual dysfunction through multiple independent pathways beyond pain severity alone. Deep infiltrating endometriosis lesions, particularly those in Enzian compartment A (vagina/rectovaginal space) and compartment B (sacrouterine ligaments, cardinal ligaments, and pelvic sidewall), emerge as independent predictors of sexual dysfunction, with the Enzian classification system demonstrating superior correlation with clinical outcomes compared to the traditional rASRM staging system. Counterintuitively, patients with sexual dysfunction reported lower pain scores, suggesting that psychosocial factors—including anxiety, depression, and relationship dynamics—play significant roles in sexual well-being alongside disease localization. Complex pain mechanisms involving central sensitization, pelvic floor dysfunction, and neurogenic inflammation contribute to persistent pain manifestations including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and deep dyspareunia. Sexual dysfunction significantly impairs quality of life and is associated with lower self-image scores, particularly in surgically treated patients, underscoring the need for comprehensive, multidisciplinary treatment approaches combining medical therapy (progestins, GnRH analogs, combined oral contraceptives) and surgical excision of deep infiltrating disease. Early intervention and continuous management addressing both anatomical disease and psychosocial factors represent promising strategies to improve sexual function and overall outcomes in women with endometriosis.

title: The Retroverted Uterus and Pelvic Floor Dysfunction: 400 BC to 2025 AD
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive historical review establishes that uterine retroversion, occurring in approximately 16–18% of women, is significantly associated with multiple pelvic floor dysfunctions and gynecological symptoms. The retroverted uterus can develop secondarily due to endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, or pelvic tumors, and during pregnancy, gravid retroversion may cause uterine incarceration leading to urinary retention and bowel obstruction. Key clinical associations include urinary retention, obstructed defecation, constipation, dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, and pelvic pain, with particularly strong links to uterine prolapse (4.5-fold increased risk), cystocoele, and vaginal vault descent. Cyclical exacerbation of defecatory symptoms occurs premenstrually when uterine congestion increases. Management options range from conservative approaches including pessaries and physical therapy to surgical interventions such as laparoscopic ventrosuspension for non-prolapse cases or pelvic floor repair when prolapse is concurrent. Notably, retroversion shows no significant association with certain urodynamic findings like stress incontinence, detrusor overactivity, or recurrent urinary tract infections.

title: Prevalence of gynecological disorders in women with symptomatic pelvic venous disorders
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery: Venous and Lymphatic Disorders
summary:
# Summary

This study examined gynecological disorders in women with symptomatic pelvic venous insufficiency (PVI), revealing that endometriosis is a prominent finding associated with both pelvic pain and vascular pathology. Endometriosis causes pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia in over 71% of affected patients, and angiogenic activities within endometrial implants combined with hormonal variation appear to stimulate ovarian vein dilatation, creating a potential link between endometriosis and venous insufficiency. Among women with PVI and confirmed organic pathology, endometriosis was the most common diagnosis (45%), followed by pelvic inflammatory disease and other uterine pathologies, indicating substantial overlap between gynecological and vascular etiologies of chronic pelvic pain. Venous stenting proved highly effective for symptom relief across all pain phenotypes, reducing pelvic pain visual analog scores from 7.61 to 1.93 and demonstrating particular benefit in endometriosis patients (scores reduced from 6.52 to 2.5). The findings underscore the importance of comprehensive diagnostic evaluation combining transvaginal ultrasound, venography, and gynecological assessment to distinguish between competing causes of chronic pelvic pain in women.

title: Colo‐Ovarian Fistula With a Background of Endometriosis: A Report of Three Cases
journal: The Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology Research
summary: Endometriosis affects approximately one in ten women and causes chronic pelvic pain and infertility through retrograde menstruation of endometrial tissue into the pelvis. A serious but underrecognized complication is colo-ovarian fistula formation, which develops when ovarian endometriomas or sigmoid colon endometriosis create abnormal connections between the colon and ovary, often exacerbated by colonic diverticula and dense pelvic adhesions. Endometriosis-related pelvic inflammatory disease increases the risk of fistula development and manifests as tubo-ovarian abscesses, complicated by immunological abnormalities that perpetuate inflammation. Imaging diagnosis via MRI is challenging, but helpful diagnostic clues include free air within endometriomas and contiguous luminal structures connecting the colon to the ovary. Treatment approaches vary from conservative management with hormonal therapies such as GnRH antagonists and dienogest to surgical intervention, with conservative approaches proving effective in selected cases while avoiding surgical risks in patients with dense adhesions. This case series demonstrates that colo-ovarian fistulas represent a rare but significant endometriosis complication requiring increased clinical awareness for proper diagnosis and individualized management.

title: Laparoscopic Identification of the Inferior Hypogastric Nerve for the Gynecologist
summary: This article emphasizes the critical importance of identifying and preserving the inferior hypogastric nerve (IHN) during laparoscopic gynecological surgery, particularly in endometriosis cases. The IHN, which derives from the superior hypogastric plexus and runs parallel to the ureter in the meso-ureter, is essential for vaginal and cervical sensation, and its injury during surgery can result in significant morbidity including diminished vaginal lubrication, urinary retention, and anorectal dysfunction. Deep endometriosis carries particular risk for IHN infiltration and injury, making careful surgical visualization essential. The authors present a case of a 40-year-old woman with chronic pelvic pain, an ovarian hemorrhagic cyst, and superficial endometriosis who underwent laparoscopic excision of the Douglas pouch peritoneum while preserving the IHN. Surgical excision of the peritoneum treating the superficial endometriosis resulted in complete resolution of her chronic pelvic pain at three-month follow-up with negative imaging. The article underscores that careful laparoscopic identification and preservation of the IHN during endometriosis surgery is achievable and essential for preventing postoperative neurological complications while effectively treating the underlying pathology.

title: Global prevalence of adenomyosis and endometriosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis and endometriosis are closely related chronic disorders characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue located either within the uterine myometrium (adenomyosis) or at extrauterine sites including the peritoneum and ovaries (endometriosis). Both conditions are significantly associated with common clinical manifestations including dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility, with meta-analysis revealing that adenomyosis affects 31% and endometriosis affects 38% of women with infertility. Global prevalence data demonstrate that adenomyosis and endometriosis occur in approximately 1% and 5% of the general population respectively, though prevalence increases substantially in symptomatic populations, reaching 41-49% and 18-42% respectively among women with gynecological symptoms. Diagnostic approaches differ between the two conditions, with adenomyosis best identified through histopathology and MRI, while endometriosis shows highest detection rates via laparoscopy. Notably, diffuse adenomyosis is associated with a threefold increase in miscarriage risk, and adenomyosis is present in 38.2% of women with recurrent miscarriage and 34.7% with failed assisted reproductive technology, underscoring the reproductive consequences of these disorders.

title: Urogynecological Outcomes After Minimally Invasive Surgery for Deep Endometriosis: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Deep endometriosis, which accounts for 15–30% of all endometriosis cases, frequently involves the uterosacral ligaments, rectovaginal septum, bowel, and urinary tract, causing lower urinary tract symptoms including urgency, frequency, dysuria, and suprapubic pain through direct organ involvement and neuroinflammatory mechanisms. Ureteral endometriosis poses a serious risk of silent obstruction and hydronephrosis, while bladder endometriosis affects up to 85% of cases with urinary tract involvement. Minimally invasive laparoscopic surgery with nerve-sparing techniques is the standard treatment approach, with several studies demonstrating significant postoperative improvements in lower urinary tract symptoms, bladder capacity, and uroflowmetry, particularly in patients with severe baseline symptoms. However, functional deterioration remains a clinically relevant risk, with some studies documenting worsening urinary function, voiding difficulties, and incontinence scores, especially among women with minimal preoperative dysfunction. The variable outcomes highlight the importance of patient selection and surgical technique, as nerve-sparing approaches reduce postoperative complications compared to conventional resection methods.

title: Early Contralateral Recurrence of Ovarian Endometriosis After Unilateral Surgery: A Case Report
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent gynecological condition characterized histologically by the presence of endometrial glands and stroma outside the uterus, affecting 10-20% of women of reproductive age and up to 50% of infertile women. Ovarian endometriomas (OMAs), the cystic form of ovarian endometriosis, are strongly associated with advanced disease stage and cause multiple debilitating symptoms including lower abdominal pain, dysmenorrhea, menstrual irregularities, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility. This case report documents a notable clinical pattern: a 26-year-old patient experienced early contralateral recurrence of ovarian endometriomas on the right ovary within the first year following laparoscopic excision of left-sided disease, with left ovarian involvement identified as a significant risk factor for recurrence. Multiple factors predict endometrioma recurrence, including advanced disease stage at initial treatment, prior endometriosis surgery, increased serum CA125 levels, larger cyst size, and pathological features such as endometrial tissue infiltration depth within the cyst wall. The management of recurrent ovarian endometriosis requires comprehensive surgical excision combined with postoperative hormonal suppression or dietary therapy over at least six months to reduce recurrence rates and symptom burden. These findings emphasize the aggressive nature of advanced-stage ovarian endometriosis and the necessity for integrated surgical and medical approaches to prevent early recurrence and preserve fertility.

title: Uterine “twisting sign”: A new potential ultrasonographic soft marker for deep endometriosis
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies the "twisting sign"—a ultrasonographic marker defined as uterine rotation of 15°-90° on fundal transverse section—as a novel soft marker for deep endometriosis (DE), particularly posterior compartment disease. The twisting sign was detected in 24.1% of participants and showed strong associations with rectosigmoid nodules (44.8%), uterosacral ligament involvement (41.4%), and ovarian fixation to the uterine wall (37.9%), with multivariate analysis confirming it as an independent predictor of rectosigmoid junction nodules. The underlying mechanism involves mechanical forces from posterior DE nodules, adhesions, and fibrotic reactions causing uterine axis rotation and displacement; notably, the twisting sign correlated with the absence of the posterior sliding sign and higher prevalence of diffuse adenomyosis with moderate-to-severe extension. Transvaginal sonography-detected uterine rotation significantly correlated with the total number of posterior DE nodules, and intraoperative findings confirmed the imaging findings in surgically evaluated patients, supporting the twisting sign's utility as a complementary ultrasonographic marker for detecting posterior compartment deep endometriosis alongside established IDEA criteria markers.

title: Sonographic and Clinical Progression of Adenomyosis and Coexisting Endometriosis: Long-Term Insights and Management Perspectives
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis is a benign uterine disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial glands and stromal tissue invading the myometrium, causing significant clinical symptoms including dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain. The disease frequently coexists with endometriosis, including deep infiltrating forms with bowel involvement and endometriomas, complicating clinical presentations and affecting quality of life and reproductive potential. Diffuse adenomyosis exhibits more severe symptomatology than focal disease, with disease morphology and depth of myometrial involvement (inner versus outer) correlating with specific symptom patterns—dysmenorrhea and heavy bleeding predominating in diffuse disease with inner myometrial involvement, while dyspareunia associates with outer myometrial involvement. Long-term hormonal therapy with continuous progestins, particularly Dienogest 2 mg daily, effectively reduces symptoms and adenomyosis severity over 18 months, with focal adenomyosis showing greater regression than diffuse disease. Transvaginal ultrasonography and magnetic resonance imaging reliably detect adenomyosis using direct signs (myometrial cysts, hyperechoic islands, linear striations) and indirect signs (irregular junctional zone, myometrial thickness asymmetry), enabling non-invasive diagnosis in premenopausal and younger nulliparous patients. Untreated patients experience progressive disease worsening, emphasizing the clinical importance of early diagnosis and personalized hormonal management for symptom relief and disease stabilization.

title: Diagnostic performance of the #Enzian classification via ultrasound compared to laparoscopic findings in endometriosis: a retrospective cohort study
journal: Journal of the Turkish German Gynecological Association
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial-like tissue and presents clinically with severe dysmenorrhea, deep dyspareunia, persistent pelvic pain, gastrointestinal and urinary disturbances, fatigue, and infertility through disruption of the peritoneal environment and anatomical distortion. This retrospective cohort study evaluated transvaginal ultrasound using the updated Enzian classification system for detecting deep infiltrating, peritoneal, and ovarian endometriosis compared to laparoscopic findings, demonstrating that compartments FA and FB achieved exceptional diagnostic accuracy (98.82% and 98.59% respectively) with perfect sensitivity and minimal false positives, while the left ovarian compartment showed 92.87% accuracy. However, significant diagnostic limitations emerged, particularly in compartment A (vaginal/rectovaginal involvement) with markedly low sensitivity of 12.12% and in tubal assessments, which posed challenges with reduced specificity and risked underestimation of disease severity. The Enzian ultrasound classification provides a reliable diagnostic framework with variable performance across anatomical compartments, enabling enhanced communication between imaging specialists and gynecologic surgeons, supporting surgical planning and predicting laparoscopic operating time, though multimodal imaging approaches may be necessary to address persistent diagnostic gaps, particularly in tubal involvement.

title: Is Pelvic Floor Muscle Resting Activity Associated with Pelvic and Genital Pain, Dyspareunia, and Pelvic Floor Muscle Contraction? A Cross-Sectional Study of Women with Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Women with endometriosis commonly experience pelvic and genital pain and dyspareunia, conditions previously hypothesized to correlate with elevated pelvic floor muscle (PFM) resting activity. This cross-sectional study used surface electromyography to assess PFM resting activity in women with endometriosis and found no significant associations between resting activity and reports of pelvic pain, genital pain, or dyspareunia location and concerns. However, the study identified a positive relationship between PFM resting activity and muscle activation during maximal voluntary contractions, suggesting that increased baseline tone does not impair the ability to further contract these muscles. Notably, resting activity was negatively associated with age, indicating younger women with endometriosis exhibited higher PFM baseline tone. While previous research using digital palpation has linked increased PFM tone to pain in endometriosis, this electromyographic study challenges the direct association between resting muscle activity and pain symptoms, highlighting the complexity of pain mechanisms in endometriosis involving both visceral and referred pain components.

title: Diagnostic Challenge of Appendiceal Endometriosis: A Case Report
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-dependent condition affecting 6-10% of reproductive-age women, presents with pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, with gastrointestinal involvement occurring in up to 37% of cases. Appendiceal endometriosis is a rare manifestation found in 0.05-1.69% of endometriosis patients, but its prevalence increases to 35.8% in stage IV disease and is frequently associated with ovarian and bladder endometriosis. This condition clinically mimics acute appendicitis, presenting with right lower quadrant pain, elevated inflammatory markers such as C-reactive protein and leukocytosis, and potentially causing chronic pelvic pain, dyschezia, hematochezia, and intestinal obstruction. Definitive diagnosis requires histopathological examination revealing endometrial glands and stroma within the appendiceal wall, as standard imaging cannot reliably distinguish appendiceal endometriosis from acute appendicitis. Laparoscopic appendectomy provides both diagnostic confirmation and optimal management, often combined with dienogest therapy, while comprehensive histological sampling is essential given that many lesions are microscopic and may occur independently or alongside other pelvic implants. Multidisciplinary assessment is crucial for women with recurrent right lower quadrant pain to differentiate appendiceal endometriosis from acute surgical emergencies.

title: Dual ultrasound combination improves the accuracy of preoperative assessment in rectal endometriosis: a prospective cohort study
summary: This prospective study demonstrates that combining transvaginal ultrasound (TVS) with endorectal ultrasound (ERUS) significantly improves preoperative diagnostic accuracy for rectal endometriosis, a severe manifestation of estrogen-dependent endometriosis that commonly infiltrates the bowel. Rectal endometriosis predominantly affects the anterior rectal wall and causes debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea (87% of patients), dyschezia, painful defecation, rectal bleeding, and intestinal obstruction, often coexisting with adenomyosis (52.2%) and ovarian endometriomas (65.2%). ERUS proved superior for assessing the depth of rectal wall invasion, demonstrating 100% sensitivity for detecting mucosal and submucosal infiltration, while TVS effectively identified adjacent lesions in the uterosacral ligaments, rectovaginal space, and rectosigmoid junction. All patients underwent laparoscopic surgery with three approaches—shaving, disc excision, or segmental resection—with segmental resection reserved for larger nodules associated with luminal stenosis. Surgery produced significant clinical improvements in pain, emotional well-being, control, work capacity, and sexual function, with minimal complications. The study establishes dual ultrasound imaging as the optimal preoperative assessment strategy for tailoring surgical approach and improving outcomes in rectal endometriosis.

title: Urine and Serum miRNA Signatures for the Non-Invasive Diagnosis of Adenomyosis: A Machine Learning-Based Pilot Study
summary: This pilot study demonstrates that dysregulated microRNAs (miRNAs) can serve as non-invasive biomarkers for adenomyosis diagnosis when analyzed using machine learning approaches. Adenomyosis, a chronic gynecological condition causing dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, chronic pelvic pain, and reduced fertility, involves dysregulation of specific miRNAs including miR-10b, miR-30c-5p, miR-21, miR-145, and miR-183-3p, which contribute to pathological mechanisms such as inflammation, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and fibrosis through regulation of critical signaling pathways. The researchers applied machine learning models (Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machine, and Random Forest) to miRNA signatures detected in both serum and urine samples, finding that urine-based models achieved superior diagnostic accuracy compared to serum-based approaches, with certain models reaching perfect discrimination (AUC 1.00) between adenomyosis patients and negative controls. Notably, urine miRNA profiling outperformed serum analysis and demonstrated discriminatory power comparable to or exceeding traditional imaging modalities like transvaginal ultrasound and MRI. These findings suggest that liquid-biopsy miRNA profiling combined with machine learning represents a promising, minimally invasive complementary diagnostic tool that could improve diagnostic accuracy and reduce the diagnostic delay currently associated with adenomyosis.

title: Exome-Based Identification of Candidate Genes in Sporadic Adenomyosis Cases
summary: This exome-based study identified multiple candidate genes contributing to sporadic adenomyosis pathogenesis through genetic variants and functional mechanisms. Adenomyosis is strongly associated with menorrhagia, dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility, with significant overlap in clinical presentation and underlying pathophysiology with endometriosis. Key candidate genes include EFHB (involved in calcium regulation, cellular migration, proliferation, and myometrial contractions), MEIS1 (a cofactor for HOXA10 that regulates endometrial gene expression and whose suppression enhances proliferation), and HOXA10 (significantly downregulated in adenomyosis and endometriosis). Additional candidate genes implicated in adenomyosis include HGFAC (which activates HGF to promote epithelial–mesenchymal transition and glandular invagination), SULT2B1 (involved in sex hormone homeostasis and pain perception), CYP27A1 (affecting vitamin D metabolism), ERCC2 (involved in DNA repair), CTSK (involved in inflammation), and ADAM15. The study found that specific variants—p.(Val331Ile) in EFHB and p.(Phe14Val) in MEIS1—were frequently shared among adenomyosis cases and associated with severe pain, suggesting these genetic alterations may disrupt the balance between cellular proliferation and apoptosis, ultimately promoting endometrial invagination into the myometrium.

title: Establishing an electronic patient-reported outcome (ePRO) for patients with endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain: A pilot feasibility study
journal: Journal of Clinical and Translational Science
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by ectopic endometrial-like tissue found in multiple pelvic and extra-pelvic sites, including the ovaries, bowel, bladder, and lungs, which triggers inflammation, fibrosis, and persistent pain associated with chronic pelvic pain (CPP) and related conditions such as dysmenorrhea and infertility. The condition's complex pathophysiology involves hormonal, immunological, and genetic factors, with CPP often involving centralized pain mechanisms where the nervous system amplifies pain signals, frequently accompanied by comorbidities including bladder pain syndrome, irritable bowel syndrome, depression, and anxiety. This pilot feasibility study developed an electronic patient-reported outcome (ePRO) tool to remotely capture standardized data on pain, psychological health, and functional outcomes in endometriosis and CPP patients, demonstrating that participants with higher central sensitization and anxiety scores were more likely to complete the assessment. Patients exhibited significant psychological distress, with most meeting clinical thresholds for depression and anxiety on standardized scales (PHQ-9, GAD-7), alongside high pain catastrophizing, moderate-to-severe pain intensity, and limited readiness for self-management. The study identified that older age was associated with longer survey completion times and multiple sitting requirements, while lower socioeconomic status and rural residence were predictors of reduced internet access and e-health literacy. These findings support the feasibility of ePRO tools for multidisciplinary endometriosis management while highlighting the need to address disparities in digital access to ensure equitable implementation.

title: Fluorescence-Guided Bladder and Ureter Visualization in Benign Robotic Gynecologic Surgery: A Series of Five Cases
summary:
# Summary

This case series demonstrates the clinical utility of indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence imaging for ureteral identification during robotic-assisted benign gynecologic surgery, particularly in patients with endometriosis and anatomically complex pelvises. While intravesical ICG for bladder delineation proved unreliable—especially in patients with prior cesarean sections due to fibrosis-induced impaired dye diffusion—retrograde ureteric ICG instillation consistently provided clear, uniform fluorescence for safe ureteral visualization and ureterolysis. The five cases presented included patients with deep infiltrating endometriosis, adenomyosis, stage 3 endometriosis, and unusual anatomic variants such as an ectopic left kidney with atypical ureteral pathways, all scenarios where precise ureteral identification is critical to prevent injury during complex pelvic dissection. ICG fluorescence proved especially valuable in fibrotic fields where normal anatomic landmarks were distorted, offering trainees improved visual guidance in minimally invasive surgery where tactile feedback is limited. Despite these promising results, broader adoption of ICG-guided techniques remains limited by the lack of standardized dosing, dilution protocols, and instillation techniques, particularly for intravesical applications.

title: Evaluation and Treatment of Chronic Pelvic Pain
summary:
# Endometriosis and Chronic Pelvic Pain: Clinical Manifestations and Mechanisms

Endometriosis is a major gynecologic pathology associated with chronic pelvic pain affecting approximately 90% of patients, characterized by the "painful triad" of dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and nonmenstrual pelvic pain, alongside infertility and fatigue. The condition generates pain through multiple mechanisms: endometriosis lesions release proinflammatory cytokines that activate peripheral nociceptors causing nociceptive pain, while direct compression or infiltration of nerves such as the obturator or sciatic nerve produces neuropathic pain. Endometriosis frequently coexists with other chronic pain conditions—patients have significantly elevated odds of developing vulvodynia, irritable bowel syndrome (up to 18 times higher risk), fibromyalgia, interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome, and migraine headaches. Beyond endometriosis, adenomyosis and uterine leiomyomas represent additional common gynecologic contributors to chronic pelvic pain, with adenomyosis co-occurring in 15–91% of endometriosis cases. Multiple non-gynecologic musculoskeletal and neurologic factors including pelvic floor dysfunction, vulvodynia, bladder pain syndrome, and pelvic neuropathies compound chronic pelvic pain, while nociplastic pain mechanisms—involving central nervous system sensitization and influenced by female sex, obesity, trauma, and physical inactivity—contribute to widespread pain, fatigue, and functional disability.

title: Deep pelvic endometriosis: clinical features, diagnosis, and treatment - a comprehensive review
summary: Deep infiltrative endometriosis (DIE) is a complex gynecologic condition characterized by endometrial tissue infiltrating more than 5 mm into pelvic organs, particularly the rectovaginal septum and uterosacral ligaments, and is strongly associated with chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. The pathogenesis involves multiple mechanisms including altered estrogen and progesterone receptor signaling, upregulation of aromatase leading to increased local estradiol production, oxidative stress from iron accumulation, elevated inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1β, and reduced natural killer cell function in the peritoneal cavity. Diagnosis relies on imaging modalities including transvaginal ultrasound (which achieves 91% sensitivity and 98% specificity) and MRI with vaginal and rectal gel, with the "slide sign" useful for predicting Pouch of Douglas obliteration. Medical treatment options include oral contraceptives, progestogens, GnRH analogs and antagonists, with specific agents like dienogest, norethindrone acetate, and elagolix demonstrating efficacy in reducing pain symptoms, while danazol effectively addresses dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia. Surgical approaches including shaving excision, disc excision, and colorectal segmental resection provide definitive treatment, with shaving excision demonstrating only 4% symptom recurrence rates and 65.4% pregnancy rates in women seeking conception.

title: Clinical characterization of endometriosis phenotypes
summary:
# Summary: Clinical Characterization of Endometriosis Phenotypes

Endometriosis presents as a heterogeneous disease with distinct clinical phenotypes—superficial endometriosis (SE), deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE), and adenomyosis (AM)—each displaying characteristic pain profiles. Adenomyosis emerges as the most symptomatic phenotype, strongly associated with high-frequency pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dysuria, and dysmenorrhea, with disease severity increasing when AM coexists with other endometriosis subtypes. Superficial endometriosis alone produces the mildest symptoms, while DIE specifically correlates with dyschezia and bowel infiltration rather than generalized pain intensity. Combined phenotypes (SE/AM and SE/DIE/AM) demonstrate significantly elevated pain across all dimensions compared to isolated SE, and adenomyosis cases typically present at an older age than other subtypes. Notably, the revised American Society for Reproductive Medicine (rASRM) staging score fails to correlate with symptom severity or clinical presentation, highlighting the importance of phenotype-specific characterization for predicting patient symptoms and clinical outcomes in endometriosis management.

title: Diagnostic discrepancies and clinical value of second medical opinions (SMO) for endometriosis: a nationwide study analysis
summary: Endometriosis is a highly prevalent condition associated with debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, non-menstrual pelvic pain, and chronic fatigue, with 61% of patients reporting negative impacts on daily life and 88% requiring regular pain medication. The condition frequently causes psychological burden, with anxiety and depression rates significantly exceeding general population prevalence, alongside substantial functional impairment manifested by work interruption (56%), school absence (61%), and sexual dysfunction (69%). A major clinical challenge is prolonged diagnostic delay stemming from non-specific symptoms, absence of definitive biomarkers, and underestimation of symptomatology, which perpetuates emotional distress, strained relationships, and prolonged opioid use. Second medical opinions demonstrated substantial clinical value by modifying initial diagnoses in 51% of cases, reducing diagnostic uncertainty from 25% to 5%, and providing definitive diagnosis at a mean of 3.8 years post-symptom onset, while overturning incorrect endometriosis diagnoses in 28% of cases. Comprehensive management requires multidisciplinary approaches integrating pain management, hormonal therapy, surgical intervention, psychological support, and fertility preservation strategies, with emerging tools including MRI guidelines and saliva-based biomarkers potentially enhancing diagnostic accuracy. These findings underscore the critical importance of specialist consultation networks and standardized symptom assessment protocols in improving diagnostic timeliness and reducing the substantial disease burden on patient quality of life.

title: Long-Term Burden of Adolescent-Onset Endometriosis: A Case Report Highlighting Recurrent Disease and Fertility-Preserving Dilemmas
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory disease that causes significant clinical burden, particularly when onset occurs during adolescence. The condition manifests with diverse symptoms including chronic pelvic pain (with neuropathic, nociceptive, and nociplastic components), severe dysmenorrhea, back pain, gastrointestinal complaints, dyspareunia, and infertility, with laparoscopic findings showing ovarian endometriomas and deep infiltrating disease even in young patients. Adolescent-onset endometriosis follows a prolonged clinical course characterized by recurrent disease and repeated surgeries, especially in younger patients with advanced-stage disease, and is associated with serious obstetric complications including placenta previa, preterm birth, increased cesarean delivery rates, and elevated cancer risk (ovarian, thyroid, and breast). Management involves long-term medical therapy with combined oral contraceptives or progestins, though treatment-refractory cases may require surgical intervention including hysterectomy with bilateral adnexectomy, which this case report demonstrates can provide definitive pain relief. The substantial lifetime burden of adolescent-onset endometriosis underscores the need for early recognition, as diagnostic delays averaging six years from symptom onset significantly impact patient outcomes.

title: Fluorescence-guided nerve-sparing surgery for deep endometriosis using indocyanine green
summary:
# Summary

Deep endometriosis, characterized by lesions in the uterosacral ligaments and rectum that frequently cause chronic pelvic pain and postoperative voiding dysfunction, presents significant surgical challenges due to the proximity of pelvic autonomic nerves and vital structures. Indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence imaging with near-infrared visualization has emerged as a valuable intraoperative tool that enables real-time identification of endometriosis lesions, pelvic autonomic nerves, ureters, and blood vessels during surgery. By enhancing visualization of these critical anatomical structures, ICG-guided surgery facilitates nerve-sparing surgical approaches that treat deep endometriosis while preserving pelvic autonomic function. The fluorescence-guided technique improves the surgeon's decision-making process by allowing simultaneous assessment of tissue perfusion, hemostasis, and risk of pelvic organ injury. This application of ICG fluorescence represents an important advancement in minimizing postoperative complications associated with deep endometriosis surgery, particularly voiding dysfunction related to nerve injury.

title: Infertility management in patients with bowel endometriosis: the current landscape and the promise of randomised trials
summary: Bowel endometriosis, which affects 8-12% of patients with deep endometriosis, presents a significant clinical challenge for fertility management due to multiple mechanisms contributing to subfertility, including distorted pelvic anatomy, abnormal utero-tubal transport, poor oocyte quality, impaired implantation, and inflammatory environments created by endometriotic nodules. The condition frequently coexists with other infertility factors such as endometriomas, hydrosalpinx, and adenomyosis, and is associated with severe pain and dyspareunia that can further reduce conception likelihood. Laparoscopic surgical excision improves fertility outcomes particularly in younger patients without adenomyosis and with minimal additional infertility factors, with success dependent on completeness of resection, surgical expertise, and proper candidate selection. For patients with favorable reproductive prognosis (age <35, patent tubes, normal ovarian reserve, no adenomyosis), natural conception after surgery is achievable, whereas older patients or those with diminished ovarian reserve should pursue assisted reproductive technologies without delay. Notably, patients with untreated colorectal endometriosis achieve similar fertility outcomes after IVF, and the timing between surgery and ART initiation significantly impacts outcomes. Medical therapies can alleviate pain but are unsuitable for patients seeking conception, making individualized treatment selection critical.

title: The impact of laparoscopic deep endometriosis surgery on sexual functioning and distress
summary: Deep endometriosis significantly impairs sexual functioning and quality of life through multiple mechanisms, including dyspareunia caused by lesions in the posterior vaginal fornix, pouch of Douglas, uterosacral ligaments, and rectum. Laparoscopic surgical resection of deep endometriosis lesions produces substantial improvements across all domains of sexual functioning, including desire, arousal, lubrication, orgasm, and satisfaction, while simultaneously reducing dyspareunia, sexual distress, and pain scores.
