Beyond sexual outcomes, surgery improves overall quality of life, reduces depressive symptoms, enhances positive affect, and alleviates multiple pain syndromes including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyschezia, and dysuria. Complete resection was achieved in 94% of surgeries, with 56% of patients requiring bowel surgery, yet neither bowel resection nor major postoperative complications negatively affected postoperative sexual functioning. Three to six months after surgery, most sexual functioning domains normalized to levels comparable with healthy controls, though some domains including arousal, lubrication, and satisfaction remained modestly lower in deep endometriosis patients. These findings demonstrate that surgical intervention substantially restores sexual function and psychological well-being in women with deep endometriosis.

title: Perceptions of endometriosis surgery on TikTok: quality and implications for patient counselling
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic gynecological condition characterized by endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus that presents with multiple symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. Surgical management, particularly laparoscopic resection and excision, remains a cornerstone of definitive diagnosis and treatment, with reported benefits including symptom relief in 79% of cases and improved fertility outcomes. However, patient-generated content on TikTok reveals significant barriers to endometriosis care, including delayed diagnosis, misdiagnosis, medical gaslighting, and inadequate presurgical counselling, alongside common postoperative complications such as pain, bloating, and recurrent symptoms. While patient-created videos scored low on evidence-based quality measures and often conveyed negative tones focused on recovery challenges, they achieved engagement levels comparable to professional content, suggesting their considerable influence on patient perspectives. The study underscores a critical gap between emotionally resonant patient narratives and evidence-based guidance, highlighting the need for improved healthcare professional training, earlier diagnosis protocols, and accessible care options to enhance endometriosis management and patient counselling.

title: Ultrasonic surgical aspiration (CUSA®) for laparoscopic excision of endometriosis: a prospective case series demonstrating safety and precision in fertility-preserving surgery
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis affects approximately 10% of reproductive-age women and is associated with chronic pelvic pain, infertility, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and reduced quality of life. This prospective case series evaluated the Cavitron Ultrasonic Surgical Aspirator (CUSA®) as a novel approach for laparoscopic excision of endometriosis, demonstrating significant advantages over conventional surgical modalities. CUSA operates through ultrasonic cavitation that selectively fragments hydrated endometriotic tissue while preserving collagen-rich vessels and nerves, generating minimal thermal spread compared to monopolar and bipolar electrosurgery, which cause unintended thermal injury to surrounding tissues. The continuous irrigation-aspiration system maintains optimal surgical field visualization while dissipating heat, resulting in ultra-low blood loss (median <10 mL), rapid operative times (8.5±3.0 minutes), and zero intraoperative complications. Patients experienced significant postoperative pain reduction, with Numeric Pain Rating Scores decreasing by 3.2 points at 6-8 weeks follow-up, and histology confirmed complete excision of all visible endometriotic lesions. These findings support CUSA as a precision, tissue-sparing, fertility-preserving surgical technique with a short learning curve and clinical advantages over conventional energy devices and traditional cystectomy approaches.

title: Women with endometriosis who undergo IVF: a contemporary review of therapeutic strategies for successful outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that causes significant clinical manifestations including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia, while substantially reducing fertility. The pathogenesis involves multiple mechanisms, particularly retrograde menstruation (Sampson's theory) and vascular or lymphatic dissemination of endometrial cells, with estrogen driving lesion proliferation once established. Endometriosis-related infertility stems from chronic inflammation impairing oocyte quality, compromised endometrial receptivity, and adhesive disease, with ovarian endometriomas particularly detrimental due to impaired follicular growth, oocyte maturation, and fertilization capacity. In vitro fertilization remains the most effective treatment for endometriosis-related infertility, with emerging therapeutic strategies including GnRH analogue pre-treatment to reduce disease burden and inflammatory impact, progestin priming to optimize stimulation, and letrozole stimulation with frozen embryo transfer to enhance endometrial receptivity. Advanced-stage endometriosis (stages 3–4) associates with diminished ovarian reserve and poorer reproductive outcomes, necessitating careful consideration of surgical intervention prior to IVF given the additional ovarian reserve reduction from endometrioma resection. Future therapeutic approaches targeting oxidative stress and systemic inflammation may further improve outcomes for women with endometriosis undergoing assisted reproduction.

title: ‘I think it is helpful … I mean it’s not always helpful’ — diagnostic complexity in endometriosis: a qualitative study
journal: The British Journal of General Practice
summary: Endometriosis is a heterogeneous condition presenting with diverse clinical manifestations including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea, heavy menstrual bleeding, bladder and bowel symptoms, and fatigue, while also impacting fertility, mental health, and quality of life. Primary care clinicians face significant diagnostic complexity when managing endometriosis due to uncertainty about whether obtaining a definitive diagnosis will change first-line medical management or enable more effective interventions than empirical treatment trials alone. While diagnosis provides important benefits such as access to specialist care, social validation of symptoms, support community membership, and guidance for evidence-based treatments including surgery and GnRH analogue therapy, the heterogeneity of endometriosis makes it difficult to predict future clinical impacts and treatment responses. Clinicians must navigate tension between recognizing the value of a disease-based diagnosis within a biomedical hierarchy and acknowledging that laparoscopic confirmation carries diagnostic risk without guaranteed therapeutic benefit. The study highlights how chronic pelvic pain and difficulty conceiving create particular diagnostic uncertainty in primary care, where empirical treatment approaches may sometimes suffice without formal diagnosis. These findings underscore the complex intersecting considerations that influence diagnostic decision-making in endometriosis management.

title: Can hematological and biochemical parameters clinically predict the diagnosis of deep infiltrating endometriosis?
summary:
# Summary

Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) is characterized by a distinctive clinical and biochemical profile that can aid in diagnostic prediction. Patients with DIE experience significantly higher rates of dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain compared to those with ovarian endometriosis, reflecting the infiltrative nature of lesions penetrating the rectovaginal septum and adjacent organs. Elevated serum CA125 levels emerge as the most useful individual biomarker (AUC = 0.782), while the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) and HALP index demonstrate moderate discriminative ability, indicating underlying systemic inflammation in DIE patients. Additionally, DIE patients show reduced hemoglobin, lymphocyte counts, and ferritin levels compared to controls, suggesting immune dysregulation and iron depletion associated with chronic bleeding. A combined biomarker model incorporating CA125, NLR, and HALP substantially improves diagnostic accuracy (AUC = 0.84), with dysmenorrhea severity, dyspareunia, pain scores, and reduced hemoglobin and lymphocyte counts emerging as independent predictors of DIE in multivariable analysis. These findings demonstrate that integrating clinical symptoms with hematological and biochemical parameters provides a clinically relevant approach for preoperative identification of DIE.

title: Uterine artery S/D ratio and serum CA125 as predictors of early pregnancy loss in women with ovarian endometriomas: a retrospective case-control study
summary:
# Article Summary

Women with ovarian endometriomas who experience early pregnancy loss exhibit elevated uterine artery systolic/diastolic (S/D) ratios and increased serum CA125 levels compared to those with successful pregnancies. Endometriosis-associated inflammation, immune dysregulation, and vascular dysfunction impair endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation, contributing to miscarriage risk in this population. Multivariate logistic regression analysis identified bilateral mean uterine artery S/D ratio and CA125 levels as independent predictors of early spontaneous miscarriage, with receiver operating characteristic curve analysis demonstrating good predictive value (AUC = 0.75 and 0.76, respectively). The combined assessment of these two biomarkers provides clinically useful risk stratification for identifying endometriosis patients at higher risk of early pregnancy loss. Vascular dysfunction reflected by abnormal uterine artery blood flow parameters directly correlates with impaired placental perfusion and pregnancy complications in women with endometriomas. These findings suggest that evaluating uterine artery hemodynamics and CA125 levels during early pregnancy can help clinicians identify high-risk patients requiring enhanced monitoring and intervention strategies.

title: Development of an AI-based magnetic resonance imaging reading support program (AMP) for deep endometriosis diagnosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, affecting 5-10% of reproductive-age women, is associated with chronic pelvic pain and infertility, with deep endometriosis representing the most aggressive subtype characterized by invasive nodular lesions in the uterosacral ligaments, rectovaginal septum, bladder, and rectum that cause adhesions and severe pain. While magnetic resonance imaging is superior to transvaginal ultrasonography for detecting deep endometriosis, small and irregularly shaped lesions create diagnostic challenges and delays. The authors developed AMP (AI-based MRI reading support program), comprising three machine learning models using nnU-Net for plaque and ovarian cyst segmentation and LightGBM for adhesion detection, which demonstrated strong correlations with expert radiologist measurements. In clinical utility testing, AMP increased plaque detection sensitivity from 0.73 to 0.91 and improved inter-organ adhesion detection by 1.2-1.3-fold while reducing inter-reader variability and supporting less experienced radiologists. These findings establish AMP as a reliable non-invasive tool with potential to minimize diagnostic delays and reduce radiologist workload in deep endometriosis evaluation.

title: Sociodemographic and clinical characteristics of patients with endometriosis: a facility-based cross-sectional study from Bangladesh
summary: This facility-based cross-sectional study from Bangladesh characterizes the clinical presentation and pathophysiology of endometriosis, a condition where ectopic endometrial tissue outside the uterus causes inflammation, scarring, and adhesions in the pelvic cavity. Dysmenorrhea was the predominant symptom, affecting 92.6% of patients, while other cardinal features included non-menstrual pelvic pain (64.5%), dyspareunia (50%), and chronic pelvic pain overall, with dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia correlating with greater pain severity than non-menstrual pain. The ectopic tissue triggers heightened inflammatory responses and uterine contractility, manifesting in diverse symptoms including urinary dysfunction (dysuria 26.5%, polyuria 40.9%), gastrointestinal complications (diarrhea 14%, constipation 7.5%), and approximately 36% of patients experiencing infertility. Deeply infiltrative endometriosis most commonly involved the pelvic side wall (42.6%), with additional sites including the posterior wall, rectum, sigmoid, and ureter, producing location-specific symptoms. Women of Southeast Asian ethnicity demonstrated higher risk for moderate-to-severe disease, potentially due to delayed care-seeking and underlying genetic-environmental factors. Treatment primarily relied on NSAIDs and hormonal therapies, though oral contraceptive usage remained low despite evidence of symptom reduction.

title: Non-Invasive Diagnosis of Endometriosis by Questionnaires in Patients Using Contraception
summary: Endometriosis manifests through a diverse spectrum of pain symptoms, including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dysuria, and dyschezia, along with systemic symptoms such as infertility, bloating, and nausea. Women with endometriosis experience significantly higher pain intensity and prevalence across multiple pain descriptors—including cramping, stabbing, neuropathic, and lightning-like pain—distributed across widespread anatomical locations from the lower abdomen to the thighs and legs. A validated decision tree questionnaire-based approach achieved high diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity 0.924, specificity 0.917) for non-invasive endometriosis detection, offering substantially better diagnostic efficiency than traditional waiting periods. The questionnaire method successfully identified cases across all endometriosis types (ovarian, peritoneal, and deep infiltrating) even in patients using hormonal contraception, with pain severity, duration, and distribution patterns emerging as significant predictive indicators. These findings demonstrate that reliable non-invasive questionnaire-based endometriosis diagnosis is feasible and could substantially reduce diagnostic delays, complementing conventional imaging modalities like transvaginal ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging.

title: Endometriosis and Reproductive Sparing Surgery: A Narrative Review and AGREE II-S-Based Evaluation of International Guidelines
summary:
# Summary: Endometriosis Management and Reproductive-Sparing Surgery

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by the presence of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, causing a classical tetrad of symptoms—dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dysuria, and dyschezia—while also compromising fertility and quality of life. Lesions primarily localize to the pelvic cavity and organs, though extra-pelvic involvement in the pleura, pericardium, and central nervous system has been documented, with deep infiltrating endometriosis commonly affecting the rectum, sigmoid colon, bladder, and ureters. Current international guidelines recommend a stepped approach wherein medical hormonal therapy precedes surgery in most cases, with surgical intervention (preferably laparoscopic excision) reserved for persistent symptoms despite treatment or urgent indications such as hydronephrosis or bowel stenosis. For endometriomas specifically, guidelines now recommend against routine excision prior to assisted reproductive technology, as surgery risks diminished ovarian reserve and reduced anti-Müllerian hormone levels; instead, asymptomatic endometriomas should be managed conservatively. Laparoscopic cystectomy provides superior long-term pain relief and lower recurrence rates compared to drainage-and-ablation or sclerotherapy approaches, though ablative techniques preserve ovarian tissue and may be appropriate when fertility concerns are paramount. Preoperative anti-Müllerian hormone testing and fertility counseling, with consideration of oocyte or embryo cryopreservation in bilateral disease, reflect evolving paradigms prioritizing reproductive capacity alongside symptom control.

title: Endometriosis Imaging Diagnosis Beyond Specialized Centers: A Retrospective Cross-Sectional Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cross-sectional study examined the diagnostic accuracy of imaging modalities for endometriosis in non-specialized clinical centers. Deep endometriosis, defined as infiltration of surrounding structures by more than 5 mm, commonly occurs at extrauterine sites and presents clinically with chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and neuropathic pain. While pelvic MRI demonstrated excellent sensitivity (100%) and specificity (93.9%) for detecting ovarian endometriomas, it exhibited substantially lower sensitivity (75.6%) for deep endometriosis detection and yielded false-negative results in approximately one-quarter of surgically confirmed cases. In contrast, transvaginal ultrasound performed by experienced sonographers achieved diagnostic accuracy comparable to or superior to surgical findings when assessing deep endometriosis in multiple anatomical locations. Notably, symptom severity emerged as an independent predictor of deep endometriosis status, with patients reporting severe symptoms having 3.55 times higher odds of harboring deep endometriosis compared to those with milder symptoms, an association independent of endometrioma size. The study highlights the critical limitations of non-invasive imaging in non-specialized settings and emphasizes the importance of maintaining high clinical suspicion for deep endometriosis in symptomatic patients, even when imaging results appear negative.

title: Infiltrative Endometriosis Clinically Mimicking Ovarian Malignancy: Diagnostic Utility of Intraoperative Frozen Section
summary: Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue predominantly located in the pelvic cavity, commonly affects the ovaries, posterior broad ligament, cul-de-sac, and uterosacral ligaments, with rare extrapelvic manifestations in the gastrointestinal tract, abdominal wall, pleura, pericardium, and central nervous system. The disease presents with multiple clinical symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, and is observed in up to 30% of infertile women and 45% of those with chronic pelvic pain. Atypical presentations of infiltrative endometriosis, including elevated tumor markers such as CA-125 (which correlates with disease severity) and inconclusive imaging findings, can clinically mimic ovarian malignancy and complicate diagnosis. Histopathological examination identifying endometrial glands and stroma with estrogen receptor positivity remains the diagnostic gold standard, though endometriotic lesions may demonstrate hyperplasia and atypia with potential for malignant transformation. Intraoperative frozen section analysis proves valuable in differentiating benign endometriotic lesions from malignant ovarian tumors when clinical and radiological features are inconclusive, highlighting the importance of considering endometriosis as a differential diagnosis in suspicious pelvic masses.

title: Prognostic Factors for Pain and Fertility Outcomes Following Laparoscopic Endometriosis Surgery: A Single-Center Experience
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a common condition causing chronic pelvic pain and infertility, with ectopic endometrial tissue frequently found on the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and gastrointestinal tract. Laparoscopic surgery effectively treats endometriosis by removing lesions and adhesions, resulting in significant pain reduction across all disease severity groups, though notably, factors such as disease severity, location, BMI, and CA125 levels did not predict pain improvement outcomes. The most important prognostic factor for fertility was younger age at treatment, with women treated before age 30 showing substantially higher pregnancy rates (adjusted odds ratio 20.57) compared to older patients, while disease severity and other demographic factors showed no significant association with pregnancy success. Preoperative imaging using transvaginal ultrasound and MRI demonstrated high accuracy for detecting endometriosis, particularly for ovarian and bowel involvement. These findings establish laparoscopic surgery as an effective therapeutic intervention for both pain relief and fertility restoration in endometriosis, with timing of treatment being the critical prognostic factor for reproductive outcomes.

title: Uncommon diagnostic aspects of adolescent endometriosis: case series with narrative review of the literature
summary:
# Summary

Adolescent endometriosis is a complex inflammatory and estrogen-dependent condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue with distinctive clinical and pathological features. The disease arises through multiple mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, genetic-epigenetic alterations, and potentially embryonic Müllerian remnants, with inflammation and local estrogen production via aromatase expression driving tissue proliferation and pain symptomatology. Clinical presentations in adolescents typically include dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and heavy menstrual bleeding, with frequent associations with depression, anxiety, and in some cases adenomyosis or bowel involvement characterized by fibrosis and nodule formation. Enhanced pain signaling results from local neurogenesis and nerve fiber recruitment, with bowel endometriosis showing particularly extensive neural invasion. First-line treatments include combined oral contraceptives, dienogest, and levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems, with NSAIDs for pain management and GnRH agonists reserved for second-line therapy. Risk factors include early menarche, positive family history, and Müllerian abnormalities, while rare presentations may include hydrosalpinx or coexisting ovarian pathology, emphasizing the diagnostic complexity of adolescent endometriosis.

title: Data-driven diagnosis and clinical presentation of high-tone pelvic floor dysfunction
journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
summary: High-tone pelvic floor dysfunction (HTPFD) is characterized by hyper-contractile, tender pelvic floor muscles that fail to relax and are strongly associated with multiple pelvic pain symptoms including dyspareunia, persistent pelvic pain occurring at least 14 days per month, and sensations of heaviness in the pelvis. A pelvic myofascial tenderness score of ≥12/60 on examination demonstrates good diagnostic accuracy for identifying HTPFD and correlates with patient-reported pain reproduction. Patients with HTPFD exhibit a distinctive clinical phenotype characterized by pain exacerbation with physical activity and walking, pain radiating to other body regions, associated nausea and vomiting, and notably impaired sexual function with severe dyspareunia and intercourse avoidance. This condition frequently co-occurs with other chronic pelvic pain conditions, particularly endometriosis (60% prevalence) and interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (80% prevalence), and is associated with nociplastic pain features including widespread pain, low back pain, and migraine headaches. Patients with HTPFD are typically younger and nulliparous and demonstrate poor analgesic response to nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, suggesting a central pain processing component that may limit the efficacy of peripherally directed treatments. These data-driven findings establish a comprehensive clinical presentation of HTPFD that distinguishes it from other pelvic pain conditions and emphasize the importance of systematic pelvic floor examination in the diagnostic evaluation of chronic pelvic pain.

title: Lipedema in Women and Its Interrelationship with Endometriosis and Other Gynecologic Diseases: A Scoping Review
summary: This scoping review reveals that lipedema, a chronic condition characterized by painful, symmetrical subcutaneous fat accumulation in the extremities resistant to weight loss, shares significant pathophysiological overlap with endometriosis and other hormone-sensitive gynecologic disorders. Both conditions demonstrate hormonal dependence, chronic inflammatory profiles, and genetic susceptibility mechanisms, with symptom onset and progression strongly linked to reproductive milestones including puberty, pregnancy, and menopause. Lipedema exhibits distinctive features including easy bruising and hormonally responsive adipose tissue, alongside discordance between lower extremity adiposity and systemic metabolic markers, distinguishing it from common obesity phenotypes. Recent molecular investigations have increasingly implicated steroidogenic enzymes and intracellular hormone metabolism pathways in lipedema pathophysiology, alongside genetic susceptibility markers that may explain disease clustering. The review identifies lipedema's frequent comorbidity with polycystic ovary syndrome, uterine fibroids, premenstrual dysphoric disorder, insulin resistance, and lymphatic dysfunction, suggesting a shared systemic inflammatory and hormonal etiology. These findings suggest that lipedema warrants recognition as part of a spectrum of hormone-dependent gynecologic conditions rather than as an isolated metabolic disorder.

title: Patient‐centered priorities in endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain: A mixed‐methods and thematic analysis of intake narratives
summary:
# Summary

This mixed-methods study examined patient-centered priorities among individuals with endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain, revealing a complex symptom landscape extending far beyond traditionally recognized manifestations. Endometriosis is conventionally associated with dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, but patients reported 16 distinct pain concerns including lower back pain, migraines, neuropathic pain, and bowel-related pain, alongside 20 non-pain symptoms such as fatigue, bloating, constipation, anxiety, and depression. Notably, superficial endometriosis cases showed greater need for diagnostic clarity, while ovarian endometriosis was particularly associated with fertility concerns in younger patients (ages 25–34), whereas older patients prioritized quality of life and education. Treatment preferences varied widely, with many patients seeking alternatives to hormonal therapy and expressing interest in surgical options (excision surgery, hysterectomy), pelvic floor physiotherapy, and fertility-sparing approaches, underscoring the need for integrated, personalized care strategies that address both pain management and comorbid conditions including irritable bowel syndrome, polycystic ovary syndrome, interstitial cystitis, and adenomyosis.

title: Rectosigmoid Endometrioma Mimicking Rectal Hematoma: A Diagnostic Dilemma Managed Conservatively
summary: This case report describes a rectosigmoid endometrioma, a form of deep infiltrating endometriosis affecting the bowel in approximately 5%-12% of endometriosis patients, with the rectosigmoid colon representing up to 90% of bowel involvement. The patient presented with classic manifestations including painless hematochezia, cyclical alterations in bowel habits, dyschezia, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia, stemming from chronic cyclical hormonal stimulation that causes repeated hemorrhage, fibrosis, and smooth muscle hypertrophy of ectopic endometrial tissue. Imaging with magnetic resonance imaging—the gold standard diagnostic modality—revealed an 8.2 × 12 cm intraluminal rectal mass and concurrent left ovarian endometrioma. Conservative management with sequential GnRH analogue therapy followed by maintenance dienogest treatment achieved dramatic lesion reduction and symptom resolution, demonstrating that hormonal suppression can effectively manage selected cases and avoid surgical intervention. This case illustrates the diagnostic challenge of rectosigmoid endometriosis, which can mimic malignancy or hematologic conditions, and highlights the efficacy of first-line hormonal therapy in achieving significant clinical improvement without surgery.

title: Clinical diagnosis and treatment of bowel endometriosis and the distribution characteristics of lesions
summary:
# Summary

Bowel endometriosis (BE) presents with characteristic gynecological and gastrointestinal symptoms, including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dyschezia, diarrhea, and constipation. The pathogenesis involves multiple mechanisms including iron-induced oxidation, antioxidant imbalance, abnormal estrogen metabolism, and immune dysfunction. Diagnostic imaging, particularly endoscopic ultrasound (90.2% positive predictive value) and pelvic MRI (88.2%), effectively identifies BE and differentiates it from colorectal malignancy, with transvaginal ultrasound and bimanual examination providing additional clinical confirmation. Surgical treatment tailored to lesion characteristics—limited excision for smaller lesions and segmental bowel resection for larger, multifocal, or deeply invasive lesions—successfully resolves symptoms in all patients and improves fertility outcomes. BE frequently coexists with adenomyosis (67.1% of cases) and ovarian endometriotic cysts, with rectal involvement being most common (81.8%), and adjuvant gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist therapy is administered in 77.3% of patients to optimize clinical outcomes.

title: Ablation compared with excision in the surgical management of peritoneal endometriosis: a retrospective study of pain, re-operation, and pregnancy outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study of 67 patients with histologically confirmed peritoneal endometriosis compared laparoscopic excision and ablation surgical approaches for managing the disease's multiple clinical manifestations, including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and infertility. Both surgical techniques—excision with complete lesion removal and ablation using bipolar thermal coagulation—produced significant and sustained reductions in all pain symptoms over time, with improvement correlating directly with time elapsed since surgery and enhanced further by postoperative hormone therapy using combined oral contraceptives, progestins, or hormonal intrauterine devices. Among the subset of 27 patients desiring conception, approximately 63% achieved pregnancy postoperatively regardless of surgical technique, demonstrating favorable fertility outcomes from both approaches. Both excision and ablation required re-operation in approximately 18% of cases, suggesting comparable efficacy despite different mechanistic approaches to lesion management. The findings underscore that endometriosis-associated pain requires individualized multimodal management strategies, combining surgical intervention with appropriate hormonal suppression and supportive therapies such as pelvic floor physiotherapy to optimize long-term symptom control and fertility restoration.

title: The value of the AAGL staging system for predicting recurrence after conservative surgery for ovarian endometriomas
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian endometriomas, the most common form of endometriosis causing chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, exhibit high recurrence rates following conservative laparoscopic surgery, averaging 20% at 2 years and 50% at 5 years. This study demonstrates that the AAGL staging system better predicts postoperative recurrence than the revised ASRM system, with an AAGL score greater than 16 associated with significantly increased hazard ratios for recurrence. Independent risk factors for recurrence include preoperative serum CA125 levels, intraoperative bleeding volume, and higher AAGL scores, whereas BMI, age, gravidity, parity, and prior pelvic surgery were not predictive. The AAGL system shows superior concordance with surgical complexity assessment compared to the r-ASRM system, providing improved severity stratification for disease management. Recurrence negatively impacts fertility outcomes, with patients experiencing recurrence achieving lower clinical pregnancy rates than those without recurrence.

title: Clinical and cost-effectiveness of medical management versus surgery for deep infiltrating endometriosis: synopsis from the DIAMOND RCT.
summary: Deep infiltrating endometriosis, which occurs when endometrial cells grow into deeper tissues and organs including the bowel, bladder, and vagina, causes significant clinical manifestations including chronic pelvic pain, abnormal bleeding, inflammation, and scarring that substantially impair quality of life. Two primary treatment approaches have demonstrated efficacy: laparoscopic excision surgery, which directly removes endometriotic lesions and reduces pain symptoms, and hormonal medical management, which works by downregulating endometrial cell growth and preventing new lesion formation. The DIAMOND randomized controlled trial evaluated the clinical and cost-effectiveness of these competing strategies, comparing the surgical approach's direct tissue removal benefits against medical management's lower risk profile and sustained hormonal suppression. While laparoscopic excision shows promise in improving pain outcomes in observational studies, hormonal treatment offers a less invasive therapeutic option, though each approach carries distinct advantages and limitations regarding symptom relief, recurrence prevention, and healthcare costs. This comparison addresses a critical clinical question for patients with deep endometriosis who must weigh the risks and benefits of definitive surgical intervention against conservative medical management to optimize individual treatment outcomes.

title: Diagnostic accuracy of machine learning for endometriosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated machine learning's diagnostic accuracy for endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory gynecological condition affecting 8%-10% of reproductive-age women and up to 50% of those with infertility, characterized by ectopic endometrial-like tissue growth in pelvic and extra-pelvic regions. Machine learning models demonstrated substantial diagnostic performance, with those based on genetic information (AUC 0.982), imaging features (AUC 0.979-0.983), and clinical features achieving high sensitivity and specificity, offering noninvasive alternatives to the current gold standard of laparoscopic visualization. Models leveraging salivary microRNAs, circulating microRNAs, and miRNA expression profiles showed particular promise, as microRNAs regulate key pathogenic mechanisms including inflammation, cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and tissue remodeling. Ensemble and boosting algorithms including LightGBM, XGBoost, and AdaBoost outperformed traditional approaches like logistic regression, while deep learning methods such as ResNet-152 and convolutional neural networks achieved exceptional performance for identifying specific endometriosis subtypes, including ovarian endometriotic cysts. Beyond genetic markers, machine learning successfully integrated diverse diagnostic data sources including proteomics, lipomics, microbiomics, ultrasound imaging, and biomarkers like CA-125 and dysmenorrhea history. These findings underscore machine learning's potential to enable early, noninvasive endometriosis diagnosis and reduce diagnostic delays for this condition associated with severe pain and reproductive complications.

title: Mind the gap: underreporting of key compartments in endometriosis MRI with free-text and non-disease-specific templates
summary: Endometriosis, a condition affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age and causing chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, requires comprehensive MRI evaluation for surgical planning given its complex multidisciplinary management. The Enzian classification divides endometriosis into standardized compartments across multiple anatomical sites including the ovaries, peritoneum, bladder, rectum, ureter, and intestine, with both the Enzian system and Deep Pelvic Endometriosis Index demonstrating good inter-reader agreement and excellent diagnostic accuracy. This study demonstrates that endometriosis-specific MRI templates substantially improve documentation completeness to 80% across all Enzian compartments, with odds ratios up to 13-fold higher for critical compartments compared to free-text reports, whereas free-text and non-disease-specific general templates significantly underreport key anatomical areas such as uterosacral ligaments, fallopian tubes, ureters, and the rectovaginal space. Template specificity, rather than mere structure alone, drives comprehensive endometriosis reporting and supports multidisciplinary communication for optimal surgical planning. These findings provide quantitative evidence that endometriosis-specific structured templates should replace generic reporting formats to ensure accurate documentation of all disease compartments and improve patient care.

title: Current Endometriosis Classifications (rASRM, #Enzian, AAGL2021) and their Correlation with Operative Time
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic condition affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, is defined by ectopic endometrial tissue occurring as peritoneal lesions, ovarian endometriomas, and deep infiltrating disease. Three primary classification systems—rASRM, Enzian, and AAGL2021—are used to characterize endometriosis severity, though the rASRM system has limited capacity to describe deep endometriosis and preoperative planning capability. All three classification systems demonstrate significant positive correlations with operative time, with compartment C3 (deep colorectal involvement) associated with the longest surgical duration, followed by compartments A3, FI, and FU representing deep infiltrating endometriosis. The Enzian and AAGL2021 classifications offer superior preoperative diagnostic utility compared to rASRM, enabling better surgical planning by precisely mapping disease across anatomical compartments. In the studied cohort, peritoneal endometriosis was most prevalent (85.6%), followed by ovarian endometriosis (49.6%), deep endometriosis (46.8%), and adenomyosis (22.3%), with these multiple disease manifestations contributing to surgical complexity and extended operative times.

title: Endometriosis and Fertility: A Practical Guide
summary: Endometriosis is a systemic estrogen-related disease characterized by ectopic endometrial growth that causes chronic inflammatory responses, chronic pain, and organ destruction, while significantly impairing fertility through multiple mechanisms. Women with endometriosis have two to four times higher risk of infertility compared to the general population, with reduced ovarian reserve, lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels, and associated adenomyosis further compromising nidation probability and increasing miscarriage risk; infertile women with endometriosis also experience worse quality of life and depressive symptoms. Diagnostic approaches include transvaginal ultrasound as first-line imaging for detecting endometriomas and deep infiltrating disease, with the ENZIAN classification documenting findings and fertility assessment using the endometriosis fertility index. Therapeutic options include hormone treatments inducing amenorrhea—such as dienogest, LNG-IUD, and GnRH agonists—which reduce inflammatory irritation, preserve ovarian reserve, and decrease endometrioma symptoms and recurrence. Surgical management involves careful consideration of benefits and harms, as surgical resection of endometriomas increases spontaneous pregnancy probability but reduces AMH levels, while cyst excision remains superior to other approaches for minimizing recurrence. For assisted reproductive technology, pretreatment prior to embryo transfer and salpingectomy or proximal fallopian tube occlusion in cases of hydrosalpinx significantly improve clinical pregnancy rates.

title: The outcomes of laparoscopic surgeries for urinary bladder and vesicouterine pouch endometriosis in the Polish population
summary:
# Summary

This study examined laparoscopic surgical outcomes for bladder and vesicouterine pouch endometriosis in a Polish population, conditions where endometrial tissue invades the urinary tract and reproductive structures. Bladder endometriosis predominantly presents with dysuria, dysmenorrhea, and recurrent urinary tract infections, while vesicouterine pouch endometriosis manifests primarily through dysmenorrhea and intermenstrual pain. The underlying pathophysiology involves retrograde menstruation and iatrogenic implantation, with inflammatory cytokines disrupting normal endometrial function and causing infertility through multiple mechanisms including fallopian tube destruction and implantation failure. Laparoscopic surgical resection, the gold standard treatment, significantly improved all major symptoms including dysuria, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia, with concurrent improvements in sleep quality and mental health; medical therapies including progesterone-based agents, combined oral contraceptives, and GnRH analogues were available as adjunctive options. Notably, vesicouterine pouch endometriosis frequently co-occurred with ovarian endometriosis and uterosacral ligament involvement, while bladder endometriosis was often associated with adenomyosis, highlighting the multifocal nature of advanced disease.

title: Comprehensive Approaches to Endometriosis Management and Targeted Strategies for Bowel Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a complex gynecological condition characterized by the presence of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, causing chronic cyclic pain and infertility through multiple pathogenic mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, and lymphatic/vascular dissemination. The disease presents in three major phenotypes—peritoneal, ovarian (cyst-forming), and deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE)—with bowel involvement representing the most severe form, accounting for 3-37% of all cases and affecting predominantly the sigmoid colon and rectum. Genetic and epigenetic factors, including polymorphisms in inflammation, estrogen metabolism, angiogenesis, and immune regulation genes, combined with lifestyle and reproductive risk factors, predispose individuals to endometriosis development. Bowel endometriosis manifests with diverse gastrointestinal symptoms including abdominal pain, dyschezia, constipation, diarrhea, cyclical rectal bleeding, and in severe cases, occlusive complications. Surgical excision of DIE lesions, including discoid resection and segmental resection, effectively treats associated symptoms and substantially improves quality of life, though standardized surgical approaches remain lacking. The Enzian classification system provides a standardized framework for characterizing deep endometriosis anatomical involvement across three axes spanning the rectovaginal space, parametrial structures, and rectosigmoid wall.

title: The assessment of association between endometrioma size and other endometriosis compartments according to #ENZIAN classification
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that endometrioma size, classified using the ENZIAN system, delineates two distinct endometriosis phenotypes with different clinical characteristics and disease mechanisms. Larger endometriomas (O3) present as localized ovarian disease predominantly in younger women, occurring predominantly as unilateral cysts with minimal association with adenomyosis or deep rectal involvement. In contrast, smaller endometriomas (O1/O2) are independently associated with extensive pelvic disease, including higher rates of adenomyosis, deep infiltrating rectal endometriosis, and bilateral involvement, likely representing secondary ovarian involvement arising from longstanding widespread pelvic endometriosis. The authors propose that adenomyosis and deep pelvic disease create a pro-inflammatory, fibrotic pelvic microenvironment that facilitates peritoneal implants and adhesions, which physically restrict cyst expansion and result in smaller lesions, whereas larger cysts may develop from functional ovarian cysts in younger women with active ovulation. These findings suggest that endometrioma size carries pathophysiological significance and may inform treatment decisions, particularly regarding surgical approaches and reproductive counseling, with the ENZIAN classification providing a standardized framework for characterizing these distinct disease patterns.

title: Misdiagnosis rate of endometriosis and strategies employed to identify endometriosis by analyzing patient characteristics in low-resource settings
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a common gynecological condition affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, characterized by a constellation of clinical manifestations including dysmenorrhea (particularly early-onset), heavy menstrual bleeding, and pelvic pain that significantly impairs quality of life. The pathophysiology involves retrograde menstruation combined with sex hormone-mediated inflammation, where unopposed estrogen stimulation promotes endometrial cell growth while progesterone resistance develops in ectopic tissue. Key risk factors include nulliparity and shorter menstrual cycles, whereas multiparity and higher BMI are protective, with progesterone-based hormonal therapies (progestins and oral contraceptives) effectively inhibiting angiogenesis, endometrial proliferation, and tissue invasion. Despite laparoscopy with histopathological examination remaining the diagnostic gold standard, imaging modalities such as ultrasonography and MRI provide non-invasive alternatives, yet nearly half of suspected cases are misdiagnosed, leading to substantial diagnostic delays that are partially attributable to inadequate patient education about menstrual symptoms in low-resource settings.

title: Differences in response to dienogest therapy among different phenotypes of endometriosis: A single-center retrospective cohort analysis
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory disorder associated with dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, with deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) causing the most severe symptoms and longest disease duration. Dienogest, a selective synthetic progestin, treats endometriosis across all three anatomical phenotypes (ovarian, superficial peritoneal, and deep infiltrating) by inhibiting ovulation, reducing estrogen levels, suppressing inflammatory responses and angiogenesis, and limiting lesion growth. While dienogest significantly reduced pain scores and improved quality of life in all phenotype groups after six months of treatment, the ovarian endometrioma group achieved the highest lesion reduction rate (47.5%) due to the drug's effectiveness against intracystic bleeding in cystic lesions, whereas superficial and deep infiltrating lesions showed limited improvement due to their substantial fibrotic architecture that reduces drug penetration. DIE emerged as an independent negative predictor for significant pain relief and was associated with higher adverse event rates, including breakthrough bleeding, weight gain, and mood swings, though DIE patients paradoxically showed the greatest magnitude of quality-of-life improvement. Baseline factors including higher pain scores and hemoglobin levels predicted better treatment outcomes, while elevated CA125 levels, higher disease stage scores, and coexisting adenomyosis predicted reduced treatment efficacy.

title: Activity–Selectivity
summary: Endometriosis affects up to 20% of reproductive-age women and is characterized by chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, abnormal bleeding, and infertility. This study evaluated chalcone derivatives, which possess inherent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties similar to flavonoids, as potential therapeutic agents. The researchers identified distinct activity profiles: compounds with high lipophilicity and low topological polar surface area (TPSA) exhibited selective cytotoxicity against 12Z endometriotic cells while sparing Ishikawa normal endometrial cells, with compounds 24 and 28 demonstrating the highest selectivity. Conversely, polyhydroxylated chalcones with multiple hydrogen-bond donors and acceptors displayed cytoprotective behavior toward normal endometrial cells. Electron-withdrawing substituents and increased lipophilicity correlated with enhanced 12Z cytotoxicity, while elevated TPSA and hydrogen-bonding capacity favored cytoprotective selectivity. These findings establish clear structure-activity relationships enabling the design of chalcone-based compounds with potential for selective targeting of endometriotic lesions while preserving normal endometrial function.

title: Development and validation of a clinical nomogram for predicting cumulative live birth rate in ovarian endometrioma patients undergoing ethanol sclerotherapy and in vitro fertilization/intracytoplasmic sperm injection
summary: Ovarian endometriomas impair fertility by distorting pelvic anatomy and reducing ovarian reserve, with traditional surgical stripping substantially lowering anti-Müllerian hormone levels and antral follicle counts. Ethanol sclerotherapy offers a superior alternative, selectively ablating cyst epithelium through cytotoxic effects while preserving the follicular pool and maintaining or improving AMH levels compared to surgical excision. This minimally invasive approach achieves comparable oocyte retrieval and may enhance IVF/ICSI outcomes by reducing local inflammation, making it an effective treatment for endometrioma patients seeking fertility preservation. The authors developed and validated a clinical nomogram incorporating four independent predictors—previous live birth history, controlled ovarian hyperstimulation protocol, oocyte retrieval number, and cyst diameter—to predict cumulative live birth rates in ethanol sclerotherapy-treated patients undergoing in vitro fertilization. The nomogram demonstrated good discriminatory ability in both training and validation cohorts, providing a clinically useful tool for personalizing fertility counseling in endometrioma patients. This work establishes ethanol sclerotherapy as a fertility-preserving treatment strategy that maintains reproductive potential while effectively managing symptoms and cyst burden.

title: Impact of Endometriosis on the Progression of Inflammatory Bowel Diseases: A Multicenter Retrospective Study
summary: Women with endometriosis have a significantly elevated risk of developing inflammatory bowel diseases (IBD), particularly Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, likely due to shared immune-mediated inflammatory pathways involving estrogen-driven immune dysregulation and elevated proinflammatory cytokines such as TNF-alpha. Both conditions present overlapping clinical symptoms including chronic abdominal pain, bowel movement disorders, and dysmenorrhea, which can complicate diagnosis and disease management. Interestingly, patients with comorbid endometriosis and IBD demonstrated less severe disease progression and reduced surgical intervention rates compared to those with IBD alone, alongside significantly lower exposure to immunosuppressants and biologics including anti-TNF agents. This paradoxical finding suggests that the immunological mechanisms underlying endometriosis may partially counteract IBD severity, though patients with concurrent disease did experience more extraintestinal manifestations such as articular involvement. The study highlights the importance of recognizing the bidirectional relationship between endometriosis and IBD in women of reproductive age, as diagnostic overlap and distinct treatment patterns warrant integrated clinical evaluation to optimize outcomes for this vulnerable population.

title: Assessment of postoperative uterine isthmus thickness on MRI after surgical resection of retrocervical deep infiltrating endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Surgical resection of retrocervical deep infiltrating endometriosis effectively eliminates the disease lesions but results in significant thinning of the posterior uterine isthmus on postoperative MRI. The degree of isthmus thickness reduction correlates with lesion size, preoperative cervical volume, and the occurrence of hemorrhagic complications during surgery. Notably, patients with preoperative urinary symptoms and those requiring digestive resection with ileostomy demonstrated greater postoperative isthmus thinning, and post-voiding residue of 100 cc or greater postoperatively was significantly associated with this anatomical change. While the clinical implications for future pregnancy remain unclear, the observed myometrial weakening mirrors known risks from other uterine surgeries such as cesarean section, highlighting the need for prospective studies to determine whether reduced isthmus thickness after endometriosis surgery affects pregnancy outcomes and uterine rupture risk.

title: Trends in endometriosis hospitalizations in Germany, 2000-2023
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, characterized by the growth of endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus in the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and pelvic and abdominal cavities. The condition causes multiple debilitating symptoms including severe lower abdominal and pelvic pain, heavy irregular menstrual bleeding, dysmenorrhea, deep dyspareunia, and impaired fertility, while frequently occurring alongside comorbidities such as irritable bowel syndrome, interstitial cystitis, depression, and anxiety disorders. Diagnosis and treatment typically involve laparoscopic procedures and surgical intervention to remove or ablate endometriotic lesions, though the disease has a high recurrence rate and some cases require more radical approaches such as hysterectomy or adnexectomy. German hospitalization data from 2000 to 2023 reveals a dramatic increase in endometriosis-related inpatient admissions, rising from 43.7 to 88.1 per 100,000 women, with significant regional variation and the largest increases in Berlin, Bavaria, and Brandenburg. This escalation in hospitalizations is attributable to increased public and clinical awareness, advances in diagnostic methods, expanded specialized care structures and endometriosis centers, and earlier detection of more complex cases with multiple comorbidities requiring surgical intervention. The rising trend underscores the substantial healthcare burden of endometriosis and the need for continued investment in specialized diagnostic and treatment infrastructure.

title: Prevalence of migraine subtypes in women with endometriosis and/or dysmenorrhea: results from a cross-sectional multicenter trial
summary: This cross-sectional multicenter study examined the association between surgically confirmed endometriosis (SCE) and migraine subtypes in women with endometriosis and/or dysmenorrhea. Women with SCE had significantly higher prevalence of any migraine diagnosis (41.0% vs. 32.5%), with notably elevated odds for migraine with aura (OR 1.61) and pure menstrual migraine (OR 11.27). Menstrually-related migraine was particularly severe, associated with higher attack frequency, greater pain intensity, and more substantial functional impairment across work, leisure, and family activities. The underlying mechanisms likely involve dysregulated estrogen signaling, hormonal fluctuations, neuroinflammatory processes, and central sensitization, as both endometriosis and migraine are estrogen-dependent conditions. Women with SCE demonstrated greater use of hormone treatments, predominantly progestin-only regimens taken continuously, while those with pure menstrual migraine had lower rates of endocrine treatment. These findings highlight the substantial clinical burden of comorbid endometriosis and migraine, particularly menstrually-related forms, and suggest a shared pathophysiological basis warranting integrated treatment approaches.

title: Impact of lesion size and localization on symptom severity in intestinal endometriosis
summary: Intestinal endometriosis, a deep endometrial invasion into the bowel wall primarily affecting the rectosigmoid region, causes a constellation of gastrointestinal and pain-related symptoms including dyschezia, hematochezia, abdominal pain, dyspareunia, and dysmenorrhea. Lesion size and localization significantly impact symptom severity; large rectal nodules classified as Enzian C3 (>3 cm) correlate with markedly higher severity of dyschezia, hematochezia, and abdominal pain compared to smaller lesions. Additional extrarectal intestinal involvement (Enzian FI) affecting the sigmoid colon, small bowel, and other regions is associated with increased bloating frequency and more severe hematochezia, with combined C and FI lesions producing the most severe symptoms. Notably, intestinal endometriosis frequently coexists with peritoneal, ovarian, and deep endometriosis as well as adenomyosis in nearly 95% of cases, reflecting the systemic nature of the disease. Symptom evaluation through patient diaries and questionnaires remains diagnostically important, as specific gastrointestinal manifestations can prompt clinical suspicion for intestinal involvement and guide appropriate imaging evaluation.

title: Pelvic Congestion Syndrome: The Gynecological Perspective
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic congestion syndrome (PCS) is a chronic pelvic pain condition caused by venous reflux and obstruction in the pelvic venous system, with significant clinical overlap with endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain, manifesting through dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dull aching pain exacerbated by standing, intercourse, and the premenstrual period. The pathophysiology involves both mechanical and hormonal factors, including congenital or acquired valve insufficiency, pregnancy-induced venous dilatation, and estrogen-mediated venous relaxation, with matrix metalloproteinase upregulation degrading collagen and smooth muscle in affected vessels. Diagnostic imaging via transvaginal ultrasound identifies characteristic features including ovarian vein dilatation (>7–8 mm), reduced flow velocity (<3 cm/s), myometrial vein dilatation (>5 mm), and side-specific adnexal tenderness on examination. The condition predominantly affects premenopausal multiparous women, with pregnancy identified as a major risk factor, and anatomical asymmetry—particularly the acute angle between the left ovarian and renal veins—contributing to left-sided predominance. Hormonal therapy with medroxyprogesterone acetate reduces symptoms, and emerging evidence suggests neurovascular entrapment of pelvic nerves contributes to pain pathophysiology, with symptom relief reported following sacral plexus decompression.

title: The Role of Nutrition on the Pathogenesis of Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory gynecological disease affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue in the pelvis and distant organs, and presents with pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dysuria, and infertility. Dietary factors significantly influence endometriosis pathogenesis and symptom severity through modulation of steroid hormone metabolism, oxidative stress, and inflammatory responses; specifically, Mediterranean and low-FODMAP diets rich in fruits, vegetables, and fish oil reduce symptoms and improve quality of life, while processed and red meat consumption increase disease risk. Key micronutrients including vitamin D, vitamin C, zinc, curcumin, and B vitamins (B6, B12, B2) modulate oxidative stress and suppress inflammatory pathways by downregulating cytokines such as MCP-1, HGF, and IGF-1, as well as angiogenic factors like VEGF. Vitamin D deficiency particularly increases endometriosis risk by failing to suppress neuroangiogenic pathways and genetic mechanisms driving ectopic endometrial proliferation, while flavonoids inhibit proliferation of ectopic endometrium through antiproliferative and anti-inflammatory effects. These findings establish nutritional intervention as a complementary therapeutic strategy to reduce endometriosis symptoms and disease progression.

title: Efficacy and Safety of Traditional Chinese Medicine Retention Enema for Endometriosis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue and manifesting as pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. While standard treatments including hormonal therapy (oral contraceptives, progestins, GnRH agonists, aromatase inhibitors) and surgical interventions are effective, they are frequently associated with significant adverse effects such as vasomotor instability, mood disturbances, decreased bone mineral density, and weight gain. This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that herbal retention enema, a traditional Chinese medicine approach utilizing botanical formulations like Shaofu Zhuyu Decoction containing Angelica sinensis, Paeonia lactiflora, and Ligusticum chuanxiong, achieves superior clinical efficacy compared to Western medical therapies by providing localized drug delivery to affected pelvic tissues while bypassing hepatic first-pass metabolism. Herbal retention enema showed significantly higher total effective rates, improved dysmenorrhea symptoms, reduced CA-125 and inflammatory markers (IL-1β, TNF-α), and was associated with fewer adverse events than conventional hormonal therapy. A trend toward improved pregnancy outcomes was observed with herbal retention enema, and the pooled analysis suggested favorable effects on endocrine markers including estradiol and follicle-stimulating hormone levels. These findings suggest herbal retention enema represents a potentially safer and more effective alternative therapeutic option for endometriosis management.

title: Effectiveness, Safety, and Patient Satisfaction of Laparoscopic Uterosacral Nerve Ablation in Terms of Pain Relief in Women with Chronic Pelvic Pain
summary: Endometriosis is the most common cause of chronic pelvic pain, a condition strongly associated with negative cognitive, behavioral, sexual, and emotional consequences that substantially impair quality of life. Laparoscopic uterosacral nerve ablation (LUNA) interrupts afferent sensory nerve fibers transmitted through the Lee-Frankenhauser nerve plexus and uterosacral ligaments to treat chronic pelvic pain by ablating these pain-transmitting neural structures. In this study, LUNA significantly improved postoperative pain tolerance, reduced pain intensity and frequency, decreased distress from pain, and enhanced patients' ability to cope with pain, while also reducing painkiller use and improving sexual function and social engagement. However, LUNA demonstrated differential effectiveness depending on diagnosis: it was effective for dysmenorrhea and chronic pelvic pain when endometriosis was absent, but proved ineffective in patients with concomitant endometriosis. Presacral neurectomy emerged as an alternative for refractory pelvic pain, particularly when combined with surgical endometriosis treatment, though it carries higher complication rates including urinary urgency, constipation, and postoperative bleeding compared to LUNA. These findings establish LUNA as a viable surgical option for select chronic pelvic pain patients, though careful patient selection based on endometriosis status is critical for optimizing outcomes.

title: High prevalence of endometriosis in women with chronic pelvic pain and confirmed pelvic varicosities: a multiparametric MRI study
summary: This multiparametric MRI study reveals a striking 61.5% co-occurrence of endometriosis in women with confirmed pelvic varicosities, establishing a significant clinical association between these two conditions as major contributors to chronic pelvic pain. Endometriosis presents with characteristic findings in the posterior uterine serosa, uterosacral ligaments, rectovaginal septum, and ovarian regions, while pelvic venous disorders manifest as non-cyclical pelvic pain and dyspareunia distinct from endometriosis-related dysmenorrhea and dyschezia. Both conditions share elevated inflammatory mediators including interleukin-6 and C-reactive protein, suggesting a common inflammatory pathophysiology underlying their association. Multiparametric MRI demonstrated high diagnostic sensitivity (76–96%) for detecting deep infiltrating endometriosis and pelvic venous insufficiency simultaneously, enabling comprehensive evaluation of both vascular and gynecologic abnormalities in women presenting with chronic pelvic pain. These findings underscore the clinical importance of recognizing endometriosis and pelvic venous disorders as frequently coexisting etiologies in chronic pelvic pain and highlight MRI's utility as an integrated diagnostic tool for identifying both pathologies in affected women.

title: Treatment of ovarian endometriosis: Number 1 – 2026
journal: Revista Brasileira de Ginecologia e Obstetrícia
summary: Ovarian endometriomas are pseudocysts containing ectopic endometrial tissue within the ovary that cause significant clinical morbidity through pain and infertility. The infertility associated with endometriosis involves multiple mechanisms including elevated cytokines, chemokines, and oxidative stress, while endometriomas specifically impair folliculogenesis and reduce ovarian reserve, with bilateral involvement posing particularly high risk for ovarian failure. Surgical excision via cystectomy effectively treats endometriomas and reduces dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and pelvic pain, though it carries the risk of further reducing ovarian reserve; in contrast, drainage and puncture techniques have unacceptably high recurrence rates and may increase infection and adhesion risk. Medical management with hormonal agents can reduce endometrioma size but may induce fibrosis complicating surgery and paradoxically increase follicle loss, while GnRH analogs are ineffective at preventing recurrence post-operatively and combined oral contraceptives show conflicting evidence. Transvaginal ultrasound and MRI demonstrate high sensitivity and specificity for endometrioma diagnosis, with laparoscopic visualization and histopathology providing definitive confirmation. Clinicians must weigh the symptom relief benefits of surgical excision against ovarian reserve depletion risk, particularly in bilateral disease, and consider alternative preservation-focused techniques such as drainage with sclerotherapy or alcohol ablation.

title: Ionic liquid-coated gold core polymeric nanoparticles for selective neutrophil hitchhiking towards endometriosis treatment
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory gynecological disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial-like tissue in the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and pelvic peritoneum, causing severe pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility in up to 50% of affected women. Current treatments are limited to hormonal therapies and surgical interventions, which suffer from high recurrence rates and increased risks of complications with repeated procedures. This study introduces ionic liquid-coated gold core polymeric nanoparticles (Au-PLGA-IL NPs) as a novel non-surgical, minimally invasive therapeutic approach that exploits neutrophil hitchhiking to selectively target endometrial lesions. The Au-PLGA-IL NPs demonstrated high biocompatibility, selective neutrophil targeting capability, and enhanced photothermal efficiency (33.6% for the CA2HEP(1:1) variant) through near-infrared irradiation at 808 nm, which induced localized hyperthermia and significant apoptosis in endometrial epithelial cells. The ionic liquid coating enhanced cellular uptake and plasmonic energy dissipation while maintaining excellent biocompatibility with cell viabilities exceeding 80%, as confirmed through confocal microscopy and fluorescence-activated cell sorting studies using human whole blood. This innovative nanotherapeutic platform offers a promising, fertility-preserving treatment option that addresses key limitations of existing endometriosis therapies by harnessing innate immune cell trafficking to achieve targeted lesion ablation.

title: Bladder Endometriosis as Part of Complex Pelvic Deep Endometriosis: Surgical Challenges and Outcomes in a Reference Center
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease, and when it infiltrates more than 5 mm beneath the peritoneal surface, it is classified as deep endometriosis. Bladder endometriosis, affecting 1–12% of women with endometriosis, represents a significant clinical challenge as it presents with lower urinary tract symptoms including dysuria, frequency, urgency, and hematuria, alongside pelvic pain symptoms such as dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia. In 96.3% of cases, bladder endometriosis coexists with bowel, uterine, and ureteral involvement, indicating a multiorgan disease pattern that complicates surgical management. Surgical approach depends on lesion depth: shaving or mucosal skinning techniques treat partial-thickness infiltration, while full-thickness lesions require bladder resection or, in extensive cases, bladder augmentation with intestinal segments. Postoperative complications occur in 10% of cases and are associated with larger lesions, full-thickness infiltration, trigonum involvement, and prior surgery, with complex cases often requiring bi-disciplinary gynecology-urology teams. Transvaginal ultrasound, pelvic MRI, and renal ultrasound are mandatory diagnostic modalities, with histopathological confirmation essential for diagnosis.

title: Comparison Between Catheter-Directed Sclerotherapy and Surgical Removal of Large Ovarian Endometriomas: A Retrospective, Single-Center Observational Study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriomas, cystic lesions of endometrial tissue found primarily in the ovaries and associated with infertility and deep infiltrating endometriosis, pose a clinical challenge in balancing effective treatment with preservation of ovarian function. This study compared catheter-directed sclerotherapy (CDS) using 95% ethanol with laparoscopic surgical removal for treating large ovarian endometriomas, evaluating treatment efficacy using cyst size reduction, CA-125 biomarker levels, and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) as a measure of ovarian reserve. While both approaches significantly reduced endometrioma size and CA-125 levels within six months, CDS preserved ovarian reserve substantially better than surgery, with AMH levels remaining nearly stable in the CDS group compared to marked reductions in both unilateral and bilateral surgical groups. Notably, bilateral surgery caused the most dramatic AMH decline, increasing the risk of premature ovarian failure, whereas CDS avoided direct tissue damage by targeting only the endometriotic lesion with minimal blood loss and no hemorrhagic complications. These findings establish CDS as a promising minimally invasive alternative to surgery for large endometriomas, particularly for patients concerned about maintaining fertility potential.

title: Improved Outcomes with Nerve-Sparing Technique: A Retrospective Comparison of Colorectal Surgeries for Bowel Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Deep infiltrating endometriosis typically localizes to the posterior pelvic compartment and frequently involves the bowel, causing characteristic symptoms including infertility, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, and bowel dysfunction such as dyschezia, constipation, and rectorrhagia. Deep endometriotic lesions often invade along nerve pathways, creating surgical complexity that necessitates careful nerve-sparing approaches during colorectal intervention. This retrospective comparison evaluated laparoscopic colorectal procedures for bowel endometriosis, finding that the nerve-sparing partial rectal resection (LscPR) technique achieved superior outcomes, including the most comprehensive postoperative symptom improvement, recovery of rectal sensory and motor function, and notably low complication rates without rectovaginal fistula, anastomotic stenosis, or urinary dysfunction. The mesentery-preserving approach used in LscPR promoted better anastomotic healing and reduced postoperative intestinal dysfunction compared to conventional segmental resection (LscSgR), which carried higher complication rates and compromised rectal function. While shaving resection (LscShR) avoided some complications, it was associated with higher recurrence rates; recurrence was rare in the LscPR group, potentially enhanced by postoperative medical therapy with GnRH analogs or dienogest. These findings demonstrate that nerve-sparing surgical techniques represent an important advancement in managing bowel endometriosis by balancing complete disease eradication with preservation of autonomic nerve function and quality of life.

title: Random Survival Forest Model for Predicting Recurrence of Postoperative Ovarian Endometrioma
summary:
# Summary: Random Survival Forest Model for Predicting Recurrence of Postoperative Ovarian Endometrioma

Endometriosis causes chronic inflammation through ectopic endometrial tissue that generates oxidative stress and immune activation, with ovarian endometriomas representing a particularly challenging manifestation due to high postoperative recurrence rates. A random survival forest predictive model identified multiple independent risk factors for recurrence: larger cyst diameter (>4 cm), younger age at surgery (<25 years), preoperative cyst rupture, greater dysmenorrhea severity (VRS-5 score), increased menstrual duration, and elevated serum biomarkers including CA199, triglycerides, and fasting blood glucose. Clinical and demographic factors such as earlier age of menarche, shorter menstrual cycles, higher rAFS endometriosis staging, and fewer postoperative pregnancies also associated with recurrence risk. While hormone therapy demonstrates effectiveness in treating endometriosis with approximately 67% efficacy, ablation-based surgical approaches may reduce recurrence by diminishing follicular reserve, and interestingly, GnRH agonist treatment did not emerge as a significant protective factor. These findings establish a comprehensive predictive framework integrating inflammatory biomarkers (CA125, CRP, white blood cell count), reproductive history variables, and surgical parameters to stratify individual recurrence risk in ovarian endometrioma patients.

title: Effect of endometrioma size and bilaterality on clinical, surgical, and laboratory parameters in endometriosis: A retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease, causes dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, pelvic pain, and infertility, with severity correlating to endometrioma characteristics. Larger endometriomas (>70 mm) and bilateral involvement are independently associated with significantly elevated rates of pelvic pain, infertility, and pelvic adhesions compared to smaller lesions. Endometrioma size and bilaterality inversely correlate with anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels—a marker of ovarian reserve—while positively correlating with CA-125, a biomarker of inflammatory activity and disease burden. The study identified that larger and bilateral endometriomas >50 mm cause direct detrimental effects on folliculogenesis and ovascular vascularization, contributing to diminished ovarian reserve and prolonged infertility duration. Notably, patients with the largest cysts (>70 mm) with bilateral involvement demonstrated the lowest preoperative AMH levels and highest CA-125 levels, with reduced body mass index. These findings establish that endometrioma morphology serves as an important predictor of clinical outcomes, laboratory parameters, and reproductive potential in endometriosis.

title: Symptom Profiles and Anatomical Distribution of Deep Infiltrating Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is a widespread condition causing chronic pelvic pain and infertility, with deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) representing a severe form affecting visceral organs including the bladder, ureter, bowel, and uterosacral ligaments. The pathogenesis of endometriosis is primarily attributed to retrograde menstruation, whereby menstrual fluid refluxes into the peritoneal cavity. Deep infiltrating endometriosis presents with diverse symptom profiles depending on anatomical location: bowel involvement produces characteristic gastrointestinal symptoms including dyschezia, constipation, rectal pain, and bleeding, while ovarian involvement presents with dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, and dyspareunia. Approximately 95% of DIE cases are associated with severe pain and infertility, with abdominal pain being the most prevalent symptom across all sites. Surgical intervention, including lysis of adhesions and low anterior resection, achieves 50-80% symptom reduction, though diagnosis remains challenging as colonoscopy has poor sensitivity for detecting serosal bowel lesions, making laparoscopy with histopathologic examination the gold standard for confirmation.

title: The relationship between C-reactive protein, carbohydrate antigen 125, and hematological parameters to endometriotic nodule localization in pelvis
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic pelvic inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue implantation, retrograde menstruation, and immunological dysfunction, manifesting with dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. Deep infiltrating endometriosis exhibits extensive anatomical distribution, commonly localizing to the recto-vaginal septum, uterosacral ligaments, para-rectal space, and uterovesical region. The study identified CA 125 and C-reactive protein (CRP) as significant biomarkers for endometriosis diagnosis, with both markers elevated in deep infiltrative disease and correlating strongly with the number of pelvic nodule localizations. Hemoglobin levels were significantly higher in ovarian endometriomas, while hemoglobin, platelet, neutrophil, and lymphocyte counts individually showed limited diagnostic value. The pathophysiology involves activated platelets promoting smooth muscle metaplasia and fibrogenesis within lesions, endometriotic lesions generating oxidative damage through excess iron accumulation, and subsequent chronic inflammatory processes that may increase ovarian cancer risk through hyperestrogenism and oxidative stress. Among routinely measured hematological parameters, CA 125 emerged as an independent risk factor for deep infiltrative endometriosis and, combined with CRP, provides valuable diagnostic guidance for advanced-stage disease, though invasive procedures remain necessary for definitive localization and diagnosis.

title: Integrating IVF and Surgical Management in Endometriosis-Associated Infertility: A Review
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition affecting 30–50% of individuals with infertility through multiple mechanistic pathways, including reduced ovarian reserve, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain.
