The disease develops through retrograde menstruation, altered immunity, genetic predisposition, and stem cell differentiation at ectopic sites, with endometriomas causing direct ovarian dysfunction through inflammatory damage and mechanical distortion. Management approaches differ by disease stage: for stages I–II, laparoscopic ablative techniques can enhance spontaneous conception, while minimally invasive approaches such as laser vaporization and ultrasound-guided sclerotherapy preserve ovarian parenchyma better than cystectomy while maintaining comparable pregnancy rates. In vitro fertilization effectively treats endometriosis-related infertility with preserved endometrial receptivity and unaffected oocyte quality for stages I–II disease, though stages III–IV show reduced oocyte retrieval and live birth rates. The integrated surgical-reproductive strategy should balance lesion removal against iatrogenic ovarian damage, with the Endometriosis Fertility Index and rASRM classification informing individualized treatment decisions combining medical therapy, surgical intervention, and assisted reproductive techniques.

title: Intra-ureteric indocyanine green for ureteric visualisation in complex benign gynaecological surgery: a prospective series of 50 cases
summary:
# Summary

Deep endometriosis significantly increases the risk of iatrogenic ureteric injury during complex benign gynecological surgery, particularly when disease involves the rectovaginal septum, uterosacral ligaments, pelvic sidewall, and rectosigmoid. This prospective series of 50 high-risk cases demonstrated that intra-ureteric indocyanine green (ICG) with near-infrared imaging provides superior real-time visualization of the ureter throughout the surgical field, achieving 100% bilateral ureteric fluorescence with zero adverse events or ureteric injuries. The technique enables continuous dynamic mapping of the ureteric course, potentially reducing reliance on prophylactic ureteric stenting—which carries its own morbidity including urinary tract infections, acute renal impairment, and fistula formation. ICG remains intraluminal within the ureter with rare adverse effects (0.05–0.07%), offering a radiation-free alternative to conventional preventive strategies. By improving ureteric identification during ureterolysis, this fluorescence-based approach may mitigate fibrosis and reduce complexity in subsequent pelvic surgeries. These findings support intra-ureteric ICG as an effective adjunctive tool for safer surgical management of deep endometriosis.

title: Entheseal pain of levator ani muscle: A novel etiology in chronic pelvic pain and lower urinary tract symptoms—A urodynamic perspective
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Summary

Entheseal pain of the levator ani muscle, the pelvic floor's key stabilizer, represents a distinct clinical entity in chronic pelvic pain that drives lower urinary tract symptoms through mechanisms distinct from myofascial dysfunction alone. Patients with levator ani entheseal pain exhibit significantly higher prevalences of urinary symptoms including frequency, residual sensation, and painful bladder symptoms, alongside pain-related comorbidities such as dysmenorrhea, irritable bowel syndrome, and systemic myofascial pain. The unique association between entheseal pain and morning stiffness suggests divergent pathophysiology compared to isolated muscle pain, with central sensitization mechanisms potentially underlying abnormal voiding perception. Patients with combined muscle and entheseal pain show the highest symptom burden across subjective measures including postvoid residual sensation and painful bladder syndrome. NSAIDs effectively reduce both pelvic floor myofascial pain and associated lower urinary tract symptoms in responsive patients. These findings establish levator ani entheseal pain as a novel urological etiology requiring distinct diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in the management of chronic pelvic pain and lower urinary tract dysfunction.

title: Beyond nociception: rethinking pain in ovarian endometriosis a pilot study
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study challenges the conventional understanding of ovarian endometriosis pain by demonstrating that nociplastic mechanisms—involving altered central pain processing rather than solely inflammatory mechanisms—play a significant role in the condition. Patients with ovarian endometriosis showed elevated Fibromyalgia Survey scores in 41.7% of cases compared to 0% in controls, indicating widespread pain sensitization and central nervous system involvement, alongside expected increases in dysmenorrhea, dyschezia, dysuria, and deep dyspareunia. The study found that 75% of ovarian endometriosis patients had comorbid adenomyosis and 83.3% had deep infiltrating endometriosis, with evidence of decreased gray matter volume in key pain-processing brain regions including the thalamus, cingulate gyrus, putamen, and insula. Cross-organ pain sensitization was observed through shared peripheral neural pathways involving the colon, bladder, and reproductive organs, explaining the frequent co-occurrence of painful bladder and bowel symptoms. Notably, current treatment approaches—medical therapies with limited efficacy and surgical management with up to 37% persistent pain rates—inadequately address this centrally mediated pain phenotype, suggesting a need for centrally acting medications targeting nociplastic mechanisms. These findings support implementing validated pain assessments like the Fibromyalgia Survey in clinical practice to better characterize and treat the complex pain mechanisms underlying ovarian endometriosis.

title: Pre-Operative Sonographic Assessment of Ovarian Location and Mobility Predicts Intra-Operative Ovarian Resectability During Vaginal Hysterectomy: A Diagnostic Accuracy Study
summary:
# Summary

This diagnostic accuracy study demonstrates that pre-operative transvaginal ultrasound assessment of ovarian location and mobility reliably predicts intra-operative ovarian findings during vaginal hysterectomy, with excellent correlation between pre-operative imaging and surgical observations. Specifically, sonographic evaluation of ovarian mobility using the sliding sign accurately identifies the presence of peri-ovarian adhesions and predicts the feasibility of salpingo-oophorectomy via the vaginal route, while assessment of ovarian location predicts intra-operative position with 96% accuracy. Notably, the absence of ovarian mobility did not increase the risk of intra-operative or post-operative complications, suggesting that immobile ovaries can be safely managed by selecting alternative surgical approaches such as endoscopy. Mobile ovaries located close to the uterine corpus indicate feasibility of vaginal removal, whereas non-mobile ovaries or those near internal iliac vessels suggest consideration of endoscopic approaches. Pre-operative sonographic characterization of adnexal anatomy is therefore crucial for surgical planning, enabling higher precision and successful outcomes in patients undergoing vaginal hysterectomy, particularly those with endometriosis-related pelvic adhesions.

title: Pathologic, Laboratory, and Surgical Findings of Topical Statin Gels (Simvastatin, Atorvastatin, and Rosuvastatin) in a Rat Model of Peritoneal Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, characterized histologically by ectopic endometrial glands and stroma, is associated with dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, infertility, and chronic pelvic pain that significantly impact quality of life. Oxidative stress and elevated inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1β) drive endometriosis pathogenesis through reactive oxygen species-mediated cellular damage. Topical statin gels (simvastatin, atorvastatin, and rosuvastatin) effectively suppressed peritoneal endometriosis in a rat model through multiple complementary mechanisms: reducing lesion volume by 72–97%, decreasing adhesion severity, downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines and matrix metalloproteinases, and enhancing peritoneal fibrinolytic activity via upregulation of tissue plasminogen activator. Simvastatin demonstrated superior efficacy, achieving a 97% reduction in lesion volume and the most pronounced anti-adhesion effects by decreasing Hoffmann adhesion scores from 7.2 to 1.9. These pleiotropic statin effects—antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-proliferative, and pro-fibrinolytic—suggest topical statin gel therapy represents a promising novel therapeutic approach for treating endometriosis and preventing postoperative adhesions.

title: Safety and Reproductive Outcomes of Minimally Invasive Nerve-Sparing Surgery for Deep Endometriosis in Infertile Women: A One-Year Follow-Up Study
summary:
# Summary

Deep endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by endometrial tissue infiltrating organs to depths greater than 5 mm, primarily affects the rectovaginal septum, uterosacral ligaments, fallopian tubes, ovaries, intestine, and bladder, and is responsible for infertility and multiple pelvic dysfunctions including sexual dysfunction and bowel dysfunction in affected women. Minimally invasive nerve-sparing complete excision of deep endometriosis effectively treats endometriosis-related infertility while maintaining safety, with the one-year follow-up study demonstrating no serious surgical complications, conversions to open surgery, or major postoperative morbidities such as anastomotic leakage or colostomy requirements. Among 45 infertile women undergoing this surgical approach, 33% achieved spontaneous conception with a mean time to conception of 6.7 months, and 93% of those who conceived resulted in live births, though outcomes were significantly influenced by duration of infertility prior to surgery and number of previous operations. The surgical restoration of pelvic anatomy through complete removal of endometriotic implants directly contributed to fertility recovery and symptom improvement, particularly reduction of deep dyspareunia. Notably, higher annual income was positively associated with spontaneous pregnancy success, suggesting that economic factors influence both access to definitive surgical treatment and postoperative outcomes in endometriosis management.

title: Risk Factors of Adolescent Endometriosis Onset and Recurrence: A Retrospective Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study identifies multiple independent risk factors for adolescent endometriosis onset and recurrence. Dysmenorrhea, genital tract malformations, elevated CA125, hyperfibrinogenemia, and dyslipidemia emerge as significant predictors of initial disease development, with genital tract malformations showing the strongest association (aOR 16.15). Adolescent endometriosis presents distinctly from adult disease, characterized predominantly by peritoneal involvement and active red lesions rather than fibrotic disease, and manifests with complex clinical phenotypes including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, gastrointestinal disturbances, and menstrual irregularities. Recurrence risk is substantially higher in patients with ovarian endometriomas or deep infiltrating disease, predicted by larger cyst diameter and elevated CA125, whereas isolated peritoneal endometriosis does not recur. The underlying pathophysiology involves retrograde menstruation and disrupted uterine peristalsis facilitated by Müllerian anomalies, coupled with systemic inflammation, metabolic dysregulation, and a hypercoagulable state evidenced by elevated fibrinogen. Early surgical correction of anatomical anomalies significantly reduces postoperative recurrence, emphasizing the importance of timely diagnosis to prevent cascading complications including ovarian dysfunction, compromised fertility, and serious psychosocial consequences such as anxiety and depression.

title: Preliminary Experience of Robotic Endometriosis From a Tertiary Care Center in India: The Learning Curve
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a common gynecological condition affecting 6–10% of reproductive-age women that manifests with chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, with deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) presenting particularly severe symptoms including high preoperative pain scores and infertility in over half of cases. While minimally invasive laparoscopic surgery remains the gold standard treatment, robotic-assisted surgery (RAS) has emerged as a viable alternative, offering enhanced three-dimensional visualization, superior precision, and improved postoperative pain and quality of life outcomes comparable to laparoscopy. However, DIE cases are more challenging surgically, requiring significantly longer operative times, generating higher blood loss and transfusion needs, and resulting in prolonged hospitalization and time to ambulation compared to non-DIE cases. Advanced visualization technologies such as Firefly imaging with indocyanine green can further enhance identification of endometriotic lesions during robotic procedures. The study documents the learning curve for RAS in endometriosis treatment at an Indian tertiary center, supporting its safety and feasibility as endometriosis surgery becomes increasingly complex.

title: Coexistence of Ovarian Mature Cystic Teratomas and Endometriosis: An Update
summary:
# Summary

Mature cystic teratomas (MCTs) and endometriosis are among the most common benign gynecological conditions in women of reproductive age, and their coexistence occurs in approximately 22.54% of patients undergoing ovarian cystectomy. The concurrent presence of both conditions is associated with significantly higher rates of dysmenorrhea and elevated serum CA125 levels compared to MCTs alone, reflecting endometriosis as an estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory condition. Surgical management of coexisting MCTs and endometriosis presents particular challenges, as the inflammatory environment of endometriosis creates unclear cleavage planes between normal ovarian tissue and cyst capsules, increasing the risk of unintentional removal of ovarian tissue and resulting in greater postoperative decline in anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels at one month—a 31.97% reduction in the complex group versus 18.02% in the teratoma-alone group. This acute drop in AMH reflects direct damage to AMH-secreting follicles from more extensive surgical manipulation, though levels typically recover through recruitment of primordial follicles. Given these findings, current clinical trends favor medical therapy as a first-line approach for endometriomas and expectant management for MCTs to preserve ovarian function and reserve in women of childbearing age.

title: Development and External Validation of Machine Learning Model to Predict Live Birth Following Assisted Reproductive Technology in Women with Ovarian Endometriomas: A Decision-Support Tool
summary: This study developed and validated machine learning models to predict live birth outcomes in women with ovarian endometriomas undergoing assisted reproductive technology (ART). Ovarian endometriomas damage ovarian structure, trigger chronic inflammation and oxidative stress, and impair ovarian reserve through follicle loss, collectively reducing ART success rates. The researchers identified four key predictive variables—patient age, treatment of endometriomas, endometrial thickness, and number of 2PN zygotes—and found that the XGBoost algorithm demonstrated superior predictive performance compared to seven other machine learning approaches. Age and features associated with ovarian reserve (antral follicle count, FSH levels, and AMH levels) emerged as the strongest predictors of successful live birth, while the endometriomas themselves had limited direct predictive power. Two complementary decision-support tools were developed: Model-2 uses pre-treatment variables to guide reproductive strategy selection before ART initiation, while Model-1 incorporates post-retrieval data to optimize embryo transfer decisions. These models provide clinicians with evidence-based tools to counsel patients with endometriosis-related infertility and personalize ART management strategies.

title: The impact of adenomyosis localization in myometrium on fertility and pregnancy outcomes: a narrative systematic review of the literature
summary: Adenomyosis is characterized by infiltration of endometrial glands and stroma into the myometrium, and its localization within myometrial layers significantly impacts reproductive outcomes. Two distinct adenomyosis subtypes—intrinsic (inner myometrium) and extrinsic (outer myometrium)—exhibit markedly different clinical presentations and fertility consequences. Intrinsic adenomyosis predominantly affects older women and is strongly associated with recurrent pregnancy loss, secondary infertility, abnormal uterine bleeding, and dysmenorrhea, often following previous uterine surgeries. Conversely, extrinsic adenomyosis occurs in younger, nulligravid women with lower BMI and shows a striking association with severe endometriosis phenotypes, including deep infiltrating endometriosis and ovarian endometriomas, correlating with primary infertility. Adenomyosis overall affects approximately 10-25% of women with fertility problems and those undergoing assisted reproductive technologies. The pathophysiology involves microtrauma at the endometrial-myometrial interface, tissue injury-repair mechanisms, and invagination of the endometrial basalis layer, with adenomyosis subtypes likely arising through distinct pathogenic pathways that explain their differential impacts on fertility and pregnancy outcomes.

title: The Silent Strangulation: Post-hysterectomy Ureteral Endometriosis Masquerading as Iatrogenic Injury
summary: Ureteral endometriosis is a rare but potentially devastating manifestation of deep infiltrating endometriosis that can cause silent progressive renal compromise through ureteral obstruction and fibrosis. The condition arises when endometrial glands and stroma infiltrate the ureteral adventitia, triggering cyclical hemorrhage that activates inflammatory pathways involving macrophage recruitment and cytokine secretion, ultimately stimulating fibroblast proliferation and excessive collagen deposition that leads to concentric fibrosis and muscular hypertrophy. Postoperative changes may exacerbate existing ureteral constriction, causing gradual compression that frequently produces no symptoms until advanced hydronephrosis and renal dysfunction develop, resulting in potential cortical thinning and irreversible nephron loss if diagnosis is delayed. Early identification through systematic mapping of posterior compartment disease during evaluation of deep infiltrating endometriosis involving the uterosacral ligaments, rectovaginal septum, and pelvic sidewalls, combined with magnetic resonance imaging for detection of lesions adjacent to the ureters, is critical for preserving renal function. Surgical management ranging from ureterolysis for mild extrinsic compression to segmental resection with ureteroneocystostomy for advanced strictures achieves anatomical correction and functional recovery when performed timely. This case underscores the importance of maintaining high clinical suspicion for ureteral involvement in patients with deep infiltrating endometriosis to prevent the insidious silent loss of renal function.

title: Endo-SOFT: study protocol for a national multicentre randomised controlled trial – first-line surgery versus first-line assisted reproductive technologies in patients with advanced endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease that impairs fertility through multiple mechanisms, including structural adhesions, diminished ovarian reserve, and immunological pathways that disrupt embryo implantation. The condition manifests with severe menstrual pain, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain, with women experiencing a twofold increased risk of infertility and dramatically reduced spontaneous pregnancy rates that decline from 50% in mild disease to 25% in moderate disease. The Endo-SOFT trial addresses a critical clinical dilemma: while surgery can reduce inflammatory burden and endometriosis-associated pain, it risks diminishing ovarian reserve and inducing new adhesions, potentially compromising fertility outcomes. Conversely, women with advanced endometriosis undergoing assisted reproductive technology demonstrate lower pregnancy and live birth rates despite fertility treatment, and current meta-analyses show no significant benefit of endometrioma surgery on ART outcomes. This multicentre randomised controlled trial aims to compare first-line surgical versus first-line ART approaches in advanced endometriosis patients to determine the optimal treatment strategy for maximizing fertility while managing disease-related symptoms and minimizing iatrogenic harm.

title: Demographic and hematological differences between endometriosis and menorrhagia in Saudi women: A retrospective observational study of 946 patients
summary: This retrospective study of 946 Saudi women identified distinct demographic and hematological profiles differentiating endometriosis from menorrhagia. Endometriosis predominantly affected younger women (65.2% under 40 years) and single individuals, while menorrhagia was more common in married, older women with higher obesity rates (49.1% vs 27.5%). Menorrhagia demonstrated a characteristic iron deficiency pattern with elevated red cell distribution width (56.6%), microcytosis (41.9%), anemia (46.4%), and low hematocrit (50.3%), reflecting chronic blood loss-induced iron depletion. In contrast, endometriosis showed elevated mean platelet volume (8.8% vs 3.4%), suggesting underlying inflammatory mechanisms and altered platelet activation rather than significant iron deficiency. These hematological differences likely reflect the pathophysiological distinction between endometriosis, involving retrograde menstruation and immune dysfunction during reproductive years, and menorrhagia, driven by accumulated structural changes, obesity-related anovulation, and perimenopausal hormonal fluctuations. The findings highlight the value of hematological profiling as a diagnostic tool to differentiate these gynecological conditions and guide appropriate clinical management.

title: Development of an early prediction model for endometriosis risk: the simplified adolescent factors for endometriosis (SAFE) score
summary: This study describes the development and validation of the Simplified Adolescent Factors for Endometriosis (SAFE) score, a clinical prediction tool designed to identify adolescents at high risk for endometriosis and facilitate early referral in primary care. Endometriosis is characterized by ectopic endometrial-like tissue growth outside the uterus and presents with a constellation of symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain, which significantly reduce quality of life and are associated with depression and infertility. The SAFE score incorporates six stable risk factors: three related to general pelvic pain severity (frequency of pain, treatment-seeking behavior, and analgesic use) and two related to menstrual disorders (heavy menstrual flow and painful periods between ages 16–19), plus family history of endometriosis. The SAFE score demonstrated strong discriminatory ability with area under the curve values of 0.79 across validation cohorts, providing clinicians with an evidence-based tool to guide endometriosis referrals. This approach addresses a critical clinical gap, as diagnostic delay typically spans 6–8 years from symptom onset due to nonspecific presentations and limited disease awareness. Early identification through the SAFE score in adolescents could enable timely diagnosis and intervention, potentially reducing the substantial morbidity burden associated with endometriosis.

title: Endometriosis phenotypes and staging in relation to lipid biomarkers: Findings from the ENDO Cohort Study
journal: Journal of gynecology obstetrics and human reproduction
summary:
# Endometriosis Phenotypes and Lipid Biomarkers Summary

This study from the ENDO Cohort demonstrates that endometriosis severity and phenotype significantly influence cardiometabolic risk, particularly lipid profiles. While minimal or superficial endometriosis alone was not associated with dyslipidemia, moderate to severe disease showed substantially elevated triglycerides and very low-density lipoprotein (VLDL), with adjusted prevalence ratios of 2.27 and 2.41 respectively. Most notably, women with ovarian endometriomas combined with deep infiltrating endometriosis exhibited markedly adverse lipid profiles across multiple markers, including 3.6-fold higher triglycerides, 4.1-fold higher apolipoprotein B, and 2.4-fold higher total cholesterol compared to women without endometriosis. These phenotype-specific lipid alterations suggest that disease extent and location, rather than endometriosis diagnosis alone, drive cardiometabolic complications, with ovarian endometriomas appearing to be the primary driver of dyslipidemia. The findings underscore potential mechanistic pathways involving systemic inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, and hormonal dysregulation that connect endometriosis severity to increased cardiovascular disease risk.

title: Identifying key determinants of cumulative live birth in women with ovarian endometrioma undergoing ethanol sclerotherapy followed by in vitro fertilization or intracytoplasmic sperm injection: an interpretable machine learning analysis
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease, presents clinically with dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain, with ovarian endometriomas representing severe manifestations that significantly impair fertility by depleting ovarian reserve through inflammation, oxidative stress, iron-mediated toxicity, and tissue compression. Ovarian endometriomas cause measurable reductions in anti-Müllerian hormone levels and antral follicle count, both critical predictors of ovarian response; traditional surgical cystectomy further damages ovarian tissue, whereas ethanol sclerotherapy preserves ovarian reserve parameters more effectively as a fertility-preserving alternative. Using machine learning interpretability analysis, researchers identified five key determinants of cumulative live birth following ethanol sclerotherapy combined with in vitro fertilization or intracytoplasmic sperm injection: antral follicle count, progesterone levels at gonadotropin initiation, downregulation status, cyst diameter, and previous live birth history. Higher antral follicle count and appropriate downregulation positively predicted live births, while elevated progesterone levels and larger cyst diameters negatively impacted outcomes. An Extreme Gradient Boosting model achieved an area under the curve of 0.830 for predicting cumulative live birth rates, demonstrating that machine learning can effectively identify prognostic factors in reproductive medicine and optimize patient selection for assisted reproductive technologies.

title: Endometriosis treatment pathways in the largest private health insurance in Brazil: A real-world data study
summary: Endometriosis is a prevalent chronic gynecological condition affecting 6-10% of women of reproductive age, characterized by ectopic endometrial-like tissue growth that can deeply infiltrate pelvic organs including the bowel, bladder, and ureters in severe cases. The disease causes multiple debilitating symptoms including cyclic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dysuria, and dyschezia, with infertility occurring in up to 50% of affected women and significant psychiatric consequences including anxiety and depression. This real-world data study from Brazil's largest private health insurer demonstrated that surgical intervention significantly improved all endometriosis symptoms and increased reproductive outcomes, with decreased gynecology consultations and emergency visits postoperatively while obstetrics consultations and hospital labor admissions increased. However, deep infiltrating endometriosis, which accounts for 20% of cases, showed delayed surgical intervention, longer symptom duration before treatment, higher surgical costs (40% more expensive than superficial disease), and greater risk of urgent reoperations due to severe complications including bowel perforations and fistulas. MRI demonstrated 63.7% sensitivity for detecting both endometriosis types, with diagnostic imaging accounting for 22% of overall surgical costs. The findings underscore endometriosis as a significant clinical and economic burden characterized by delayed diagnosis and complex management requiring individualized treatment strategies based on disease severity.

title: Managing the aftermath: complications and outcome after colorectal resection for deep infiltrating endometriosis—insights from a certified surgical endoscopy center
summary: Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) frequently affects the bowel, particularly the sigmoid colon and rectum, causing dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and pelvic pain, with up to 35% of patients experiencing gastrointestinal or urinary symptoms from deep infiltration. Colorectal resection for DIE carries a 20% postoperative complication rate, including anastomotic leakage, stenosis, and rectovaginal fistula, though these complications can be successfully managed with endoscopic interventions such as vacuum therapy, balloon dilatation, and bougienage. Rectal resection yielded superior outcomes compared to rectal shaving, with a 27% versus 41% recurrence rate requiring reoperation and significant symptom improvement in gastrointestinal complaints. Prior endometriosis surgery was associated with higher intraoperative disease severity, and while protective ileostomy reduced reoperation rates, it was independently linked to increased anastomotic stenosis development. Standard risk factors such as age, BMI, and disease severity showed no significant correlation with anastomotic leakage in multivariate analysis. Overall, specialized surgical management with endoscopic support successfully treated postoperative complications, with all temporary ileostomies reversed within three months.

title: Navigating complexity: review and updates of endometriosis and adenomyosis imaging
summary: Endometriosis is a complex gynecological condition causing a broad spectrum of clinical manifestations, including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, menorrhagia, and infertility, alongside systemic symptoms such as fatigue, anxiety, and depression. The pathophysiology involves multiple contributing factors including retrograde menstruation, estrogen excess, progesterone resistance, and amplified inflammation driven by inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, and prostaglandins. Endometriosis presents in three morphological forms: superficial peritoneal lesions affecting 15-80% of cases, ovarian endometriomas, and deep endometriosis involving fibromuscular structures such as the uterosacral ligaments (60-85% of deep cases), vagina, rectum, and bladder, with bowel involvement causing severe pain through fibrosis and neurogenesis. Beyond reproductive complications, endometriosis significantly increases obstetric risks including placenta previa, postpartum hemorrhage, preterm delivery, and pre-eclampsia, and elevates cancer risk, particularly a 4.2-fold increased risk of ovarian cancer alongside associations with endometrioid, clear cell, thyroid, and breast cancers through mechanisms involving estrogen stimulation and chronic inflammation. Transvaginal ultrasound serves as the first-line imaging modality with variable sensitivity across lesion types, showing highest performance for rectosigmoid deep endometriosis (85-94%) but lower sensitivity for rectovaginal septum disease, while magnetic resonance imaging provides superior sensitivity for endometriomas and deep endometriosis.

title: Establishment and characterization of a new immortalized human adenomyosis epithelial-like cell line, tAEC21
summary: This study describes the development and characterization of tAEC21, a novel immortalized epithelial cell line derived from adenomyotic endometrium, established using human telomerase reverse transcriptase (TERT) and EpCAM-based cell sorting. The tAEC21 cells exhibit authentic adenomyosis characteristics, including expression of epithelial markers (cytokeratin-5 and N-cadherin) while lacking the stromal marker CD10, and express both estrogen (ESR1) and progesterone (PGR) receptors. Functionally, the cells demonstrated estrogen responsiveness but lacked progesterone responsiveness, consistent with the hormone resistance observed in adenomyosis pathology. When stimulated with tumor necrosis factor-alpha, tAEC21 cells upregulated multiple pro-inflammatory mediators including interleukin-6, C-X-C motif chemokine ligand 8, C-C motif chemokine ligand 2, and mucin 1, supporting the role of inflammation in adenomyosis pathogenesis. The cell line successfully formed biologically relevant three-dimensional spheroid structures in heterotypic culture models, providing a valuable new research tool for investigating adenomyosis mechanisms and testing potential therapeutic interventions.

title: Age of diagnosis with endometriosis and potential predicting factors: A single-center cross-sectional study
summary: This cross-sectional study identifies clinical and demographic predictors of endometriosis diagnosis age, revealing that symptom presentation and fertility status significantly influence diagnostic timing. Dysmenorrhea was the most prevalent symptom (75.7%), followed by chronic pelvic pain (48.5%), with additional manifestations including dyspareunia, infertility, and gastrointestinal symptoms such as bloating and dyschezia present in substantial patient subsets. Notably, patients presenting with infertility received diagnoses approximately 2.72 years earlier than fertile patients, while those reporting sexual activity experienced delayed diagnosis by 4.74 years, likely reflecting societal and cultural norms that discourage premarital sexual activity and discussion of menstrual health. The severity of symptom presentation showed a complex association with diagnostic age, where patients with a single severe symptom were diagnosed 2.12 years earlier than asymptomatic cases, though having multiple severe symptoms paradoxically showed less diagnostic acceleration. Beyond clinical features, systemic barriers including medical gaslighting, lack of healthcare provider training, dismissal based on negative imaging results, and normalization of period pain substantially contributed to diagnostic delays. The study underscores that endometriosis remains a systemic inflammatory disease with delayed diagnosis averaging several years, highlighting the need for improved clinical awareness and more inclusive diagnostic approaches, particularly in populations with cultural factors that discourage health-seeking behaviors.

title: Endometriosis and adenomyosis research priorities in India and Sri Lanka: a call for regional collaboration
journal: The Lancet Regional Health - Southeast Asia
summary: Endometriosis and adenomyosis present overlapping clinical manifestations including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and subfertility, with both conditions significantly impairing quality of life and mental health. A critical barrier to effective management is the substantial diagnostic delay of 5-11 years, which is driven by limitations in healthcare availability, accessibility, and affordability, with socioeconomic status and geographic/ethnic variations further influencing disease presentation and diagnosis. India and Sri Lanka have identified research priorities focused on developing non-invasive diagnostic approaches including symptom-based screening tools, biomarkers, and advanced imaging techniques, alongside improved pelvic ultrasound for adenomyosis detection. Multidisciplinary, coordinated holistic care has demonstrated improved clinical outcomes, while educational programs targeting endometriosis and dysmenorrhea awareness are essential for reducing diagnostic delays. Several natural compounds including curcumin, resveratrol, epigallocatechin gallate, ginsenoside Rg3, and β-caryophyllene show promise in reducing inflammation and lesion size through herbal formulations. The article emphasizes the need for regional collaboration between India and Sri Lanka to address these research gaps and improve endometriosis and adenomyosis management in South Asian populations.
